src:
---
3md: 1.0
axis: section
title: 3md, explained in 3md
---
This file is a 3md document that teaches 3md. Scrub the Z axis to read it in
order. The "anatomy" plane contains a fenced code block with a literal @plane
line inside it, which the parser treats as body text, not a new plane.

@plane z=0 label="what"
# What is 3md
Markdown with a Z axis. A document is ordinary Markdown sliced into planes
along one axis you name yourself: time, frame, layer, depth, space, anything.

@plane z=1 label="anatomy"
# Anatomy
A required frontmatter block declares the version (the magic marker) and the
axis. Each `@plane` directive starts a plane; the Markdown between directives is
that plane's body. Here is a whole 3md file shown inside a code block:

```3md
---
3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: My Week
---
@plane z=0 label="Monday"
# Monday
- [ ] Standup
```

The `@plane` line above sits inside a code fence, so this document still has
four planes, not five.

@plane z=2 label="axis"
# The axis is yours
The `axis:` value is free-form metadata. The same parser serves a planner
(time), an animation (frame), an annotated contract (layer), and a scene
(space). Tools decide how to lay the planes out.

@plane z=3 label="tooling"
# Tooling
Parse it in Swift (ThreeMD) or TypeScript (@corvidlabs/threemd), validate and
render it with the `threemd` CLI, and trust that both parsers agree, thanks to
the shared conformance suite.

---
3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: 4-7-8 Breath Cycle
view: play
fps: 2
author: Meditation Guide
technique: Dr. Andrew Weil's 4-7-8 relaxation breath
rounds: 4 rounds to activate the parasympathetic nervous system
category: Health
---

One full breath cycle. Let the animation pace you — no clock-watching needed.

@plane z=0 label="Neutral"
```
          · · · · · · · · ·
        ·                   ·
      ·                       ·
    ·           ○ ○             ·
    ·          ○   ○            ·
    ·         ○     ○           ·
    ·          ○   ○            ·
    ·           ○ ○             ·
      ·                       ·
        ·                   ·
          · · · · · · · · ·
```

**Ready.**

Tip of tongue rests gently behind upper front teeth.
Lips slightly parted.

@plane z=1 label="Inhale 1"
```
          · · · · · · · · ·
        ·                   ·
      ·       ↑   ↑   ↑       ·
    ·        ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○       ·
    ·       ○           ○      ·
    ·      ○             ○     ·
    ·       ○           ○      ·
    ·        ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○       ·
      ·                       ·
        ·                   ·
          · · · · · · · · ·
```

**Inhale through nose — 1**

Belly first, then ribs, then chest.
Quiet, effortless, cool air.

@plane z=2 label="Inhale 2"
```
          · · · · · · · · ·
        ·     ↑   ↑   ↑     ·
      ·     ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○     ·
    ·      ○               ○   ·
    ·     ○                 ○  ·
    ·     ○                 ○  ·
    ·      ○               ○   ·
      ·     ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○     ·
        ·                   ·
          · · · · · · · · ·
```

**Inhale — 2**

Feel the ribs open sideways like a book.
Shoulders stay down and soft.

@plane z=3 label="Inhale 3"
```
        ↑   ↑   ↑   ↑   ↑
      ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○
    ○                         ○
   ○                           ○
   ○                           ○
   ○                           ○
    ○                         ○
      ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○
        ·   ·   ·   ·   ·
```

**Inhale — 3, 4**

Lungs full. Chest lifts gently on count 4.
Pause — breath just arrived.

@plane z=4 label="Hold 1"
```
      ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ●
    ●                         ●
   ●       · · · · · · ·       ●
   ●      ·             ·      ●
   ●     ·               ·     ●
   ●      ·             ·      ●
   ●       · · · · · · ·       ●
    ●                         ●
      ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ●
```

**Hold — 1, 2, 3**

Completely still. Oxygen diffusing.
Mind notices the silence between breaths.

@plane z=5 label="Hold 2"
```
      ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ●
    ●                         ●
   ●       ● ● ● ● ● ● ●       ●
   ●      ●             ●      ●
   ●     ●      ◉        ●     ●
   ●      ●             ●      ●
   ●       ● ● ● ● ● ● ●       ●
    ●                         ●
      ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ●
```

**Hold — 4, 5, 6, 7**

Heart rate slowing. Nervous system signaled: *safe*.
Nothing to do. Nothing to fix.

@plane z=6 label="Exhale"
```
          ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○
        ○ ↓               ↓ ○
      ○   ↓                 ↓  ○
    ·      ↓               ↓    ·
    ·       ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○ ○       ·
    ·         ↓       ↓         ·
    ·           ○ ○ ○           ·
      ·           ↓           ·
        ·         ·         ·
          · · · · · · · · ·
```

**Exhale through mouth — 1, 2, 3**

Lips make a loose *whoosh* shape.
Chest falls first, then ribs, then belly draws in.

@plane z=7 label="Exhale end"
```
          · · · · · · · · ·
        ·    ↓         ↓    ·
      ·        ○     ○        ·
    ·             ○ ○          ·
    ·            ↓ ↓           ·
    ·             · ·          ·
      ·                       ·
        ·                   ·
          · · · · · · · · ·
```

**Exhale — 4, 5, 6, 7, 8**

Empty. Utterly empty.
A brief pause before the cycle renews.

> *That was one round. Do 4 rounds total, then breathe naturally.*
> *After 1 month of daily practice, try 8 rounds.*

---
3md: 1.0
axis: version
title: Landing Headline A/B/C Test
experiment: hero-headline-q3
hypothesis: A benefit-led, specific headline lifts demo signups over a feature-led control.
metric: demo request click-through rate (CTR)
audience: paid-search visitors, US, desktop and mobile
sample-size: 9000 sessions per arm
duration: 14 days
significance: 95 percent (alpha 0.05)
---
This experiment tests three landing-page hero headlines for our scheduling product. Each plane is one variant arm. Start with the [[z=0|control]], compare the [[z=1|benefit]] and [[z=2|social-proof]] challengers, then read the [[z=3|result]].

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Variant A - Control (feature-led)"
# Variant A: Control

> ## Scheduling software for teams
> Calendars, reminders, and reports in one place.
>
> `[ Start free trial ]`

**Why it is the control:** This is the live headline we have shipped for the past nine months, so it sets the baseline CTR.

**Copy notes**
- Feature-led: names the product category, not the outcome.
- Generic verb ("Start free trial") with no urgency.
- Tested as-is, no design changes, so the headline is the only variable.

**Baseline (trailing 30 days):** 4.1 percent demo-request CTR.

Compare against the [[z=1|benefit-led challenger]].

@plane z=1 x=12 y=0 label="Variant B - Benefit-led"
# Variant B: Benefit-led

> ## Stop chasing people to book meetings
> Share one link. They pick a slot. It lands on your calendar, time zones handled.
>
> `[ Get my booking link ]`

**Hypothesis for this arm:** Leading with the pain ("chasing people") and a concrete outcome beats naming the category.

**Copy notes**
- Pain-first hook, then the mechanism in plain language.
- Specific objection killed inline ("time zones handled").
- CTA names the artifact the visitor gets ("my booking link"), not a generic trial.

This is the primary challenger against the [[z=0|control]]. Its sibling tests trust instead of benefit: see [[z=2|Variant C]].

@plane z=2 x=24 y=0 label="Variant C - Social proof"
# Variant C: Social-proof-led

> ## Join 12,000 teams who never double-book
> Pick the times you are free. We do the back-and-forth for you.
>
> `[ See how it works ]`

**Hypothesis for this arm:** A credible number plus a vivid avoided-outcome ("never double-book") borrows trust and lowers risk.

**Copy notes**
- Quantified proof up front (12,000 teams) sourced from the active-account count.
- Softer, lower-commitment CTA ("See how it works") to widen the funnel.
- Risk: the claim must be defensible, so legal signed off on the count.

Measured head-to-head with the [[z=0|control]] and the [[z=1|benefit variant]]. Outcome on the next plane: [[z=3|results]].

@plane z=3 x=36 y=0 label="Result - Winner and rollout"
# Result: Variant B wins

| Arm | Headline | Sessions | CTR | Lift vs control | p-value |
|-----|----------|----------|-----|-----------------|---------|
| [[z=0\|A]] | Feature-led | 9,042 | 4.0% | baseline | - |
| [[z=1\|B]] | Benefit-led | 8,988 | 5.6% | +40% | 0.002 |
| [[z=2\|C]] | Social proof | 9,011 | 4.7% | +18% | 0.061 |

**Decision:** Ship Variant B. The benefit-led headline beat control by 40 percent (relative) at p = 0.002, clearing our 95 percent bar.

**On Variant C:** Encouraging (+18 percent) but not significant (p = 0.061), so it stays a candidate for a follow-up test, not a rollout.

**Next steps**
- [x] Promote [[z=1|Variant B]] to 100 percent of traffic.
- [ ] Re-run [[z=2|Variant C]] copy with a stronger CTA against the new control.
- [ ] Repeat the test on mobile-only traffic, where CTR ran 0.6 points lower across all arms.

---
3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: Accessible Figure - Tidal Marsh at Dawn
description: One photograph rendered as four toggleable accessibility layers
image: marsh-dawn.jpg
wcag: 1.1.1, 1.4.5
---
This figure is a single photograph carried by four independent layers. Turn on
only what a given reader needs: the rendered image, the short alt text that a
screen reader announces, the visible caption shown to everyone, and the long
description for readers who want the full scene. Each layer below is the same
content described at a different depth.

@plane z=0 label="Rendered image" x=0 y=0
# Layer 0 - The image

![A tidal marsh at dawn, mist over still water with a wooden boardwalk leading toward distant pines](marsh-dawn.jpg)

The visual layer. It carries the `src` and a working `alt` attribute so the
picture is never silent for assistive tech. If images are turned off or fail to
load, the browser falls back to the alt string defined in [[z=1|the alt-text layer]].

- Format: JPEG, 2400 x 1600, sRGB
- Decorative? No. This image conveys information, so it needs real alt text.
- Fallback order: rendered pixels, then alt text, then caption.

@plane z=1 label="Alt text (announced)" x=0 y=-1
# Layer 1 - Alt text

> A tidal marsh at dawn, mist over still water with a wooden boardwalk leading toward distant pines.

This is the short text a screen reader speaks in place of the image. It is one
sentence, under about 150 characters, and it states what matters: the subject
(a tidal marsh), the time of day (dawn), and the one navigational element (the
boardwalk).

Rules this layer follows:

1. Describe function and content, not the word "image" (the reader already knows it is a graphic).
2. No "photo of" or "picture showing" filler.
3. End with a period so the speech synthesizer pauses naturally.

Need more than a sentence? That belongs in [[z=3|the long description]], not here.

@plane z=2 label="Visible caption" x=0 y=1
# Layer 2 - Caption

*Figure 1. Low tide at Salt Meadow Reserve, 5:42 AM, shot from the north boardwalk in late September.*

The caption is shown to every reader, sighted or not. Unlike the alt text, it
adds facts the picture alone cannot give: the place, the exact time, the season,
and the vantage point. Alt text and caption should not be identical, or screen
reader users hear the same sentence twice.

| Layer | Audience | Repeats the image? |
|-------|----------|--------------------|
| Alt text | Screen reader users | Substitutes for it |
| Caption | Everyone | Adds context |

For the camera settings and the full scene inventory, see [[z=3|the long description]].

@plane z=3 label="Long description (extended)" x=0 y=2
# Layer 3 - Long description

A wide, low-angle view across a coastal salt marsh just after sunrise. The water
in the foreground is glass-still and holds a faint orange reflection of the sky.
A weathered cedar boardwalk enters from the lower right and runs diagonally to
the left, disappearing into a low bank of ground mist. Beyond the mist, a ragged
line of pitch pines marks the far edge of the marsh, and a single great egret
stands motionless in the shallows at center-left.

This layer exists for readers who want the whole scene: data tables rendered as
prose, complex diagrams, or photographs where detail carries meaning. It is
linked from the figure rather than forced on everyone, so it does not bloat the
spoken [[z=1|alt text]] or the printed [[z=2|caption]].

Capture notes:

- Camera: 35mm, f/8, 1/125s, ISO 200
- Light: natural, golden hour, no flash
- Color: warm highlights, cool shadows, contrast ratio of text overlays kept at or above 4.5:1 per WCAG 1.4.5

Back to [[z=0|the image itself]].

3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: Mutual Non-Disclosure Agreement - Annotated
agreement: Mutual Non-Disclosure Agreement
parties: Northwind Analytics, Inc. and Cedar Grove Software LLC
effective_date: 2026-04-01
governing_law: Delaware
status: Draft v3 - in review
---
The same Non-Disclosure Agreement, viewed through three stacked layers. The
operative legal text sits at `z=0`. Above it, `z=1` restates each clause in
plain English for the business owners who have to live by it. Above that, `z=2`
holds the redline notes and open questions from counsel. Toggle the layers
independently: a deal lead reads [[z=1|the plain-English layer]], while counsel
works the [[z=2|review layer]] against the same clause numbers.

@plane z=0 label="Operative Text" x=0 y=0
# Mutual Non-Disclosure Agreement (Operative Text)

This Mutual Non-Disclosure Agreement (the "Agreement") is entered into as of
April 1, 2026 (the "Effective Date") by and between Northwind Analytics, Inc.,
a Delaware corporation ("Northwind"), and Cedar Grove Software LLC, a California
limited liability company ("Cedar Grove"). Northwind and Cedar Grove are each a
"Party" and together the "Parties."

## 1. Definition of Confidential Information

"Confidential Information" means any non-public information disclosed by one
Party (the "Discloser") to the other (the "Recipient"), whether orally, in
writing, or by inspection of tangible objects, that is designated as
confidential at the time of disclosure or that a reasonable person would
understand to be confidential given the nature of the information and the
circumstances of disclosure. Confidential Information includes, without
limitation, source code, product roadmaps, customer lists, pricing, and
unpublished financial results.

## 2. Exclusions

Confidential Information does not include information that: (a) is or becomes
publicly available through no fault of the Recipient; (b) was rightfully known
to the Recipient before disclosure; (c) is rightfully obtained by the Recipient
from a third party without a duty of confidentiality; or (d) is independently
developed by the Recipient without use of or reference to the Discloser's
Confidential Information.

## 3. Obligations of the Recipient

The Recipient shall (a) use the Confidential Information solely to evaluate and
pursue a potential business relationship between the Parties (the "Purpose"),
(b) protect it using at least the same degree of care it uses for its own
information of like importance, and in no event less than a reasonable degree
of care, and (c) limit access to its employees, contractors, and advisors who
have a need to know and are bound by confidentiality obligations no less
protective than those in this Agreement.

## 4. Compelled Disclosure

If the Recipient is compelled by law, regulation, or valid court order to
disclose Confidential Information, it may do so only to the extent required,
provided that, where legally permitted, it gives the Discloser prompt prior
written notice and reasonable cooperation so the Discloser may seek a
protective order or other remedy.

## 5. Term and Survival

This Agreement remains in effect for two (2) years from the Effective Date.
The Recipient's confidentiality obligations survive termination and continue
for three (3) years after the date of each disclosure, except that obligations
with respect to trade secrets continue for as long as the information remains a
trade secret under applicable law.

## 6. Return or Destruction

Upon the Discloser's written request or upon termination, the Recipient shall
promptly return or destroy all Confidential Information and certify its
destruction in writing, except that the Recipient may retain one archival copy
solely for legal-compliance purposes and copies created by routine automated
backup, subject to continuing confidentiality.

## 7. No License; No Warranty

Nothing in this Agreement grants any license or other right under any patent,
copyright, trademark, or trade secret. All Confidential Information is provided
"AS IS," without warranty of any kind.

## 8. Governing Law

This Agreement is governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard
to its conflict-of-laws rules.

@plane z=1 label="Plain English" x=0 y=2
# What This Actually Means (Plain English)

This layer mirrors [[z=0|the operative text]] clause by clause, in language a
non-lawyer can act on. It is a reading aid, not a substitute for the contract.

## 1. What counts as a secret

If either side shares something private (code, plans, customer lists, prices,
numbers that are not public) and it is marked confidential or obviously should
be treated that way, it is covered. The protection runs both ways: whoever
receives the information has to guard it.

## 2. What does not count

You are off the hook for information that is already public, that you already
knew, that someone else gave you fairly, or that you came up with on your own
without peeking at theirs. Keep a paper trail if you ever rely on one of these,
because the burden is on you to show it.

## 3. What you have to do with it

Three rules: use it only to explore working together, guard it at least as
carefully as you guard your own important secrets, and only let people see it
who genuinely need to and who are themselves under a confidentiality duty. See
the open question counsel raised in [[z=2|the review layer]] about contractors.

## 4. If a court asks for it

You can hand it over if the law truly forces you to, but only the part you must
disclose, and you have to give the other side a heads-up first (when you are
allowed to) so they can try to limit it.

## 5. How long it lasts

The deal itself runs two years. The duty to keep things quiet outlasts that:
three years from whenever each secret was shared, and effectively forever for
true trade secrets. Practical takeaway: do not assume the obligation ends when
the project does.

## 6. Cleaning up afterward

When asked, or when this ends, give the materials back or destroy them and put
the destruction in writing. You may keep exactly one locked-away copy for
legal reasons, and you do not have to scrub automatic backups, but those copies
stay confidential.

## 7. You get no ownership and no guarantees

Seeing someone's confidential material does not give you any rights to use
their inventions or brand, and they are not promising the information is
accurate or complete.

## 8. Which state's law applies

Delaware law governs any dispute.

@plane z=2 label="Legal Review" x=0 y=4
# Counsel Review Notes (Redline Layer)

Reviewer: M. Okonkwo, outside counsel. These notes attach to the matching
clause numbers in [[z=0|the operative text]]. Status markers: OPEN (needs a
decision), FIX (drafting change required), OK (acceptable as written).

## 1. Definition - OK

Definition is appropriately broad and includes the "reasonable person" catch-all,
which protects against the common failure of relying only on explicit marking.
No change needed.

## 2. Exclusions - FIX

The four standard carve-outs are present. Add an express statement that the
Recipient bears the burden of proving an exclusion applies. The plain-English
note at [[z=1|clause 2]] already tells the client to keep records, so aligning
the contract text with that expectation removes a future fight.

## 3. Obligations - OPEN

Two items:

1. **Contractors.** Subsection (c) lets contractors and advisors receive the
   information. Confirm with the business team that this matches reality. If
   subcontracting is not expected, narrow to "employees and advisors" to reduce
   exposure.
2. **Standard of care.** "Reasonable degree of care" is the right floor. No
   change, but flag for the client that this is an objective standard a court
   will second-guess after the fact.

## 4. Compelled Disclosure - FIX

Strong as drafted, but add a sentence clarifying that any compelled disclosure
does not strip the information of its confidential status for any other purpose.
Without it, a single subpoena response can be argued to waive protection
broadly.

## 5. Term and Survival - OPEN

The split structure (2-year deal term, 3-year survival per disclosure, indefinite
for trade secrets) is defensible. Decision needed: California courts disfavor
indefinite confidentiality for non-trade-secret material, and we have selected
Delaware law in [[z=0|clause 8]]. Confirm Delaware is enforceable against a
California LLC counterparty, or the survival period may be read down.

## 6. Return or Destruction - OK

The archival-copy and backup carve-outs are realistic and avoid an obligation
the client cannot actually perform. The plain-English framing at
[[z=1|clause 6]] correctly tells the client backups need not be scrubbed.

## 7. No License; No Warranty - OK

Standard and sufficient.

## 8. Governing Law - OPEN

See the cross-reference from [[z=2|clause 5]]. Delaware choice of law is fine
for Northwind (a Delaware corporation), but pair it with a forum-selection
clause; choice of law without choice of forum invites a fight over where any
dispute is heard. Recommend adding before signature.

---
3md: 1.0
axis: floor
title: Maplewood Court - Unit Stack (Line 04)
building: Maplewood Court
address: 412 Maplewood Avenue
line: "04"
units_per_floor: 4
elevator: central core
---
This stack documents the "04" line of units at Maplewood Court, one unit per
floor sharing the same plumbing and electrical riser. Each plane is a floor;
moving up the Z axis moves up the building. The `04` units sit on the
northwest corner, so every unit gets morning light from the east bedroom and
sunset light over the kitchen sink.

The `x`/`y` offsets below mark each unit's position on its floor plate relative
to the central elevator core (x grows east, y grows north, in feet).

@plane z=1 x=-22 y=14 label="101 - Garden"
# Unit 101 - Garden Level

**Type:** 1 bed / 1 bath, 690 sq ft
**Rent:** $1,650 / mo
**Status:** Occupied (lease ends 2026-09-30)

The only unit with private outdoor access: a 9 by 12 patio off the living room
opening onto the shared courtyard. Sits half a flight below the lobby, so it
stays cool in summer and is the easiest unit for moving furniture in.

- Floor: stained concrete, radiant heat loop
- Quirk: the riser shutoff for the whole `04` stack lives in this unit's
  utility closet, so plumbing work on any floor starts here
- Window line: north and east, partly shaded by the courtyard maple

Directly above is [[z=2|Unit 201]], which shares the kitchen drain stack.
Building-wide water shutoff requests route through here first.

@plane z=2 x=-22 y=14 label="201 - Standard"
# Unit 201 - Second Floor

**Type:** 1 bed / 1 bath, 705 sq ft
**Rent:** $1,725 / mo
**Status:** Occupied (lease ends 2027-01-31)

The baseline layout that floors 2 through 4 all repeat. Galley kitchen along
the west wall, bedroom on the northeast corner, living room facing the
courtyard. First floor with a real balcony (4 by 8, steel grate).

- Noise note: sits above the lobby vestibule, so the entry buzzer is faintly
  audible in the front hall
- HVAC: shared with the units above through the same vertical chase

The drain from this kitchen ties into the stack at [[z=1|Unit 101]]. The
identical unit one floor up is [[z=3|Unit 301]].

@plane z=3 x=-22 y=14 label="301 - Standard"
# Unit 301 - Third Floor

**Type:** 1 bed / 1 bath, 705 sq ft
**Rent:** $1,775 / mo
**Status:** Vacant - available 2026-07-15

Same plate as the floor below, one notch quieter and brighter. Far enough from
the street that traffic noise drops off, low enough that the elevator is still
faster than the stairs on a bad day.

- Recently repainted (warm white, 2026-05)
- New range and dishwasher installed during turnover
- Balcony gets direct sun from roughly 7am to 11am

This is the current showing unit. The penthouse one floor up is
[[z=4|Unit 401]]; prospective tenants often tour both in one visit.

@plane z=4 x=-22 y=14 label="401 - Penthouse"
# Unit 401 - Top Floor

**Type:** 2 bed / 2 bath, 1,040 sq ft
**Rent:** $2,650 / mo
**Status:** Occupied (lease ends 2026-12-31)

The penthouse breaks the stack pattern: it absorbs the smaller `03` unit's
footprint to gain a second bedroom and an en-suite bath. Vaulted ceiling in
the living room (10.5 ft at the ridge) and a private roof-deck stair.

- Roof access: a locked hatch in the hall ceiling serves building maintenance;
  HVAC condensers for the whole `04` stack sit on the roof above this unit
- Light: dual exposure, east and north, plus a south-facing skylight over the
  stairwell
- Premium for top-floor quiet and no upstairs neighbor

Everything below repeats the [[z=2|standard layout]]. The mechanical roof
serving this unit also serves [[z=1|the garden unit's]] water heater.

---
3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Pigeon Payments API
service: api.pigeonpay.dev
version: v2
baseUrl: https://api.pigeonpay.dev/v2
---
The Pigeon Payments API moves money between accounts with idempotent,
signed requests. This document stacks from the surface (what the API is
and why) down to the wire-level reference (exact fields, errors, and
rate limits). Read top to bottom, or drop straight to the depth you need.

@plane z=0 label="Overview" x=0 y=0
# Pigeon Payments API

A small REST API for creating charges, refunds, and payouts. Every
write is idempotent, every response is JSON, and every error carries a
machine-readable `code`.

- **Base URL:** `https://api.pigeonpay.dev/v2`
- **Format:** JSON over HTTPS only (plain HTTP is refused)
- **Auth:** bearer secret key, see [[z=1|Authentication]]
- **Stability:** additive changes only within `v2`

A first request, end to end:

```bash
curl https://api.pigeonpay.dev/v2/charges \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer sk_live_4f2a..." \
  -H "Idempotency-Key: chg_2026_06_23_001" \
  -d amount=4200 \
  -d currency=usd \
  -d source=card_9xQ
```

That returns a `charge` object. To understand the pieces, go down one
layer to [[z=1|Concepts]], or jump to the [[z=3|Endpoint reference]] if
you already know the shape.

@plane z=1 label="Concepts" x=0 y=0
# Core concepts

Three ideas carry the whole API.

**Authentication.** Send your secret key as a bearer token on every
request. Keys are environment-scoped: `sk_test_...` hits the sandbox,
`sk_live_...` moves real money. Never put a secret key in client code.

**Idempotency.** Any POST may carry an `Idempotency-Key` header. The
first request with a given key executes; replays return the original
result instead of charging twice. Keys are retained for 24 hours.

**Resources.** Everything is one of four objects:

| Resource | Created by      | Represents                     |
|----------|----------------|--------------------------------|
| charge   | `POST /charges`| money pulled from a source     |
| refund   | `POST /refunds`| money returned for a charge    |
| payout   | `POST /payouts`| money sent to a bank account   |
| event    | (system)       | a webhook-delivered state change|

Once these click, the [[z=2|request lifecycle]] explains how a call
actually flows through the system.

@plane z=2 label="Lifecycle" x=1 y=0
# Request lifecycle

A write travels through five stages. Each stage can short-circuit with
an error, documented at [[z=4|Errors]].

1. **TLS + parse.** The request must be HTTPS and a valid form or JSON
   body. Malformed bodies fail fast with `400 invalid_request`.
2. **Authenticate.** The bearer key is resolved to an account. A bad or
   revoked key returns `401 unauthorized`.
3. **Idempotency check.** If the `Idempotency-Key` was seen before, the
   stored response is replayed and no work happens.
4. **Validate + execute.** Field rules run (see [[z=3|Endpoints]]), then
   the charge, refund, or payout is attempted against the processor.
5. **Persist + emit.** The result is saved and a webhook `event` is
   queued for delivery.

Latency budget, p50 to p99:

```text
parse      ~2ms
auth       ~5ms
execute    40ms - 900ms   (processor-bound)
persist    ~8ms
```

@plane z=3 label="Endpoints" x=1 y=0
# Endpoint reference

## POST /charges

Create a charge against a saved source.

Request fields:

- `amount` (integer, required): smallest currency unit, for example
  cents. Must be `>= 50`.
- `currency` (string, required): ISO 4217 lowercase, for example `usd`.
- `source` (string, required): a `card_` or `bank_` token.
- `capture` (boolean, optional): default `true`. When `false`, the
  charge is authorized only and must be captured within 7 days.

Response: a `charge` object.

```json
{
  "id": "chg_9xQ7",
  "object": "charge",
  "amount": 4200,
  "currency": "usd",
  "status": "succeeded",
  "captured": true,
  "created": 1750636800
}
```

## POST /refunds

Refund all or part of a captured charge.

- `charge` (string, required): the `chg_` id to refund.
- `amount` (integer, optional): defaults to the full remaining amount.

## GET /charges/{id}

Fetch one charge by id. Returns the same `charge` object, or
`404 not_found`. For the full failure list, descend to [[z=4|Errors]].

@plane z=4 label="Errors and limits" x=2 y=0
# Errors and rate limits

## Error shape

Every error is HTTP status plus a JSON body with a stable `code`.

```json
{
  "error": {
    "code": "card_declined",
    "message": "The card was declined.",
    "param": "source",
    "type": "card_error"
  }
}
```

## Error codes

| Status | code             | Meaning                              |
|--------|------------------|--------------------------------------|
| 400    | invalid_request  | missing or malformed field           |
| 401    | unauthorized     | bad, missing, or revoked key         |
| 402    | card_declined    | processor refused the charge         |
| 404    | not_found        | no resource with that id             |
| 409    | idempotency_conflict | key reused with a different body |
| 429    | rate_limited     | too many requests, back off          |

## Rate limits

- 100 requests per second per live key, burst to 200.
- Limit state is returned on every response:

```text
RateLimit-Limit: 100
RateLimit-Remaining: 87
RateLimit-Reset: 1750636860
```

On `429`, wait until `RateLimit-Reset` then retry with the same
`Idempotency-Key` so the retry is safe. To revisit how that header is
honored, climb back to [[z=2|the lifecycle]] or the [[z=0|overview]].

---
3md: 1.0
axis: version
title: Payments API across v1, v2, and v3
service: payments.example.com
base_path: /api
maintainer: Platform Team
---
The Payments API has shipped three major versions. Each plane below is one
version of the same resource, the charge, so you can read the surface side by
side: the request shape, the auth model, and the breaking changes. Newer planes
sit deeper on the Z axis. Start at [[z=1|v1]] and walk forward to
[[z=3|v3]].

@plane z=1 x=0 y=0 label="v1 (legacy)"
# v1: `POST /api/v1/charges`

Status: deprecated, sunset 2026-12-31. New integrations must not use v1.

v1 was the first public release. Auth is a single API key sent as a query
parameter, amounts are floating-point dollars, and every error returns HTTP 200
with a `success: false` body.

## Request

```http
POST /api/v1/charges?api_key=sk_live_8f3a HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "amount": 19.99,
  "currency": "usd",
  "card_number": "4242424242424242",
  "exp": "12/27",
  "cvc": "123"
}
```

## Response

```json
{
  "success": true,
  "charge_id": "ch_1029",
  "amount": 19.99,
  "paid": true
}
```

## Known problems

- API key in the URL leaks into access logs and browser history.
- Raw card numbers cross your servers, so every caller is in PCI scope.
- Floating-point `amount` causes rounding drift (`0.1 + 0.2`).
- Errors return `200 OK`, so clients cannot rely on status codes.

These are exactly the issues fixed in [[z=2|v2]].

@plane z=2 x=1 y=0 label="v2 (current)"
# v2: `POST /api/v2/charges`

Status: current, recommended for all production traffic.

v2 moves the key into an `Authorization` header, switches amounts to integer
minor units (cents), and tokenizes cards through a separate endpoint so raw PANs
never touch your backend. Errors now use real HTTP status codes plus a stable
machine-readable `code`.

## Request

```http
POST /api/v2/charges HTTP/1.1
Authorization: Bearer sk_live_8f3a
Content-Type: application/json
Idempotency-Key: 7c1e-44ab

{
  "amount": 1999,
  "currency": "usd",
  "source": "tok_visa_4242",
  "capture": true
}
```

## Response

```json
{
  "id": "ch_2f9c",
  "object": "charge",
  "amount": 1999,
  "currency": "usd",
  "status": "succeeded",
  "captured": true
}
```

## What changed from v1

| Concern    | v1                       | v2                          |
| ---------- | ------------------------ | --------------------------- |
| Auth       | `?api_key=` query param  | `Authorization: Bearer`     |
| Amount     | float dollars (`19.99`)  | integer cents (`1999`)      |
| Card data  | raw PAN in request       | `source` token (`tok_...`)  |
| Errors     | `200` + `success: false` | real status + error `code`  |
| Retries    | unsafe                   | `Idempotency-Key` header    |

## Error shape

```json
{
  "error": {
    "type": "card_error",
    "code": "card_declined",
    "message": "Your card was declined."
  }
}
```

See the migration notes that carried into [[z=3|v3]].

@plane z=3 x=2 y=0 label="v3 (preview)"
# v3: `POST /api/v3/payment_intents`

Status: preview, generally available 2026-Q4. Stable enough for staging.

v3 renames the core resource from `charge` to `payment_intent` to support
multi-step flows (authorize now, capture later, handle 3-D Secure). Money is now
a structured object instead of two loose fields, and confirmation is explicit.

## Request

```http
POST /api/v3/payment_intents HTTP/1.1
Authorization: Bearer sk_live_8f3a
Content-Type: application/json
Idempotency-Key: 9d2f-77be

{
  "money": { "amount": 1999, "currency": "usd" },
  "payment_method": "pm_card_visa",
  "confirm": true,
  "capture_method": "automatic"
}
```

## Response

```json
{
  "id": "pi_3kL8",
  "object": "payment_intent",
  "money": { "amount": 1999, "currency": "usd" },
  "status": "requires_capture",
  "next_action": null
}
```

## Breaking changes from v2

1. Endpoint and resource renamed: `charge` becomes `payment_intent`.
2. `amount` plus `currency` collapse into one nested `money` object.
3. `source` is renamed `payment_method` and now requires a `pm_` token.
4. A new lifecycle replaces the boolean `captured`: statuses are
   `requires_confirmation`, `requires_capture`, `processing`, and `succeeded`.
5. Strong Customer Authentication can return `next_action` with a redirect URL.

## Migration path

- v2 stays supported through 2027, so migrate incrementally.
- Map your v2 `source` tokens to v3 `payment_method` ids during checkout.
- Treat any non-`succeeded` status as not-yet-paid rather than a hard failure.

Coming from the original design? Compare against [[z=1|v1]] to see how far the
auth and money models have moved.

3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: Apollo 11: Powered Descent, Minute by Minute
view: present
epoch: 1969-07-20T20:08:16Z
source: NASA Mission Report AS-506, Apollo 11 Technical Crew Debriefing
category: Information
---
July 20, 1969. The Eagle separates from Columbia at 100 km altitude. What follows is 12 minutes and 36 seconds that change everything.

@plane z=0 label="PDI — 20:08:16 UTC"
# Powered Descent Initiation

Altitude: **15,243 m** | Velocity: **1,695 m/s horizontal**

The descent engine ignites at 10% throttle, then ramps to full power. Armstrong and Aldrin are lying on their backs, faces pointed toward the lunar surface below.

Mission Control's guidance officer **Steve Bales**, 26 years old, watches the numbers.

> "We are go for PDI." — CAPCOM Charlie Duke

The computer running the descent is an AGC — Apollo Guidance Computer — with **4 KB of RAM** and a 2 MHz clock. It will make every critical decision in the next 12 minutes.

**Burn duration planned:** 756 seconds

@plane z=1 label="High Gate — 20:13:00 UTC"
# High Gate: 2,400 m

Altitude: **2,400 m** | Downrange: ~500 m from target

The LM pitches upright. For the first time, Armstrong can see the surface through the triangular window. He has **seconds** to assess the landing zone.

What he sees: the computer is guiding them into **West Crater** — a 180-meter bowl strewn with boulders the size of cars.

> "That's not a good place to land." — Neil Armstrong (debriefing)

He switches to **P66** — manual attitude hold — and takes the stick. He will fly the rest manually, searching for clear ground.

Fuel remaining: **~8%**

[[z=2|See the 1202 alarm two minutes earlier →]]

@plane z=2 label="1202 Alarm — 20:14:18 UTC"
# The 1202 Alarm

*This plane jumps back 56 seconds — the crisis inside the cabin.*

Altitude: **~3,000 m** | Time to landing: ~120 seconds

The AGC flashes **1202: EXECUTIVE OVERFLOW**. The computer is being asked to do more than it can handle — radar data is flooding the processor.

In Mission Control, 26-year-old **Jack Garman** has a handwritten cheat sheet taped to his console listing every alarm code and whether it's safe to continue.

> "We're GO on that alarm." — Steve Bales to Flight Director Gene Kranz

The alarm fires **four more times**. Each time: GO.

The decision to continue — made in under two seconds, by a man barely old enough to rent a car — saves the mission.

**1202 is a restart alarm, not a failure. The computer is prioritizing correctly.**

@plane z=3 label="Low Gate — 20:17:02 UTC"
# Low Gate: 150 m

Altitude: **150 m** | Horizontal velocity: **~1 m/s** (almost zeroed)

Armstrong has found a clear patch beyond West Crater. He is descending at **1 m/s vertical**, drifting slightly forward.

Fuel state is now critical. Mission rules require abort if fuel drops below **2%** before touchdown.

CAPCOM Charlie Duke, normally unflappable, is reading altitudes so fast his voice is clipping:

> "Sixty seconds." — Duke (fuel warning: 60 seconds of hover remaining)

In the LM, **Buzz Aldrin** is calling out numbers in a flat, rapid monotone:

> "Forty feet, down two and a half. Picking up some dust. Thirty feet, two and a half down. Faint shadow. Four forward. Four forward. Drifting to the right a little."

@plane z=4 label="Touchdown — 20:17:40 UTC"
# Touchdown: Sea of Tranquility

**Altitude: 0 m**
**Mission Elapsed Time: 102:45:40**
**Fuel remaining at engine cutoff: ~45 seconds**

A **contact light** — one of three 5-foot probes hanging below the landing pads — touches the surface and triggers the blue light on the instrument panel.

> "Contact light." — Aldrin

Three seconds later, engine off. The LM settles.

> "Shutdown."
> "Okay, engine stop."
> "Houston, Tranquility Base here. The Eagle has landed."

**Twelve minutes and thirty-six seconds** from PDI to wheels down. Armstrong flew the last 100 meters by hand, to a spot he chose in real time, with less flight time on the actual vehicle than any test pilot in history.

Gene Kranz, Flight Director: *"I think it was the best [landing] we could have had."*

@plane z=5 label="The Numbers"
# What the Data Says

| Moment | Altitude | Fuel Remaining |
|---|---|---|
| PDI | 15,243 m | 100% |
| High Gate | 2,400 m | ~12% |
| 1202 Alarm | ~3,000 m | ~10% |
| Low Gate | 150 m | ~4% |
| Touchdown | 0 m | **~1.2%** |

**Total burn:** 756 seconds (planned) / ~752 seconds (actual)
**Manual flight time:** ~94 seconds
**Distance flown past original target:** ~600 m downrange

The AGC 1202 alarm has since been called *the most important two seconds in computer science history* — the moment a system correctly chose what to drop and what to keep under load, and a human trusted it.

**Steve Bales** received the Presidential Medal of Freedom alongside the Apollo 11 crew. He was the youngest person ever awarded it at that time.

@plane z=6 label="Primary Sources"
# Read It Yourself

**Mission transcripts (word for word):**
- [Apollo 11 Air-to-Ground Voice Transcriptions](https://www.hq.nasa.gov/alsj/a11/a11transcript_tec.pdf) — NASA, 1969

**The 1202 decision in depth:**
- Don Eyles, *Sunburst and Luminary* (2018) — written by the MIT programmer who wrote the landing guidance code

**Aldrin's altitude calls (audio):**
- Search "Eagle has landed audio" — the 17-minute descent recording is on NASA's public archive

**Jack Garman's cheat sheet:**
- Reproduced in *Digital Apollo* by David Mindell (2008), MIT Press — the definitive technical history

> "We were always aware that we were the tip of a very long spear." — Michael Collins, Command Module Pilot, orbiting 100 km above while this happened

3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: APT Kill Chain: Recon to Exfil
view: present
incident: CORVID-2026-0041
actor: UNC4872 (suspected state-sponsored, East Asia)
target: CorvidLabs internal PKI + code-signing infra
detection: 2026-06-21T03:17Z
severity: CRITICAL
analyst: leif
tlp: RED
category: Engineering
---
> Incident timeline reconstructed from Zeek logs, EDR telemetry, and Vault audit trails. All timestamps UTC. [[z=6|Jump to containment actions]].

@plane z=0 label="Recon"
# Step 0 — Passive Recon
**2026-06-14 through 2026-06-18 | Severity: LOW**

Attacker performed passive OSINT before touching internal networks.

| Source | Artefact | Notes |
|--------|----------|-------|
| LinkedIn | 3 engineers with "PKI" in title | Harvested via Sales Navigator scraping |
| GitHub | `swift-pinata` CI workflow | Leaked `SIGN_CERT_PASS` env var name (no value) |
| Shodan | `pki.corvid.internal:8200` | Vault UI exposed on AS 14061 (DO) via dev VPN misconfiguration |
| crt.sh | `*.corvid.internal` certs | Enumerated 14 internal subdomains from CT logs |

**Attacker built a near-complete org chart from public data alone.**

> IOC: `185.220.101.47` (Tor exit) hitting crt.sh and Shodan API with identical UA string across sessions.

@plane z=1 label="Initial Access"
# Step 1 — Initial Access
**2026-06-18T22:41Z | Severity: HIGH**

Spearphish targeting `@corvid.dev` engineers. Subject: *"Re: swift-pinata signing key rotation schedule"*

```
From: leif-security@corvid-labs.net   ← typosquat, registered 2026-06-12
X-Mailer: Outlook 16.0 (spoofed)
Attachment: Q2-signing-rotation.pdf.lnk  (Windows LNK, 1.2 MB)
```

- **2 of 9** recipients clicked (macOS — LNK harmless, but macOS Safari auto-previewed the linked URL)
- Clicked URL: `https://doc-share.corvid-labs.net/view?token=eyJ...` → harvested OAuth token via open-redirect
- **One victim** had `vault` CLI session token in browser localStorage from a dev demo

**MITRE:** T1566.001 (Spearphishing Attachment) + T1550.001 (Application Access Token)

@plane z=2 label="Persistence"
# Step 2 — Credential Theft + Persistence
**2026-06-19T00:03Z – 01:55Z | Severity: CRITICAL**

Using the stolen Vault token (TTL: 24h, policy: `pki-intermediate-read`):

```sh
# Attacker commands reconstructed from Vault audit log
vault read pki_int/cert/ca_chain          # 00:03Z ✓
vault list pki_int/certs                  # 00:04Z ✓ — 847 cert serials
vault write pki_int/issue/internal-tls \  # 00:11Z ✓
  common_name="git.corvid.internal" \
  ttl=8760h
# ↑ Issued wildcard-equivalent cert for internal git, valid 1 year
```

Attacker then:
1. Enrolled their cert into a **Let's Encrypt ACME account** (durable external anchor)
2. Dropped a GitHub Actions runner token via a **fork PR** on `swift-pinata` (Actions secret poisoning attempt — **blocked** by branch protection)
3. Created a cron `~/.zshrc` entry on victim's MBP via the phish re-redirect

**Dwell time before detection: 51 hours, 14 minutes.**

@plane z=3 label="Lateral Movement"
# Step 3 — Lateral Movement
**2026-06-20T05:22Z – 14:48Z | Severity: HIGH**

Pivoted from `mbp-leif-dev` → internal build infra using the fraudulently issued cert.

```
05:22Z  TLS handshake: 185.220.101.47 → build01.corvid.internal:443
        Subject: CN=git.corvid.internal  ← attacker cert, accepted!
05:29Z  git clone ssh://build01/signing-keys.git  (empty repo lure)
06:01Z  SSH brute-force: build01 → vault01 (47 attempts, all failed)
07:14Z  LDAP bind using cert SAN as identity (Vault PKI auth backend)
14:48Z  vault read secret/ci/codesign/p12_passphrase  ← FIRST HIT ON CROWN JEWELS
```

**Blast radius estimate:** 4 internal hosts reachable with issued cert.
**Stopped short of:** HSM partition — required FIDO2 + IP allowlist. HSM never touched.

> IOC: `build01` → `vault01` LDAP bind with non-human-origin cert SAN at 07:14Z. Should have paged. Didn't. [[z=5|See detection gaps]].

@plane z=4 label="Exfil Attempt"
# Step 4 — Exfiltration Attempt
**2026-06-21T02:58Z – 03:17Z | Severity: CRITICAL**

19-minute window before automated detection triggered:

```
02:58Z  vault read secret/ci/codesign/p12_passphrase   ✓ (value returned, 64-char hex)
03:01Z  vault read secret/ci/codesign/apple_api_key    ✓
03:04Z  vault read secret/ci/notarization/asc_provider ✓
03:09Z  DNS TXT query: p12hex.<attacker-c2>.xyz         ← exfil via DNS
03:11Z  DNS TXT query: apikey.<attacker-c2>.xyz         ← exfil via DNS
03:17Z  Vault anomaly rule fires: 3 secret reads in <10min from non-CI IP
        PagerDuty: P1 → leif@corvid.dev  ← DETECTION
```

**Exfiltrated (confirmed):** p12 passphrase, Apple API key, ASC provider ID
**Not exfiltrated:** p12 file itself (stored in separate Vault namespace, token lacked access)
**Attack surface:** An attacker with these 3 values + the p12 (obtainable from CI artifacts) could sign and notarize malicious macOS binaries as CorvidLabs.

@plane z=5 label="Detection Gaps"
# Step 5 — Detection Gaps Found
**Post-incident analysis | Severity: N/A (learning)**

Things that should have fired but didn't:

- [ ] **No alert on Vault PKI issuance outside CI hours** — cert issued at 00:11Z, no human approved
- [ ] **No mTLS pinning on `build01`** — accepted any cert signed by internal CA
- [ ] **OAuth token localStorage** — dev demo left active session, no idle timeout
- [ ] **DNS exfil blind spot** — Zeek had no TXT query anomaly rule; C2 domain `<attacker-c2>.xyz` was 6 days old (would have fired on new-domain rule had it existed)
- [ ] **No break-glass on intermediate CA** — Vault `pki-intermediate-read` policy too broad; `issue` verb should have required MFA step-up
- [x] **HSM boundary held** — FIDO2 + IP allowlist prevented HSM access ✓
- [x] **Branch protection blocked CI poisoning** ✓
- [x] **Anomaly rule on rapid secret reads did fire** — but 19 minutes too late ✓

> Priority remediation: revoke intermediate CA, rotate all 3 exfiltrated secrets, enforce MFA step-up on `pki_int/issue`.

@plane z=6 label="Containment"
# Step 6 — Containment + Remediation
**2026-06-21T03:17Z onward | Owner: leif**

- [x] `03:19Z` — Revoke Vault token `s.XkQ9...` (stolen OAuth session)
- [x] `03:22Z` — Revoke fraudulent cert serial `2f:ab:9c:...` via CRL push
- [x] `03:31Z` — Rotate `secret/ci/codesign/p12_passphrase` + `apple_api_key`
- [x] `03:45Z` — Notify Apple: ASC provider ID potentially compromised
- [x] `04:10Z` — Rotate intermediate CA (new serial, OCSP stapling enabled)
- [x] `06:00Z` — Ship emergency 3.2.1 patch: invalidate all tokens issued during dwell window
- [ ] `2026-06-28` — Deploy DNS TXT anomaly detection in Zeek (owner: infra)
- [ ] `2026-06-28` — Add MFA step-up policy to `pki_int/issue` verb (owner: security)
- [ ] `2026-07-05` — mTLS pinning on all internal build hosts (owner: infra)
- [ ] `2026-07-12` — Red team exercise targeting HSM partition (owner: leif)

**Estimated impact avoided:** Code-signing capability for `CorvidLabs` Apple Developer account — 23 published packages, ~140k weekly downloads. Supply chain attack surface closed.

> Full incident report: `incident/CORVID-2026-0041.md` in the internal wiki.

axis: time
title: Nocturne Atlas - Track Listing
artist: Halcyon Drift

runtime: "38:14"

An album, one track per plane, laid out along the time axis as the running order.
Each plane is a track sheet: timing, key, mood, and a line of liner notes.
Scrub the Z axis to move through the record side A into side B.

@plane z=0 label="A1 - Harbor Lights" time="00:00" length="4:12" key="A minor"
# A1. Harbor Lights

The opener. A slow synth swell and a single repeating piano figure.

- BPM: 84
- Mood: arrival, anticipation
- Liner: *We started here because every map needs a port.*

The record drifts out to sea on [[z=1|the next track]].

@plane z=1 label="A2 - Salt and Static" time="04:12" length="3:48" key="C major"
# A2. Salt and Static

Brighter. A motorik drum pattern enters, tape hiss left in on purpose.

- BPM: 112
- Mood: motion, open road
- Liner: *The static is the sound of the radio almost finding a station.*

@plane z=2 label="A3 - Low Country" time="08:00" length="6:30" key="D dorian"
# A3. Low Country

The long centerpiece of side A. A slow build with a brass swell at 4:30.

- BPM: 76
- Mood: vast, patient
- Liner: *Recorded in one take at 3am with the windows open.*

@plane z=3 label="B1 - Cold Coast" time="14:30" length="4:05" key="F sharp minor"
# B1. Cold Coast

Side B opens darker and sparser. Just bass, a vocal sample, and reverb.

- BPM: 90
- Mood: solitude, distance
- Liner: *The coldest song we have ever made, and our favorite.*

@plane z=4 label="B2 - Halcyon Drift" time="18:35" length="5:20" key="B flat major"
# B2. Halcyon Drift

The title track. Everything from the record returns, layered together.

- BPM: 100
- Mood: gathering, release
- Liner: *If you only hear one, hear this one.*

@plane z=5 label="B3 - Last Light" time="23:55" length="14:19" key="A minor"
# B3. Last Light

The closer, and the longest cut. It resolves back to the opening key of A minor,
closing the loop on the whole record.

- BPM: 60
- Mood: dusk, farewell
- Liner: *Ends on the same chord [[z=0|Harbor Lights]] began on. The atlas folds shut.*

author: Mara Quill
medium: four-panel comic

---
A four-panel comic strip. Each plane is one panel, read in Z order.
The keeper waits, the storm breaks, the light holds, the morning comes.
Scrub the Z axis to read the panels in sequence.

@plane z=0 label="Panel 1 - The Watch" caption="Night falls on the cape"
# Panel 1

A wide establishing shot. The lighthouse stands alone on a black rock.

```
.......*..........
....~~~~~~~~~......
...|######|.......
...|##()##|.......
...|######|.......
...|######|.......
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```

KEEPER: *Another quiet night. Good.*

The sea is flat. The lamp room glows soft gold.

---
3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: Corvid Design System - Layer Stack
team: CorvidLabs Design
version: "2.0"
basis: 8px spatial grid
---
A design system documented as a stack of layers. Each plane is one layer of
the system, from the spatial grid at the bottom up through color, type, and the
finished components. Toggle layers independently in the viewer, or stack them
to see how a component is built from the layers beneath it.

@plane z=0 label="Layer 0 - Spatial Grid" unit="8px"
# Layer 0 - Spatial Grid

Everything aligns to an 8px grid. Spacing tokens are multiples of the base unit.
The grid below shows the column and gutter rhythm of a card.

```
+ . . + . . + . . + . . +
. . . . . . . . . . . . .
. . . . . . . . . . . . .
+ . . + . . + . . + . . +
. . . . . . . . . . . . .
. . . . . . . . . . . . .
+ . . + . . + . . + . . +
```

Tokens: `space-1: 8px`, `space-2: 16px`, `space-3: 24px`, `space-4: 32px`.
The color layer sits directly on top: see [[z=1|color]].

@plane z=1 label="Layer 1 - Color" mode="light + dark"
# Layer 1 - Color

Semantic color tokens, not raw hex, are what components consume.

| Token | Light | Dark | Use |
|-------|-------|------|-----|
| `bg.surface` | #FFFFFF | #14171C | card and sheet backgrounds |
| `bg.subtle` | #F4F6F8 | #1C2127 | hover, zebra rows |
| `fg.default` | #14171C | #E8ECF1 | body text |
| `fg.muted` | #5B6470 | #9AA4B2 | captions, hints |
| `accent` | #2E6BFF | #5C8CFF | primary actions, links |
| `danger` | #D7263D | #FF5C6E | destructive actions |

Contrast: every text-on-surface pair meets WCAG AA (4.5:1 or better).

@plane z=2 label="Layer 2 - Typography" scale="1.25 major third"
# Layer 2 - Typography

A modular type scale with a 1.25 ratio, built on the 8px grid for line height.

| Role | Size | Line | Weight |
|------|------|------|--------|
| `display` | 40px | 48px | 700 |
| `title` | 32px | 40px | 700 |
| `heading` | 24px | 32px | 600 |
| `body` | 16px | 24px | 400 |
| `caption` | 13px | 16px | 400 |

Stack: system UI sans for interface, monospace for code and tokens.
With grid, color, and type defined, components can be assembled: see [[z=3|components]].

@plane z=3 label="Layer 3 - Components" depends="grid + color + type"
# Layer 3 - Components

A component is the three layers below it, composed. Here is the Button,
specified as the sum of grid spacing, color tokens, and type roles.

```
+------------------------+
|  [  Save changes  ]    |   primary: bg=accent  fg=#FFFFFF
+------------------------+
|  [  Cancel  ]          |   secondary: bg=bg.subtle fg=fg.default
+------------------------+
|  [  Delete  ]          |   danger: bg=danger fg=#FFFFFF
+------------------------+
```

Button spec:
- Padding: `space-1` vertical, `space-2` horizontal (from [[z=0|the grid]]).
- Fill and text: from [[z=1|color]] semantic tokens.
- Label: `body` weight 600 (from [[z=2|typography]]).
- States: default, hover (`bg.subtle` overlay), focus (2px `accent` ring), disabled (40% opacity).

Every component in the library is documented this same way: a recipe over the layers beneath it.

3md: 1.0
axis: stanza
title: Tide Tables
poet: Eira Vane
form: free verse, four stanzas
theme: memory and the sea
---
A poem in four stanzas. Each plane holds one stanza.
Read them in Z order, or let each stanza stand alone as its own plane.

@plane z=0 label="Stanza I - Ebb"
# I

The water keeps its own slow ledger,
counting the stones it takes and returns,
and we, who learn arithmetic late,
mistake the borrowing for loss.

Read on to [[z=1|the second stanza]].

@plane z=1 label="Stanza II - Slack"
# II

There is an hour when the sea forgets
which way it meant to go,
and the whole gray field of it lies still,
deciding nothing, owing nothing,
a held breath the length of a coast.

@plane z=2 label="Stanza III - Flood"
# III

Then it remembers. It comes back
the way a name comes back,
all at once and from everywhere,
filling the prints we left
as if we had never stepped away.

@plane z=3 label="Stanza IV - Return"
# IV

So I have stopped grieving the going out.
The tide is not a door that shuts.
It is the long sentence of a voice
that pauses only to say more,
and will, and will, and will.

Begin again at [[z=0|the ebb]].

3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: ASL Fingerspelling Flashcards
view: single
subject: American Sign Language
level: beginner
handshape_count: 26
category: Education
---
Tap through each letter. Read the handshape cue, try it with your hand, then flip to check. Work A → Z or shuffle by jumping to any plane.

@plane z=0 label="A"
# A
```
  ___
 /   \
| fist |
 \___/
  thumb
  side
```
**Handshape:** Closed fist, thumb resting against the side of the index finger — *not* tucked inside.

**Memory hook:** The **A**-frame of a tent: solid, upright, no gaps.

**Common error:** Students tuck the thumb like an **S**. Keep thumb visible on the side.

[[z=1|Next: B →]]

@plane z=1 label="B"
# B
```
  ||||
  ||||   ← four fingers flat, together
  |  |
  thumb
  tucked
```
**Handshape:** Four fingers straight up and pressed together. Thumb folded across the palm.

**Memory hook:** The two bumps on a capital **B** become your fingers stacked tall.

**Drill:** Spell B-U-S, B-A-G, B-I-G — you'll use B constantly in everyday words.

[[z=0|← A]] · [[z=2|C →]]

@plane z=2 label="C"
# C
```
   __
  /
 |      ← curved hand, open
  \
   --
```
**Handshape:** Curve all fingers and thumb into a **C** shape — like you're holding a tennis ball.

**Memory hook:** The letter **C** literally is the shape. This one's free.

**Watch out:** Don't let the pinky drop away; keep all five fingers in the curve.

[[z=1|← B]] · [[z=3|D →]]

@plane z=3 label="D"
# D
```
  O   ← index tip touches thumb tip
  |
  |||  ← middle, ring, pinky curl
```
**Handshape:** Index finger points up. Middle, ring, and pinky curl down. Thumb tip meets index tip, forming a circle on the side.

**Memory hook:** The circle + straight line = the printed letter **D**.

**Speed tip:** Transition D → E smoothly: just collapse the index finger.

[[z=2|← C]] · [[z=4|E →]]

@plane z=4 label="E"
# E
```
  ||||  ← fingers hooked/bent
  ||||
  ====  ← thumb tucked under
```
**Handshape:** All four fingers bend/hook at the middle knuckle. Thumb tucks underneath, pressing against the fingertips.

**Memory hook:** Three horizontal bars of the letter **E** become three rows of knuckles.

**Common error:** Flattening it into a claw. Keep the thumb actively pressing up.

[[z=3|← D]] · [[z=5|F →]]

@plane z=5 label="F"
# F
```
  O     ← index tip + thumb tip circle
  |||   ← middle, ring, pinky straight up
```
**Handshape:** Index fingertip touches thumb tip (small O). Middle, ring, and pinky extend straight up.

**Memory hook:** The three prongs of the letter **F** are your three standing fingers.

**Contrast drill:** Practice F next to [[z=9|K]] — beginners confuse the two. F's circle faces forward; K has no circle.

[[z=4|← E]] · [[z=6|Quick-check quiz →]]

@plane z=6 label="Quiz: A–F"
# Quick Check: Letters A – F

Cover the handshape descriptions and test yourself. Say the letter aloud, form the shape, then peek.

| Letter | One-word cue | Most common mistake |
|--------|-------------|-------------------|
| **A** | Fist, thumb side | Thumb tucked (makes S) |
| **B** | Flat four, thumb in | Fingers spread apart |
| **C** | Open curve | Pinky drooping |
| **D** | Index up, circle side | Circle too large |
| **E** | Hooked, thumb under | Flat claw, thumb out |
| **F** | Circle, three up | Confusing with K |

**Practice word:** Spell **F-A-C-E** three times, aiming for smooth transitions.

> "Fluency isn't speed — it's clarity at increasing speed." — common ASL instructor mantra

[[z=0|Start over: A →]]

3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: Infant Developmental Milestones by Month
source: CDC "Learn the Signs. Act Early." and AAP Bright Futures guidance
note: Ranges are typical. Every baby develops on their own timeline.
---
A month-by-month walk through what most babies are doing across four domains:
motor, social/emotional, language, and cognitive. Each plane is one age window.
Use these as gentle checkpoints, not a scorecard. If a milestone is missing well
past its window, that is worth a conversation with your pediatrician.

@plane z=2 x=0 y=0 label="2 months"
# 2 Months: The Social Smile Arrives

The fog of the newborn weeks starts to lift. Reflexes still dominate, but the
first real two-way moments appear.

- **Motor:** Holds head up briefly during tummy time; movements become smoother
  and less jerky than the newborn flailing.
- **Social/emotional:** The first genuine social smile, in response to your face
  and voice (not just gas). Begins to self-soothe by bringing hands to mouth.
- **Language:** Coos and makes gurgling "ooh" and "aah" sounds. Turns toward
  familiar voices.
- **Cognitive:** Watches faces intently and follows a moving object with the eyes
  for a few seconds.

> Pediatrician checkpoint: the 2-month well visit usually includes the first
> round of vaccines and a weight/length/head-circumference plot.

Next up, the hands take over: see [[z=4|4 months]].

@plane z=4 x=2 y=0 label="4 months"
# 4 Months: Reaching and Belly Laughs

Babies become active explorers. The world is now something to grab.

- **Motor:** Holds head steady without support; pushes up on elbows during tummy
  time; may roll from tummy to back. Brings hands together and reaches for toys.
- **Social/emotional:** Laughs out loud and enjoys playing with people; may cry
  or fuss when play stops.
- **Language:** Babbles with expression and copies some sounds back to you in a
  back-and-forth "conversation."
- **Cognitive:** Reaches for a toy with one hand, follows objects across the full
  arc of vision, and watches faces closely.

Hands that can grab (from [[z=2|2 months]]) now feed straight into sitting and
the pincer grasp ahead at [[z=6|6 months]].

@plane z=6 x=4 y=0 label="6 months"
# 6 Months: Sitting Up to the World

The midpoint of the first year. Babies sit, mouth everything, and start solid
foods around now.

- **Motor:** Sits with little or no support; rolls in both directions; rocks on
  hands and knees as a pre-crawl rehearsal. Rakes small objects toward self.
- **Social/emotional:** Knows familiar faces and begins to recognize strangers.
  Loves looking at self in a mirror.
- **Language:** Strings vowels together when babbling ("ah," "eh," "oh") and
  responds to own name. Makes sounds to show joy and displeasure.
- **Cognitive:** Looks for partially hidden objects; brings things to the mouth
  to investigate; shows curiosity and tries to reach for out-of-reach toys.

> Feeding note: most babies are ready for first solids around 6 months, signaled
> by good head control and sitting with support.

@plane z=9 x=6 y=0 label="9 months"
# 9 Months: Stranger Danger and Object Permanence

Mobility and memory click into place. Out of sight is no longer out of mind.

- **Motor:** Sits without support, gets into a sitting position alone, crawls,
  and pulls to stand at furniture. Develops the pincer grasp (thumb and
  forefinger) to pick up tiny bits of food.
- **Social/emotional:** Clingy with familiar adults and wary of strangers; may
  show separation anxiety when you leave the room.
- **Language:** Understands "no," copies sounds and gestures, and points at
  things. Babbles strings like "mamama" and "babababa."
- **Cognitive:** Looks for objects you hide (object permanence) and plays
  peekaboo with real anticipation.

The clinginess that starts here (a leap from [[z=6|6 months]]) eases as
confidence grows by [[z=12|12 months]].

@plane z=12 x=8 y=0 label="12 months"
# 12 Months: First Words, First Steps

The first birthday. Many babies stand alone, take first steps, and say their
first true words.

- **Motor:** Pulls up to stand and cruises along furniture; may stand alone and
  take a few independent steps. Puts objects into a container and takes them out.
- **Social/emotional:** Has favorite people and toys; shows affection; plays
  games like pat-a-cake. May test reactions during a meal or at bedtime.
- **Language:** Says "mama" and "dada" with meaning plus one or two other words;
  uses simple gestures like waving "bye-bye" and shaking the head for "no."
- **Cognitive:** Explores objects by shaking, banging, and throwing; finds hidden
  objects easily; follows a simple one-step request ("give it to me").

> First-birthday checkpoint: the 12-month visit often includes a developmental
> review of the whole first year, from the [[z=2|social smile]] to first steps.

@plane z=18 x=10 y=0 label="18 months"
# 18 Months: The Determined Toddler

Now firmly a toddler. Walking is steady, words multiply, and the will is strong.

- **Motor:** Walks alone and may run stiffly; pulls toys while walking; helps
  undress; drinks from a cup and eats with a spoon (messily).
- **Social/emotional:** Shows affection openly, plays simple pretend (feeding a
  doll), and may have temper tantrums and stranger fear. Points to show you
  something interesting.
- **Language:** Says several single words and points to one body part when asked.
  Understands far more than they can say.
- **Cognitive:** Knows what ordinary objects are for (phone, brush, spoon),
  scribbles, and follows one-step verbal commands without gestures.

Compared with the [[z=12|first steps]] at a year, movement is now purposeful and
the vocabulary explosion is just beginning.

3md: 1.0
axis: beat
title: Bit Autopsy: The Airline Food Reconstruction
view: present
comic: Leif Halverson
venue: The Punchline, SF
date: 2026-06-23
status: workshopping
category: Story
---
A joke written backwards. Each plane peels off a layer of the onion — setup, misdirect, pivot, punchline — so you can see exactly where the audience turned.

@plane z=0 label="The Setup"
# The Setup

You know what I love about airplane food?

Nothing. Obviously nothing. I'm not a psychopath.

But here's what I've been thinking about lately — those little foil trays. You peel back the foil and there's always a *mystery compartment* you didn't order.

> **Crowd read at this beat:** Nodding. They've been there. You have them.

- Keep eye contact, let it breathe
- Do NOT rush to the misdirect here — the nod is money

@plane z=1 label="The Misdirect"
# The Misdirect

And I always think: what IS that? Is it a bonus? A mistake? Is this flight attendant *freestyling*?

So I ate it. I don't know what it was. Tasted like beige.

It tasted like someone described "food" to a committee.

> **Crowd read:** Light laugh at "beige." They think the bit is about the food being bad.

**They are wrong.**

This is where you pivot. The bit is not about airplane food at all.

@plane z=2 label="The Pivot"
# The Pivot

But then I thought — I do this in my *whole life.*

Someone puts a mystery compartment in front of me and I just... eat it.

A job I didn't quite apply for. A relationship that showed up in the tray next to my actual plans.

My therapist calls this "low self-advocacy."

I call it "bonus food."

> **Crowd read:** First confused pause, then the slow laugh — the "oh no he's going somewhere real" laugh.

- Lean into the silence. 2 full seconds.

@plane z=3 label="The Escalation"
# The Escalation

And the worst part? I always eat it *first.*

Before the thing I actually ordered. The mystery compartment gets priority.

I paid two hundred dollars for this seat. I chose the pasta. And I'm over here eating whatever *Gerald in the galley* decided to throw in.

> **Crowd read:** Gerald is doing work. Don't explain Gerald. Gerald is self-explanatory.

**Tag possibilities:**
- "Gerald has no last name. Gerald answers to no one."
- "I don't even know what airline this is anymore — I just got in the tray."

@plane z=4 label="The Punchline"
# The Punchline

So yeah. I don't love airplane food.

But I respect it.

Because that mystery compartment? That little foil-covered cube of unknown beige?

**That's a metaphor for every major decision I've ever made.**

And it's still better than the pasta I ordered.

> **Crowd read:** The "I did not see that coming but I should have" laugh. The release laugh.

- Thank them, exit clean, do NOT repeat the word "metaphor."

@plane z=5 label="Bit Notes"
# Post-Mortem Notes

**What's working:**
- "Tasted like beige" — keeper, never cut this
- "Gerald in the galley" — crowd keeps laughing at a name, always a good sign
- The pivot at z=2 is the hinge; if you rush it, the bit dies there

**What needs work:**
- The escalation (z=3) runs 12 seconds too long at full energy — try half-speed on "I chose the pasta"
- "Low self-advocacy" might be too therapy-coded for certain rooms; have "no backbone" as a swap

**Kill-switch:** If the crowd is cold through z=1, skip z=2 entirely and go straight to Gerald. Sacrifice the theme, save the laugh.

**Next step:** Try this at Cobb's open mic Thursday, record from the back.

3md: 1.0
axis: stop
title: Blackpoll Warbler: The Atlantic Sprint
view: present
species: Setophaga striata
route: Boreal Canada → Caribbean / South America
peak: Late September – early November
record_nonstop_flight: ~2,770 km over open Atlantic
weight_at_departure: ~20 g (nearly double pre-migration mass)
category: Maps
---
*The Blackpoll Warbler weighs less than a AA battery. Every fall it makes one of the longest nonstop overwater flights of any bird on Earth. This is its journey, stop by stop.*

@plane z=0 label="Breeding Grounds"
## Stop 0 — Boreal Spruce, Northern Canada
**July – August · ~55°N**

Deep in the spruce-fir belt of Quebec, Labrador, and the Yukon, the male Blackpoll sings his thin, rising *tsit-tsit-tsit-tsit* from the canopy top. By late August he has gone quiet.

The bird begins hyperphagia — eating nearly nonstop, targeting high-lipid berries and insects. Body fat climbs from 3 % to 40 % of total mass. Organs shrink. The digestive tract partially atrophies to save weight.

**What to watch for:**
- Pale yellowish feet (field mark)
- Striped cap, white cheeks
- Constant foraging, barely pausing

> *"It is building a fuel tank for a flight over the void."*
> — Pete Dunne, *Pete Dunne's Essential Field Guide Companion*

@plane z=1 label="Staging: Maritime Canada"
## Stop 1 — Bay of Fundy Staging
**Late August – mid September · ~45°N**

Thousands funnel into coastal Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, and Maine. The Bay of Fundy's shoreline is thick with them in goldenrod and alder thickets. Grand Manan Island sees peak counts of **5,000+ per day** during good weather.

They wait. They study the sky.

**Departure conditions required:**
- High-pressure system building to the northwest
- NW winds at 1,500–3,000 m altitude
- Clear skies, falling temperatures

A bird that leaves in a SW headwind dies at sea. The wait can stretch two weeks.

**eBird hotspots:** Brier Island, NS · Seal Cove, Grand Manan · Pemaquid Point, ME

@plane z=2 label="The Atlantic Launch"
## Stop 2 — The Point of No Return
**Mid September – early October · ~42°N**

From Cape Cod, Long Island, or the Jersey Shore, the birds depart after dark, climbing to 3,000–5,000 m. At that altitude, jet-stream-adjacent NW winds carry them SE at ground speeds exceeding **100 km/h**.

```
ALTITUDE PROFILE (approximate)
5,000 m |               ████
4,000 m |           ████████
3,000 m |       ████████████
2,000 m | ██████████████████
1,000 m | ████████████████████
   0 m  | ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓  ← ocean
         Cape Cod → Bermuda →
```

Heart rate: ~1,300 beats per minute at cruise.
Flight duration: **72–88 hours nonstop.**
Fat burned: ~0.5 % of body mass per hour.

They cannot land. There is only water below.

@plane z=3 label="Bermuda Rescue"
## Stop 3 — Bermuda: The Lifeboat
**Late September – October · 32°N, 64°W**

Bermuda sits alone in the Atlantic, 1,050 km from Cape Hatteras. For exhausted or storm-blown birds it is survival. When a cold front stalls a cohort over open ocean, Bermuda's cedar groves and golf courses fill with warblers — thousands landing in a single night.

**Local birders call these "fallouts."**

A healthy bird may skip Bermuda entirely, arcing southeast toward the Caribbean. A storm-caught bird may weigh only 7 g on arrival — less than half its departure mass.

**Recommended lodging for the birder:** The Arboretum, Paget Parish — free entry, remarkable fallout site.

*Bermuda represents ~38 % of all Blackpoll recoveries from geolocator studies.*

@plane z=4 label="Caribbean Landfall"
## Stop 4 — Lesser Antilles Landfall
**Early–mid October · ~13°–17°N**

Barbados, Trinidad, the Windward Islands. The birds drop from the sky at dawn, landing in any tree available — hotel courtyards, mango groves, mangroves. They are emaciated. They sleep.

Over 10–14 days they rebuild fat reserves on tropical fruit and insects.

**Barbados Bird Observatory counts:** peak single-day record — **820 Blackpolls** in one morning, October 9, 2019.

The bird that left New England at 20 g arrived here at roughly 9–11 g. It has burned the equivalent of a human running 5 back-to-back marathons without food or water.

@plane z=5 label="South American Wintering"
## Stop 5 — Amazonian Winter Quarters
**November – March · ~0°–10°S**

The final destination: the Amazon Basin, the Guianas, northwestern Brazil. The Blackpoll disappears into mixed-species flocks in the forest understory, spending five months in near-anonymity.

**Total round-trip distance:** ~12,400 km
**Nonstop oceanic leg:** ~2,770 km (verified by geolocator, 2015 study, *Biology Letters*)
**Survival rate, first year:** ~40 %

In spring the bird retraces the route over land — through Central America, the Gulf Coast, the Mississippi flyway — a gentler northbound journey.

> *It will be back on that same spruce in Quebec by late May.*
> *The same bird. The same tree.*

**Further reading:** DeLuca et al. (2015) "Transoceanic migration by a 12 g songbird." *Biology Letters* 11:20141045.

3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Blue Hole Descent — Dahab, 2024-11-14
view: elevator
site: Blue Hole, Dahab, Egypt (Red Sea)
diver: L. Morrow
buddy: Yasmin Okafor
tank: 12L AL 200bar / EAN32
water temp: 26°C surface · 22°C @40m
visibility: 28m
max depth: 62m
bottom time: 47 min
current: negligible
category: Information
---
Surface briefing complete. Dropping down the wall at 09:14.

@plane z=0 label="Surface (0m)"
# Surface — 09:14

Glassy conditions. Slight haze already burning off. Saddle entry off the platform, negative buoyancy from 0.

Briefed Yasmin on the arch at ~56m — swim-through only if we're on schedule at 50m, no heroics.

**Species logged at entry:**
- *Acanthurus sohal* (sohal tang) — 3 individuals patrolling the surge zone
- *Thalassoma klunzingeri* (klunzinger's wrasse) — M/F pair at rubble edge
- Floating *Halimeda* mat, likely drifted from the lagoon side

> Dissolved O₂ at surface: 7.8 mg/L. Stratification expected below thermocline.

@plane z=1 label="Reef Flat (5–12m)"
# Reef Flat — 5–12m

The plateau before the wall drops away. Acropora table corals the size of dining tables. Rubble patches between them are deceptively alive.

**Notable observations:**
- **Bleaching index: 12%** of *Acropora* tables showing partial bleaching (upper surfaces). Consistent with August thermal event data.
- *Chaetodon fasciatus* (diagonal butterflyfish) pair — territory centred on a *Pocillopora* head at ~9m. Photographed for ongoing corallivory study.
- *Pterois miles* (devil firefish) tucked under a table overhang. Motionless. Classic ambush posture — ID confirmed by fin ray count (13 dorsal spines vs. *P. volitans* 13–14, but meristic pattern matches *miles*).

Visibility opens up dramatically as the wall appears. That first glimpse of open blue below — still gets me every time.

@plane z=2 label="Upper Wall (15–25m)"
# Upper Wall — 15–25m

Vertical limestone. Sponge cover dense here: *Xestospongia testudinaria* barrels up to 90cm diameter. The wall is overhanging in places, creating micro-caves.

**Fish assemblage (transect estimate, 10m × 2m belt):**
| Family | Count | Dominant species |
|---|---|---|
| Labridae | 14 | *Thalassoma lunare* |
| Pomacanthidae | 6 | *Pomacanthus maculosus* |
| Serranidae | 3 | *Cephalopholis miniata* |
| Acanthuridae | 9 | *Acanthurus sohal* |

- *Naso unicornis* (bluespine unicornfish) — lone individual grazing film algae off the wall face at 22m. Uncharacteristically solitary; possibly displaced by the *sohal* aggression zone above.
- First *Chromis dimidiata* (half-and-half chromis) aggregation at 24m, fanning over the wall lip.

@plane z=3 label="Mid Wall (30–42m)"
# Mid Wall — 30–42m

Colour shift is real. Reds gone. Everything cyan and indigo. The *Chromis* schools thin out; larger, slower animals take over.

**Key sightings:**
- **Napoleon wrasse (*Cheilinus undulatus*)** — sub-adult, ~70cm. Circled us at 34m for 90 seconds. Scar tissue on left flank, likely spearfishing encounter. Tagged in our regional database as DH-NW-041.
- *Plectropomus pessuliferus* (roving coral grouper) — 85cm TL, resting on a ledge at 37m. Dominant male coloration.
- *Gorgonia* fan at 40m, ~1.2m span, oriented perpendicular to the wall. Feeding polyps extended — mild current must be running along the wall face even though we feel nothing.

Nitrox MOD for EAN32: **33m (1.4 bar ppO₂)**. We are past it. Monitoring NDL: 14 min remaining at this depth.

@plane z=4 label="The Arch (52–56m)"
# The Arch — 52–56m

The reason people come to Blue Hole. A 26m-wide tunnel punched through the reef at 52–56m, exiting the lagoon side to open ocean. On a good day, the far blue frames silhouettes like a cinema screen.

**Arch traverse notes:**
- Entry at 52m. Arch ceiling ~53m. Exit mouth at 56m. Total swim: ~26m, ~40 sec at moderate fin pace.
- Light column through exit: classic cathedral effect. Photographed *Lutjanus bohar* (two-spot red snapper) school backlit in exit mouth — 30+ individuals.
- *Carcharhinus amblyrhynchos* (grey reef shark) — 1 individual, ~1.4m, patrolling exit. Maintained >4m distance; no threat display. Logged as routine transit.

> ppO₂ at 56m on EAN32: **1.74 bar** — well above 1.6 limit. No lingering. Yasmin signalled abort of the deeper excursion. Correct call.

@plane z=5 label="Max Depth (62m)"
# Max Depth — 62m

Brief excursion off the arch exit, dropping to the sand shelf. 4 minutes max.

**Sediment notes:**
- Carbonate sand. Bioturbation tracks consistent with *Holothuria* sp. (sea cucumber) grazing.
- *Gymnothorax javanicus* (giant moray) nose visible from burrow at base of wall. Estimated 180cm. Did not emerge.
- **Halimeda** meadow extends from the sand shelf into the blue — unbroken to visibility limit (~28m). Significant carbon sequestration sink; flag for next survey.

NDL: 2 min. Ascending.

*Note to self: bring the CTD logger next time. The thermocline between 18–22m felt sharp enough to warrant a proper profile.*

@plane z=6 label="Ascent & Safety Stop (5m)"
# Ascent & Safety Stop — 5m

Slow ascent: 9 m/min per computer. Mandatory 3-min stop at 5m.

**Stop observations:**
- *Acanthaster planci* (crown-of-thorns starfish) — 1 individual on the reef flat at 8m. Arm span ~42cm. Active corallivore. Photographed and logged — third individual this season at this site. Threshold for intervention: 5+ per 100m². Not there yet, but trending.
- Juvenile *Amphiprion bicinctus* (twoband clownfish) — first record on this trip. Hosting in a *Entacmaea quadricolor* that has shifted ~3m shoreward since my last survey in March. Bleaching stress response?

**End of dive:**
- Tank pressure: 60 bar (ascent reserve maintained ✓)
- Max depth logged: 62m
- Bottom time: 47 min
- Safety stop: 3 min @ 5m ✓

> Post-dive: 1L water, 30-min surface interval before second dive. Complete field notes before evening briefing.
