3md: 1.0
axis: frame
legend: o=●
title: Projectile Motion Frame by Frame
subject: Physics
topic: kinematics under gravity
g: 9.8 m/s^2
---
A ball launched horizontally off a table, shown one frame at a time. Each plane
is one frame of the animation, sampled every 0.2 seconds. Horizontal velocity
stays constant while vertical velocity grows under gravity. The numbers behind
the motion are summarized in [[z=4|the final data frame]].

Launch conditions: horizontal speed 4 m/s, initial vertical speed 0, dropped from
height. The ` o ` marks the ball, the `=` row is the table edge, and the bottom
row is the ground.

@plane z=0 label="Frame 0: Launch" t="0.0 s"
```
o
=========
.........
.........
.........
.........
_________
```
# Frame 0: Launch (t = 0.0 s)

The ball leaves the table edge moving horizontally.

- Horizontal position x = 0.0 m
- Vertical drop y = 0.0 m
- Vertical speed vy = 0.0 m/s

Gravity has not yet had time to act, so the ball is still level with the table.

@plane z=1 label="Frame 1" t="0.2 s"
```
.
==o======
.........
.........
.........
.........
_________
```
# Frame 1 (t = 0.2 s)

- x = 4 x 0.2 = 0.8 m
- y = 0.5 x 9.8 x 0.2^2 = 0.20 m
- vy = 9.8 x 0.2 = 1.96 m/s

The ball has moved sideways and begun to fall. The drop is still small because
y grows with the square of time.

@plane z=2 label="Frame 2" t="0.4 s"
```
.
=========
....o....
.........
.........
.........
_________
```
# Frame 2 (t = 0.4 s)

- x = 4 x 0.4 = 1.6 m
- y = 0.5 x 9.8 x 0.4^2 = 0.78 m
- vy = 9.8 x 0.4 = 3.92 m/s

The downward speed has doubled from the previous frame while the sideways speed
is unchanged at 4 m/s.

@plane z=3 label="Frame 3" t="0.6 s"
```
.
=========
.........
.........
......o..
.........
_________
```
# Frame 3 (t = 0.6 s)

- x = 4 x 0.6 = 2.4 m
- y = 0.5 x 9.8 x 0.6^2 = 1.76 m
- vy = 9.8 x 0.6 = 5.88 m/s

Notice the vertical gaps between frames are growing while the horizontal gaps
stay equal. That is the signature of constant horizontal velocity with
accelerating vertical fall.

@plane z=4 label="Frame 4: Impact" t="0.8 s"
```
.
=========
.........
.........
.........
.........
________o
```
# Frame 4: Impact (t = 0.8 s)

- x = 4 x 0.8 = 3.2 m
- y = 0.5 x 9.8 x 0.8^2 = 3.14 m
- vy = 9.8 x 0.8 = 7.84 m/s

The ball reaches the ground after falling 3.14 m.

## Why this works
The two motions are independent. Horizontal: constant velocity, so equal steps.
Vertical: constant acceleration, so steps that grow with time. The table below
collects every frame from [[z=0|launch]] onward.

| Frame | t (s) | x (m) | y (m) | vy (m/s) |
|-------|-------|-------|-------|----------|
| 0     | 0.0   | 0.0   | 0.00  | 0.00     |
| 1     | 0.2   | 0.8   | 0.20  | 1.96     |
| 2     | 0.4   | 1.6   | 0.78  | 3.92     |
| 3     | 0.6   | 2.4   | 1.76  | 5.88     |
| 4     | 0.8   | 3.2   | 3.14  | 7.84     |

3md: 1.0

subject: Astronomy

---
A tour of the Sun's planets ordered by average distance from the Sun. Each plane
is one step outward in depth, from the innermost rocky world to the ice giant at
the edge. The boundary between rocky and giant planets falls at
[[z=5|the asteroid belt and Jupiter]].

@plane z=0 label="The Sun"
# The Sun

The star at the center, holding about 99.8% of the system's mass.

- Type: G2V main sequence star
- Diameter: about 1,391,000 km (109 Earths across)
- Surface temperature: about 5,500 C
- Composition: roughly 73% hydrogen, 25% helium by mass

Everything that follows orbits this object. Distances ahead are measured from
its center.

@plane z=1 label="Mercury"
# Mercury

The smallest planet and the closest to the Sun.

| Property        | Value            |
|-----------------|------------------|
| Distance        | 0.39 AU          |
| Diameter        | 4,879 km         |
| Day length      | 59 Earth days    |
| Year length     | 88 Earth days    |
| Moons           | 0                |

No substantial atmosphere, so temperatures swing from about 430 C in sunlight to
about -180 C at night.

@plane z=2 label="Venus"
# Venus

Earth's near twin in size, but the hottest planet in the system.

| Property        | Value            |
|-----------------|------------------|
| Distance        | 0.72 AU          |
| Diameter        | 12,104 km        |
| Year length     | 225 Earth days   |
| Moons           | 0                |

A thick CO2 atmosphere traps heat, holding the surface near 465 C, hotter than
Mercury despite being farther out. It rotates backward relative to most planets.

@plane z=3 label="Earth"
# Earth

The only known world with life and stable surface liquid water.

| Property        | Value            |
|-----------------|------------------|
| Distance        | 1.00 AU          |
| Diameter        | 12,742 km        |
| Year length     | 365.25 days      |
| Moons           | 1 (the Moon)     |

Atmosphere is about 78% nitrogen and 21% oxygen. The Moon stabilizes the axial
tilt that gives steady seasons.

@plane z=4 label="Mars"
# Mars

The outermost rocky planet, target of ongoing robotic exploration.

| Property        | Value            |
|-----------------|------------------|
| Distance        | 1.52 AU          |
| Diameter        | 6,779 km         |
| Year length     | 687 Earth days   |
| Moons           | 2 (Phobos, Deimos) |

Home to Olympus Mons, the tallest known volcano, and Valles Marineris, a canyon
system over 4,000 km long. Thin CO2 atmosphere.

@plane z=5 label="Jupiter and the Asteroid Belt"
# Jupiter and the Asteroid Belt

Between Mars and Jupiter lies the asteroid belt, the rocky-to-giant boundary.
Beyond it sits the largest planet.

| Property        | Value            |
|-----------------|------------------|
| Distance        | 5.20 AU          |
| Diameter        | 139,820 km       |
| Year length     | 11.9 Earth years |
| Known moons     | 95+              |

A gas giant of mostly hydrogen and helium. The Great Red Spot is a storm larger
than Earth that has persisted for centuries.

@plane z=8 label="Neptune"
# Neptune

The outermost major planet, an ice giant on the cold frontier.

| Property        | Value            |
|-----------------|------------------|
| Distance        | 30.1 AU          |
| Diameter        | 49,244 km        |
| Year length     | 165 Earth years  |
| Known moons     | 14+              |

The windiest planet, with gusts over 2,000 km/h. Beyond it lies the Kuiper Belt,
home to Pluto and other dwarf planets.

3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: Proof That Root 2 Is Irrational
subject: Mathematics
method: proof by contradiction
attributed: classical Greek mathematics
---
A step by step classical proof that the square root of 2 cannot be written as a
fraction of two integers. The argument is a proof by contradiction. The final
contradiction is reached at [[z=5|the conclusion]].

@plane z=0 label="The Claim and the Strategy"
# Step 0: The Claim and the Strategy

**Claim:** The square root of 2 is irrational. That is, there are no integers
a and b with b not zero such that (a / b) squared equals 2.

**Strategy:** Assume the opposite is true and show it leads to a logical
contradiction. If the assumption forces an impossibility, the assumption must be
false, so the claim holds. This technique is called proof by contradiction.

@plane z=1 label="Assume a Fraction in Lowest Terms"
# Step 1: Assume a Fraction in Lowest Terms

Suppose, for contradiction, that root 2 is rational. Then we can write:

```
sqrt(2) = a / b
```

where a and b are integers, b is not zero, and the fraction is in lowest terms.

**Lowest terms means a and b share no common factor other than 1.** Any fraction
can be reduced to lowest terms, so this assumption costs us nothing. In
particular, a and b are not both even.

@plane z=2 label="Square Both Sides"
# Step 2: Square Both Sides

Square both sides of the equation from [[z=1]]:

```
2 = a^2 / b^2
```

Multiply both sides by b squared:

```
a^2 = 2 * b^2
```

This says a squared is two times an integer, so **a squared is even.**

@plane z=3 label="Deduce That a Is Even"
# Step 3: Deduce That a Is Even

We use this fact: if a squared is even, then a itself is even.

- If a were odd, write a = 2k + 1. Then a squared = 4k^2 + 4k + 1, which is odd.
- So an odd number always squares to an odd number.
- Therefore, since a squared is even, a cannot be odd. **a is even.**

Because a is even, write it as a = 2c for some integer c.

@plane z=4 label="Deduce That b Is Even"
# Step 4: Deduce That b Is Even

Substitute a = 2c into the equation a^2 = 2 b^2 from [[z=2]]:

```
(2c)^2 = 2 * b^2
4 * c^2 = 2 * b^2
2 * c^2 = b^2
```

So b squared is two times an integer, meaning **b squared is even**, and by the
same argument as Step 3, **b is even.**

@plane z=5 label="The Contradiction"
# Step 5: The Contradiction

We have now shown both of these:
- a is even (from [[z=3]])
- b is even (from [[z=4]])

But Step 1 assumed a and b share no common factor. If both are even, they share
the factor 2. **This is a contradiction.**

The contradiction came only from assuming root 2 is rational. Therefore that
assumption is false.

> Conclusion: The square root of 2 is irrational. QED.

---
3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: Acid-Base Titration Procedure
subject: Chemistry
level: introductory lab
safety: goggles and gloves required
---
A standard strong acid, strong base titration to find the concentration of an
unknown hydrochloric acid using standardized sodium hydroxide and a
phenolphthalein indicator. Follow the steps in order. The endpoint reasoning is
explained at [[z=5|the calculation step]].

@plane z=0 label="Safety and Setup"
# Step 0: Safety and Setup

**Before touching any chemical:**
- Put on splash goggles and nitrile gloves.
- Know the location of the eyewash station and acid spill kit.
- Work over a tray to contain spills.

## Materials
- 50 mL burette and clamp on a ring stand
- 250 mL Erlenmeyer flask
- 25 mL volumetric pipette and bulb
- Standardized NaOH, 0.100 mol/L
- Unknown HCl solution
- Phenolphthalein indicator
- Distilled water wash bottle

@plane z=1 label="Prepare the Burette"
# Step 1: Prepare the Burette

1. Rinse the burette twice with a small volume of the NaOH solution, draining
   through the tip each time. This removes leftover water that would dilute it.
2. Fill the burette above the zero mark with NaOH.
3. Open the stopcock briefly to fill the tip and remove any air bubble.
4. Record the starting volume to the nearest 0.05 mL. Read at the bottom of the
   meniscus at eye level.

> Do not assume the start is exactly 0.00 mL. Record the actual reading.

@plane z=2 label="Prepare the Sample"
# Step 2: Prepare the Sample

1. Rinse the pipette with the unknown HCl, then pipette exactly 25.00 mL into
   the clean Erlenmeyer flask.
2. Add about 25 mL distilled water to the flask. This does not change the moles
   of acid, only the volume, and makes the color change easier to see.
3. Add 2 to 3 drops of phenolphthalein. The solution stays colorless in acid.

@plane z=3 label="Titrate"
# Step 3: Titrate to the Endpoint

1. Place the flask under the burette on a white tile or sheet of paper.
2. Add NaOH while swirling the flask continuously.
3. As you near the endpoint, a pink color flashes where drops land and fades.
   Slow to dropwise addition here.
4. The endpoint is the first faint pink that persists for 30 seconds while
   swirling.
5. Record the final burette reading.

The neutralization reaction is:
```
HCl  +  NaOH  -->  NaCl  +  H2O
```

@plane z=4 label="Repeat for Precision"
# Step 4: Repeat for Precision

1. Run the titration at least three times in total.
2. The first run can be a rough trial; add quickly to find the approximate
   endpoint, then go carefully on the later runs.
3. Concordant results are titres agreeing within 0.10 mL of each other.
4. Average only the concordant titres for the calculation.

| Trial | Final (mL) | Start (mL) | Titre (mL) |
|-------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| 1     |           |           |           |
| 2     |           |           |           |
| 3     |           |           |           |

@plane z=5 label="Calculate the Concentration"
# Step 5: Calculate the Concentration

At the endpoint, moles of acid equal moles of base because the reaction is 1 to 1.

1. moles NaOH = concentration x volume = 0.100 mol/L x (average titre in L)
2. moles HCl = moles NaOH
3. concentration HCl = moles HCl / 0.02500 L

## Worked example
If the average titre is 23.40 mL:
- moles NaOH = 0.100 x 0.02340 = 0.002340 mol
- moles HCl = 0.002340 mol
- concentration HCl = 0.002340 / 0.02500 = 0.0936 mol/L

Compare back to [[z=3|the titration step]] if your endpoint color was unclear,
since an overshot endpoint inflates the titre and the calculated concentration.

3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: ETA 2824-2 Movement — Layer by Layer
view: stack
caliber: ETA 2824-2
diameter: 25.60 mm
height: 4.60 mm
jewels: 25
beat: 28,800 vph
power_reserve: 38–42 hours
category: Engineering
---
An automatic movement, peeled apart. From the dial you can almost see through to the mainspring.

@plane z=0 label="Dial Side"

## Dial — The Face You Trust

```
  ┌─────────────────────────────┐
  │  ○  12  ○                   │
  │  ●           ●              │
  │ 9 ●     •     ● 3           │
  │  ●           ●              │
  │  ○   6   ○        [D]       │
  └─────────────────────────────┘
```

- Applied indices: polished brass, 0.35 mm proud of the chapter ring
- Date window at 3 o'clock — cyclops optional on the crystal above
- Seconds hand sweeps at **8 ticks/sec** — that silky ETA glide
- Cannon pinion seats friction-tight on the center wheel pipe

> Dial feet pass through movement at 1:30 and 7:30 — mark them before removing.

@plane z=1 label="Keyless Works"

## Keyless Works — Setting & Winding Interface

| Part | Function | Material |
|---|---|---|
| Crown wheel | Transmits crown rotation inward | Hardened steel |
| Clutch lever | Engages winding vs. setting | Brass + spring |
| Setting lever | Locks hand-setting position | Steel |
| Intermediate setting wheel | Drives minute wheel in set mode | Brass |
| Rocking bar | Detects stem position | Blued steel |

**Stem positions:**
- Position 0 (pushed in) → normal running
- Position 1 (one click) → date quick-set, crown wheel idle
- Position 2 (pulled out) → hands set via minute train

The rocking bar pivot is **0.6 mm** — do not let it fall free during service.

@plane z=2 label="Gear Train"

## Gear Train — The Power Path

```
Mainspring barrel
      │  (first wheel, 80 teeth)
      ▼
  Center wheel ── cannon pinion ──▶ minute hand
  (64 teeth / 8 leaf pinion)
      │
      ▼
  Third wheel (75 teeth / 8 leaf)
      │
      ▼
  Fourth wheel (seconds, 80 teeth / 7 leaf)
      │
      ▼
  Escape wheel (15 teeth / 7 leaf)
```

- Mainspring: **1.25 mm wide, 0.115 mm thick, 385 mm long**
- Center wheel completes one revolution per **60 minutes** (canonical)
- Fourth wheel: one revolution per **60 seconds** — mounts the seconds hand
- Total gear ratio barrel→escape: ~**36,000 : 1**

Power flows right-to-left across the movement bridge. [[z=3|see pallet fork]] for where it stops.

@plane z=3 label="Escapement"

## Escapement — Where Energy Becomes Time

```
      /‾‾‾\          ← entry pallet stone (ruby)
     /       \
----| escape  |----  ← 15 teeth, 1.5° locking angle
     \       /
      \_____/
         |
    ════╪════        ← pallet fork (lever escapement)
         |
     ○───┤            ← impulse pin on balance roller
         |
      balance
      wheel
```

- Pallet stones: **natural ruby**, 0.70 mm × 0.35 mm
- Draw angle: **11°** — prevents accidental unlocking under shock
- Guard pin clears the safety roller notch to within **0.05 mm**
- If the lever doesn't snap crisply between banking pins → worn pallet pivot

The fork converts the gear train's continuous torque into 28,800 discrete impulses per hour.

@plane z=4 label="Balance"

## Balance Wheel — The Heartbeat

**Frequency:** 4 Hz (28,800 A/h) — one tick every **0.178 seconds**

```
        ╔═══╗
       ║     ║      ← rim: glucydur (beryllium-copper)
      ║   ·   ║
      ║   │   ║     ← staff pivot: ø 0.07 mm ← yes, 70 microns
      ║   │   ║
       ║     ║
        ╚═══╝
          │
     ─────┴─────    ← hairspring: flat breguet overcoil
                      material: nivarox 1 alloy
                      wire width: ~0.015 mm
                      turns: 11.5
```

- Timing screws (4 × brass, 4 × steel) adjust **rate and poise**
- Hairspring collet: friction-fitted — never force
- Replacement balance costs more than the rest of the service

**Rule of thumb:** if amplitude drops below 200° the movement will mis-set dates.

@plane z=5 label="Rotor & Bridge"

## Rotor & Reversing Wheels — Automatic Wind

The 2824-2 winds in **both directions** via two reversing wheels under the oscillating weight.

```
  ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
  │   ╭────────────────────────╮     │
  │   │   rotor bearing (ball) │     │
  │   ╰──────────┬─────────────╯     │
  │              │                   │
  │   ┌──────────▼──────────┐        │
  │   │ ratchet wheel 1  →  │ ─────▶ │ ratchet click
  │   │ ratchet wheel 2  →  │ ─────▶ │ (to barrel)
  │   └─────────────────────┘        │
  └──────────────────────────────────┘
```

- Rotor weight: **21-carat gold** in the top spec; tungsten in base editions
- Rotor clamp screw: **0.5 mm slot**, torque to 0.3 N·cm — no more
- Ball bearing cage: replace every 5–7 years regardless of feel
- Reversing click springs: inspect for fatigue cracks at pivot tab

Full wind from flat takes ~650 rotor oscillations on the wrist. [[z=6|power reserve detail]].

@plane z=6 label="Mainspring Barrel"

## Mainspring Barrel — 38 Hours in a Can

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                        │
│    ┌────────────────────────────┐      │
│    │  ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓   │      │
│    │  ▓▓ mainspring coil ▓▓▓   │      │  ← barrel (ø 13.4 mm)
│    │  ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓   │      │
│    └────────────────────────────┘      │
│                 │                      │
│           arbor (center)               │
│                 │                      │
│       barrel click + ratchet           │
│      (prevents mainspring snap-back)   │
└────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Service notes:**
- Clean barrel interior with pegwood — no lint, no scratch
- Lube mainspring: **Moebius 8200** (synthetic, not grease) — 0.5 mg max
- Check for **set** in the spring: if it won't coil flat it's done
- Barrel click spring: replace if tip has any blueing or bend

> "A properly lubed barrel buys you five extra years. A dry one buys you a full overhaul." — anonymous bench veteran

Tension stored here flows through all six layers above.

3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: Major Events in the Evolution of Life
author: CorvidLabs Gallery
span: ~3.8 billion years
---
A walk along deep time. Each plane is one milestone on the Z axis, ordered by
how long ago it happened (z is millions of years before present, so larger z is
further back). Read from the deepest plane forward, or jump straight to
[[z=0.3|the rise of humans]].

@plane z=3800 x=0 y=0 label="First Life (~3.8 Ga)"
# First Life

The oldest chemical and fossil traces of life appear in rocks roughly 3.5 to 3.8
billion years old. The earliest cells were single, membrane-bound prokaryotes
with no nucleus, living in the oceans.

- **Form:** simple anaerobic cells (no oxygen required)
- **Energy:** chemosynthesis near hydrothermal vents and shallow seas
- **Legacy:** the universal genetic code, shared by every organism since

Everything that follows, from a redwood to a blue whale, descends from this
single-celled starting point. The next great leap was learning to eat sunlight:
see [[z=2400|the Great Oxidation]].

@plane z=2400 x=2 y=0 label="Great Oxidation (~2.4 Ga)"
# The Great Oxidation Event

Cyanobacteria evolved oxygen-producing photosynthesis and, over hundreds of
millions of years, flooded the atmosphere with free oxygen. For most existing
microbes this was a poison, triggering one of Earth's earliest mass extinctions.

| Before | After |
| --- | --- |
| Trace atmospheric oxygen | Oxygen-rich atmosphere |
| Anaerobic life dominant | Aerobic respiration possible |

Oxygen was the fuel that later powered large, active animals. The first complex
cells came next: continue to [[z=1800|the eukaryotic cell]].

@plane z=1800 x=4 y=0 label="Eukaryotes (~1.8 Ga)"
# The Eukaryotic Cell

A larger, more complex cell type arose, defined by a nucleus and internal
organelles. The leading explanation is **endosymbiosis**: one cell engulfed
another, and the captive became a permanent partner.

1. An ancestral host cell absorbed an oxygen-using bacterium.
2. The partner became the **mitochondrion**, the cell's power plant.
3. A separate event gave plant ancestors the **chloroplast**.

This partnership is why your cells still carry mitochondrial DNA distinct from
the DNA in your nucleus. With efficient energy in hand, life eventually went
multicellular and big: jump to [[z=540|the Cambrian Explosion]].

@plane z=540 x=6 y=0 label="Cambrian Explosion (~540 Ma)"
# The Cambrian Explosion

In a geologically brief window, most major animal body plans appeared in the
fossil record. Hard shells, jointed limbs, compound eyes, and active predators
all show up together, preserved beautifully in sites like the Burgess Shale.

- First arthropods, mollusks, and early chordates (our distant relatives)
- The arms race between predator and prey drove rapid innovation
- Vision and mineralized skeletons became widespread

Life had been microscopic for over three billion years; in the Cambrian it got
complicated fast. From these chordates came backbones, and eventually limbs for
land: move forward to [[z=375|life moves ashore]].

@plane z=375 x=8 y=0 label="Life on Land (~375 Ma)"
# Vertebrates Move Ashore

Lobe-finned fishes such as *Tiktaalik* show the transition from water to land:
sturdy, weight-bearing fins, a flexible neck, and lungs alongside gills. Their
descendants, the tetrapods, gave rise to amphibians, reptiles, birds, and
mammals.

> Tiktaalik is often called a "fishapod," part fish and part four-legged animal,
> a clear snapshot of an evolutionary transition in progress.

Plants and arthropods had greened the land first, building the food web these
pioneers walked into. The last stop on this timeline is the youngest branch:
arrive at [[z=0.3|the human lineage]].

@plane z=0.3 x=10 y=0 label="Humans (~300 Ka)"
# The Human Lineage

Anatomically modern *Homo sapiens* appears in Africa around 300,000 years ago,
the latest twig on an ancient tree. On the scale of this document, all of
recorded human history is a thin sliver at the very surface.

- **Brain and language:** complex cognition, symbolic thought, culture
- **Tools and fire:** technology that reshaped diet and environment
- **Migration:** out of Africa and across every continent

Stand here at the present and look back: every step, from [[z=3800|the first
cell]] through oxygen, complex cells, and limbs on land, was a prerequisite for
this plane existing at all.

3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Q2 FY2026 Business Review - Northwind Cloud
period: Q2 FY2026 (Apr 1 - Jun 30)
author: Office of the CFO
status: Board draft
---
Read this report at the depth you need. The surface plane (z=0) is the one-paragraph
executive summary. Each deeper plane peels back a layer: the KPI scorecard, the
segment-by-segment breakdown, and finally the line-level financial detail and
assumptions. Drill down by following the cross-plane links.

@plane z=0 label="Executive Summary"
# Executive Summary

Northwind Cloud closed Q2 FY2026 with **$48.2M in revenue**, up 19% year over year and
ahead of our $46.5M plan. Growth was led by the Enterprise segment and a strong rebound
in net revenue retention (now 114%). Operating margin improved 4 points to 12% as we held
headcount flat while revenue grew. The one risk worth the board's attention is rising cloud
infrastructure cost, which grew faster than revenue this quarter.

**Recommendation:** approve the FY2026 reforecast to $198M and fund the two hires in the
Reliability team needed to bend the infrastructure cost curve.

Go deeper: [[z=1|the KPI scorecard]], then [[z=2|segment performance]], then the
[[z=3|financial detail and assumptions]].

@plane z=1 x=0 y=0 label="KPI Scorecard"
# KPI Scorecard

Headline metrics versus plan and versus the prior-year quarter.

| Metric | Q2 FY2026 | Plan | YoY |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Revenue | $48.2M | $46.5M | +19% |
| Net revenue retention | 114% | 110% | +6 pts |
| Gross margin | 71% | 72% | -1 pt |
| Operating margin | 12% | 9% | +4 pts |
| Cash from operations | $7.1M | $5.4M | +31% |
| Logo churn (annualized) | 5.8% | 7.0% | -1.5 pts |

**What moved:** revenue and retention both beat plan, driven by Enterprise expansions.
Gross margin slipped one point because infrastructure cost outpaced revenue (see the
[[z=3|cost detail]]). Operating margin still rose because we delivered the beat with flat
headcount.

For the story behind these numbers, open [[z=2|segment performance]].

@plane z=2 x=0 y=0 label="Segment Performance"
# Segment Performance

Revenue by segment, with the drivers behind each line.

### Enterprise - $26.4M (+28% YoY)

The growth engine this quarter. We signed three seven-figure renewals (Meridian Bank,
Cascade Health, Atlas Logistics), each expanding seat count by 30% or more on multi-year
terms. Net revenue retention in this segment hit 122%. Sales cycle shortened from 91 to
74 days as the new solutions-engineering pod removed late-stage technical blockers.

### Mid-Market - $15.1M (+11% YoY)

Steady. New-logo acquisition was on plan (47 new accounts), but expansion lagged because
the self-serve upgrade flow shipped late. The flow is now live and early data shows a
2.3x lift in in-product upgrades.

### SMB / Self-Serve - $6.7M (-4% YoY)

The soft spot. Logo churn here remains elevated at 11% annualized, concentrated in
accounts that never completed onboarding. The Customer Success team launched a guided
14-day activation program in May; cohorts since then show 38% better 90-day retention,
but the full revenue effect is still a quarter or two out.

Numbers tie back to the [[z=1|scorecard]]. For the full P&L and the assumptions behind
the reforecast, go to [[z=3|financial detail]].

@plane z=3 x=0 y=0 label="Financial Detail and Assumptions"
# Financial Detail and Assumptions

## Condensed P&L (Q2 FY2026, $000s)

| Line | Q2 FY2026 | Q2 FY2025 |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Revenue | 48,200 | 40,500 |
| Cost of revenue | 13,980 | 11,340 |
| **Gross profit** | **34,220** | **29,160** |
| Sales and marketing | 16,100 | 14,800 |
| Research and development | 9,200 | 8,600 |
| General and administrative | 3,140 | 3,010 |
| **Operating income** | **5,780** | **2,750** |

## The infrastructure cost watch item

Cost of revenue grew 23% YoY against 19% revenue growth. The gap is almost entirely
compute and egress in our two largest regions:

- Compute (managed Kubernetes): +31% YoY, driven by Enterprise workloads that run hotter
  than the SMB profile our capacity model assumed.
- Network egress: +44% YoY, concentrated in the new data-export feature shipped in April.

**Mitigation:** the two Reliability hires in the recommendation would own a right-sizing
and commitment-purchase program estimated to recover 1.5 to 2.0 gross-margin points by
Q4. This is the funding ask referenced in the [[z=0|executive summary]].

## FY2026 reforecast assumptions

1. Enterprise net revenue retention holds at 118% to 122% (Q2 actual: 122%).
2. SMB churn improves to 9% annualized as the activation program matures.
3. No price increases modeled; the reforecast is volume-driven.
4. Infrastructure mitigation lands by Q4, recovering ~1.5 margin points.

Under these assumptions the full-year lands at **$198M revenue** and **13% operating
margin**. Back up to the [[z=2|segment view]] or the [[z=1|scorecard]].

3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: Measuring the Vitamin C Content of Orange Juice by Iodometric Titration
discipline: Analytical Chemistry
duration: 90 minutes
hazard-level: moderate
---
This procedure determines the ascorbic acid (vitamin C) concentration of a
juice sample by titrating it against a standardized iodine solution. Ascorbic
acid reduces iodine to iodide; once all the ascorbic acid is consumed, the next
drop of iodine reacts with a starch indicator to produce a blue-black color
that marks the endpoint.

Read every step before starting. Wear splash goggles and nitrile gloves for the
duration. The Z axis is the procedure step, in order.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Safety and Materials"
# Step 0: Safety, Materials, and Reagents

## Hazards
- Iodine solution stains skin and clothing and is an irritant. Avoid contact.
- Glassware is fragile. Inspect the burette for chips before use.
- No open flames are required; this is a room-temperature procedure.

## Equipment
- 50 mL burette with stand and clamp
- 250 mL Erlenmeyer flask (x3, one per trial)
- 25 mL volumetric pipette and pipette filler
- 100 mL beaker, wash bottle of distilled water
- White tile or sheet of paper (to read the color change against)

## Reagents
| Reagent | Concentration | Volume needed |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| Standardized iodine (I2) | 0.005 mol/L | ~120 mL |
| Starch indicator | 1% w/v | a few drops |
| Orange juice sample | as supplied | 75 mL total |

Once everything above is laid out and checked, continue to
[[z=1|preparing the burette]].

@plane z=1 x=2 y=0 label="Prepare the Burette"
# Step 1: Rinse and Fill the Burette

1. Rinse the clean burette with about 5 mL of the standardized iodine
   solution. Tilt and rotate so the liquid coats the inner wall, then drain
   through the stopcock to waste. This removes residual water that would dilute
   the titrant.
2. Close the stopcock. Using a funnel, fill the burette above the zero mark
   with iodine solution.
3. Open the stopcock briefly to fill the tip below the stopcock and expel any
   air bubble. An air bubble that leaves during titration adds false volume.
4. Drain to bring the meniscus to the 0.00 mL line. Read the bottom of the
   meniscus at eye level to avoid parallax error.

Record the exact starting reading; do not assume it is precisely zero. With the
burette ready, move on to [[z=2|preparing the sample]].

@plane z=2 x=4 y=0 label="Prepare the Sample"
# Step 2: Measure the Juice Sample

1. Swirl the juice container gently to suspend any pulp evenly. Do not shake
   hard, which introduces air bubbles and makes pipetting inaccurate.
2. Pipette exactly 25.00 mL of juice into a clean 250 mL Erlenmeyer flask using
   the volumetric pipette and filler. Never pipette by mouth.
3. Add roughly 50 mL of distilled water to the flask. This dilution does not
   change the amount of ascorbic acid present; it only gives a larger, lighter
   volume so the endpoint color is easier to see.
4. Add 5 to 8 drops of 1% starch indicator and swirl to mix.

The flask now contains a known volume of sample ready for titration. Proceed to
[[z=3|the titration]].

@plane z=3 x=6 y=0 label="Titrate to Endpoint"
# Step 3: Titrate Against Iodine

Place the flask on the white tile beneath the burette tip.

1. Add iodine solution from the burette in roughly 1 mL increments at first,
   swirling the flask continuously after each addition. The blue-black color
   that forms on contact should fade as it mixes.
2. As the color begins to fade more slowly, slow down to dropwise addition.
   This is the approach to the endpoint.
3. The endpoint is the first faint blue-black color that persists for at least
   30 seconds of swirling. Stop immediately and close the stopcock.
4. Read and record the final burette volume to the nearest 0.05 mL.

> Tip: if you overshoot (a strong, lasting blue), discard the trial and repeat.
> A perceptibly dark result is past the endpoint and will overstate the titre.

Repeat Steps 1 through 3 with fresh 25.00 mL juice aliquots until you have at
least three concordant trials (titres agreeing within 0.10 mL). Then continue to
[[z=4|the calculation]].

@plane z=4 x=8 y=0 label="Calculate and Report"
# Step 4: Calculate the Vitamin C Concentration

For each trial, the titre is the final reading minus the starting reading.
Average the concordant titres.

## Stoichiometry
Ascorbic acid reacts with iodine in a 1:1 mole ratio:

```
C6H8O6 + I2 -> C6H6O6 + 2 H+ + 2 I-
```

## Worked example
Suppose the mean titre is 18.40 mL of 0.005 mol/L iodine for a 25.00 mL sample.

1. Moles of I2 = 0.01840 L x 0.005 mol/L = 9.20 x 10^-5 mol
2. Moles of ascorbic acid = 9.20 x 10^-5 mol (1:1 ratio)
3. Mass = 9.20 x 10^-5 mol x 176.12 g/mol = 0.01620 g = 16.20 mg
4. Concentration = 16.20 mg / 25.00 mL = 0.648 mg/mL = 64.8 mg per 100 mL

## Reporting
- Quote the result as mg of vitamin C per 100 mL, with the mean and the range
  of the concordant titres.
- Compare against the label value, if printed, and comment on any difference
  (oxidation during storage typically lowers the measured value).

If your trials were not concordant, return to [[z=3|the titration]] and run
additional aliquots before averaging.

3md: 1.0
axis: floor
title: Fallingwater: Floor by Floor
view: elevator
architect: Frank Lloyd Wright
completed: 1939
client: Edgar J. Kaufmann Sr.
site: Bear Run, Mill Run, Pennsylvania
style: Organic Architecture
structure: Reinforced concrete cantilevers over waterfall
category: Information
---
Fallingwater, Bear Run PA — ride the axis from bedrock to roof.
Each floor is a lesson in how Wright stacked compression and release.

@plane z=0 label="Bedrock & Stream"

# z=0 — The Ground Plane

The building doesn't sit *on* the waterfall. It sits *over* it.

Wright anchored the structure to a single sandstone ledge at elevation **+0.0 ft**, letting Bear Run flow uninterrupted beneath. The original boulder that the Kaufmanns used as a diving board still protrudes through the living room floor — Wright refused to blast it.

**Key moves:**
- Stone piers carry all vertical load to bedrock
- Floor slab at stream level is exposed aggregate, continuous with the site
- The sound of the falls audible from every room above
- Approach path from the east kept low and compressed to amplify the reveal

> "The visit to the waterfall in the woods stays with you…"
> — Edgar Kaufmann Jr.

**Structural note:** Three 15-ft cantilevered trays extend west, each balanced by the stone cores behind them. The concrete was under-reinforced at construction; major restoration completed **1997–2002** added post-tensioned rods.

@plane z=1 label="Living Level"

# z=1 — Living Floor (+12 ft)

The **main living room** is the payoff. After a compressed entry stair, the ceiling snaps to **6'4"** above the fireplace and opens to **14'0"** at the glass corners.

**Plan geometry:**
- 2,400 sq ft total floor plate, 1,500 sq ft living area
- Four corner glass panes meet with no mullion at the corner — a 1939 miracle of detailing
- Hatch covers in the floor open to let a ladder down to the stream pool below
- Built-in seating, bookshelves, and a globe bar make the room self-contained

**Materials palette:**
| Surface | Material |
|---|---|
| Floor | Waxed flagstone (local sandstone) |
| Walls | Rough-cut sandstone, coursed to match site strata |
| Ceilings | Ochre-painted concrete soffit |
| Glazing | Original single-pane, Cherokee red steel frames |

[[z=2|Up to the guest terrace →]]

**Circulation:** The stair to the stream descends from the living room hatch — a gesture of total integration between inside and outside.

@plane z=2 label="First Terrace"

# z=2 — Kaufmann Bedroom & Terrace A (+22 ft)

The master suite occupies the southwest corner, private but never separate. The bedroom ceiling drops to **6'8"** — low enough to feel sheltered, high enough to avoid oppression.

**Terrace A** is the first of the three great horizontal planes:
- 850 sq ft of reinforced concrete deck
- No railing on the west edge — just a 2" raised curb
- Oriented toward the afternoon sun and the rhododendron hillside
- The tray reads from below as pure sky-plane, hovering over the living room terrace

**Wright's section logic:**
Each tray shifts slightly east or west of the one below, creating a pinwheel composition. No two floors stack directly — every bedroom looks over the roof of the room below.

```
Plan offset diagram (schematic):
  [TERRACE C]  ←shifted east
     [TERRACE B]  ←shifted west
  [TERRACE A]  ←shifted east
[LIVING ROOM]  ←ground anchor
```

The stone fireplace core, shared with the floor below, is the hinge that makes the pinwheeling possible.

@plane z=3 label="Bridge & Guest Wing"

# z=3 — Covered Bridge & Guest House (+32 ft)

A **136-ft trellis bridge** connects the main house to the guest suite above the hillside. It is simultaneously:

- A covered walkway (roof, no walls)
- A retaining wall for the hillside garden
- A datum line that anchors the horizontal composition from across the run

**Guest suite facts:**
- 600 sq ft, one bedroom, private plunge pool carved into the rock
- Completed **1939**, same year as main house
- Staff quarters below the bridge at grade
- Servant stair concealed in the stone retaining pier

The bridge is where Wright's section becomes undeniable: you walk *through the hill* to reach the house, then step out onto the bridge and the entire composition — three horizontal trays, waterfall, rhododendron, sky — reads as a single simultaneous moment.

> Wright called the horizontal planes "the democracy of the open hand."
> The verticals are the stone — ancient, fixed, local.

[[z=4|Up to the roof level →]]

@plane z=4 label="Second Terrace"

# z=4 — Second Bedroom Level & Terrace B (+32 ft)

Two secondary bedrooms occupy this level, each with direct access to **Terrace B**, the largest of the three decks at **1,100 sq ft**.

**Spatial lessons:**
- Ceiling height drops further to **6'4"** — the compression intensifies as you rise
- Built-in beds, dressers, and window seats eliminate the need for freestanding furniture
- Each window is a framed view: Wright chose tree canopy, never sky

**Terrace B details:**
- The east edge terminates in a curved concrete planter — no right angles at the perimeter
- Cherokee red paint on all steel; the color was mixed to match autumn foliage
- A single curved step transitions between terrace levels — 4" rise, 24" tread

**Acoustic condition:** Terrace B is directly above the waterfall. On wet years, spray reaches the underside of the slab. The roar of Bear Run is loudest here — a deliberate choice. Wright wanted the falls heard, not merely seen.

@plane z=5 label="Roof Garden"

# z=5 — Roof Terrace C & Loggia (+48 ft)

The **topmost plane** is the most compressed interior in the house — a low loggia under a 6'0" soffit — opening to the largest sky-view on the property.

**Terrace C:**
- 600 sq ft, oriented north to the hemlock forest
- A stone-paved outdoor room, not a conventional roof
- The parapet is a planter, full-width, continuous — no gap between building edge and living soil
- Drainage channels cut directly into the concrete, stained red-orange by iron mineral runoff

**The view from here:**
Standing on Terrace C, you can look down through the staggered trays to Bear Run below. All three cantilevers are visible simultaneously. The section — which Wright drew in two hours on a September morning in 1935 — is fully legible for the first and only time.

**Legacy note:** Fallingwater was named the greatest work of American architecture by the AIA in 1991. It receives ~170,000 visitors per year. The structural restoration (post-tensioning, 2002) added an estimated 200 years to its service life.

> "The reality of the building is not the walls and the roof, but the space within."
> — Wright, paraphrasing Lao Tzu

- Worked as a loom mechanic in the Lowell textile mills.
- Married Anna in 1919 at St. Patrick's Church.

Samuel and Anna's children, born between the wars.

- Eldest son of [[z=3|Samuel and Anna]].
- Served in the Pacific, 1942-1945; returned to study civil engineering.
- Married Eleanor Vance in 1948.

## Eleanor Hollis, nee Vance (1926-2011)
- Schoolteacher in Concord for thirty-one years.
- Her diaries fill the gap in records for the 1950s.

## Siblings of Thomas (collateral lines, not expanded here)

- Patrick Hollis (1925-1990), moved to Chicago; descendants out of contact.

Thomas and Eleanor raised two children, listed in [[z=1|Generation II]].

The children of [[z=2|Thomas and Eleanor]]. This is the generation most people

- Brought the Okafor branch (Lagos, Nigeria) into the tree.

- Younger sister of Robert; never married, godmother to all of his children.

Robert and Diane are the parents of the youngest generation in [[z=0|Generation I]].

This plane is updated as new births and marriages occur.

3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Self-Hosting Backups FAQ
subject: backup strategy for a home server
audience: people running a NAS or home server who want their data to survive a disk failure
convention: z=0 is the headline answer; higher z drills deeper into mechanics and edge cases
---
This FAQ is stacked by depth. Plane `z=0` is the one-line answer you can act on
today. Each deeper plane (z=1, z=2, z=3) peels back a layer: the rule of thumb,
then how it actually works, then the failure modes nobody warns you about.

Read straight through, or stop as soon as you have what you need.

@plane z=0 label="Quick answers" x=0 y=0
# Backups, in one breath

**Q: How many copies of my data do I need?**
Three. Keep the **3-2-1 rule**: 3 copies, on 2 different kinds of media, with 1 copy off-site.

**Q: Is RAID a backup?**
No. RAID protects against a *disk* dying, not against you deleting the wrong folder, ransomware, or the whole machine being stolen.

**Q: How often should backups run?**
Nightly for anything you would hate to re-create. Hourly snapshots for active projects.

**Q: How do I know a backup is good?**
Restore from it. A backup you have never restored is a hope, not a backup.

Want the reasoning? Drill into [[z=1|the rules of thumb]].

@plane z=1 label="Rules of thumb" x=2 y=0
# Why three copies, and what counts

The 3-2-1 rule maps to three independent ways your data dies:

- **3 copies** survive a single accident (one corrupt file is still good in two places).
- **2 media types** survive a media-class failure. An SSD and a spinning disk fail for different reasons; two SSDs from the same batch can fail the same week.
- **1 off-site copy** survives fire, flood, theft, and "the power supply took the whole rack with it."

**On RAID specifically:** RAID 1 mirrors and RAID 5/6 add parity, so a drive can fail and you stay online. That is *availability*, not *recoverability*. Every write, including a mistaken `rm -rf`, hits all members instantly. RAID buys uptime; backups buy a time machine.

**On frequency:** match the interval to how much work you can afford to lose (your RPO, recovery point objective). Lose at most an hour of edits? Snapshot hourly. Lose at most a day of family photos? Nightly is plenty.

Curious how the off-site and snapshot pieces actually work? See [[z=2|the mechanics]].

@plane z=2 label="How it works" x=4 y=0
# The mechanics underneath

**Snapshots vs copies.** A filesystem snapshot (ZFS, Btrfs, LVM) is not a second copy of your bytes. It is a frozen view: the filesystem keeps the old blocks alive instead of freeing them when data changes. That makes snapshots near-instant and cheap, but they live on the *same pool*. A snapshot rescues you from a bad edit; it does nothing if the pool itself dies. That is why a snapshot is step one, and `zfs send` (or `restic`/`borg`) to another machine is step two.

**Incremental backups.** Tools like restic and Borg deduplicate at the chunk level. The first run copies everything; later runs copy only changed chunks and record a new "snapshot" that points at the shared chunk store. A nightly job over 2 TB might move only a few hundred MB once the baseline exists.

**Off-site, in practice.** Three common shapes:

1. A second box at a relative's house, fed over a VPN (cheapest long-term).
2. An object-storage bucket (Backblaze B2, S3) with client-side encryption.
3. A rotated external drive you physically carry off-site weekly (low-tech, very robust).

**Verification.** Both Borg and restic have a `check` command that reads the repository and re-hashes chunks. Schedule it. Pair it with an occasional real restore of a random file (see [[z=1|the rules of thumb]] on "restore from it").

The traps live one plane deeper: [[z=3|the failure modes]].

@plane z=3 label="Failure modes" x=6 y=0
# What actually bites people

**1. Silent corruption (bit rot).** A flipped bit on disk can be backed up faithfully for months before you notice. Defense: a checksumming filesystem (ZFS, Btrfs) that scrubs on a schedule and reports errors, plus keeping enough snapshots to reach back *before* the corruption.

**2. Backing up the encryption, not the data.** If ransomware encrypts your files and your nightly job dutifully copies the encrypted versions, your backups are now useless too. Defense: keep a deep retention ladder (for example 7 daily, 4 weekly, 12 monthly) and make the backup target append-only or immutable so an attacker on the source host cannot rewrite history.

**3. Untested restores.** The classic. The backup ran green for two years; the day you need it, the archive is truncated, the passphrase is lost, or the tool version that wrote it no longer exists. Defense: a quarterly fire drill that restores to a scratch directory and diffs against the source.

**4. One credential, one blast radius.** If the source server holds the only key to the off-site bucket *and* has write access to delete old versions, a single compromise can wipe everything. Defense: application keys scoped to write-and-list only, with lifecycle/immutability rules enforced on the bucket side.

**5. The 3-2-1 that is secretly 1-1-1.** Two "copies" on the same pool, plus a sync job that propagates deletions instantly to the "off-site" copy, is one copy wearing a costume. Defense: confirm each copy is independent and that at least one is *not* a live mirror.

Back to the [[z=0|one-breath answers]] when you just need the checklist.

---
3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Fault Tree Cascade - API Latency Incident
view: stack
category: Engineering
---

An incident review as a layered fault tree. Each depth moves from symptom to
mechanism to prevention.

@plane z=0 label="Symptom"
# Symptom

```
checkout-api p95 latency

10:04  220 ms
10:08  410 ms
10:12  960 ms
10:16  1800 ms
10:20  2400 ms
```

Users saw slow checkout confirmation pages. Error rate stayed low, which made
the incident look smaller than it felt.

@plane z=1 label="Immediate Cause"
# Immediate Cause

```
checkout-api
    |
    v
inventory-read
    |
    v
primary postgres

pool wait: 1430 ms
```

Connection pool wait dominated request time. Threads were not blocked; they
were waiting politely in a very long line.

@plane z=2 label="Contributing Factors"
# Contributing Factors

```
               promo traffic +42%
                    |
cache TTL cut 15m -> 2m
                    |
read replica lag 38s
                    |
fallback to primary
```

No single change broke the system. The cascade needed traffic, lower cache
life, and replica lag at the same time.

@plane z=3 label="Control Breaks"
# Control Breaks

```
alerting gap
  p95 page latency: yes
  DB pool wait:     no
  replica lag:      delayed

runbook gap
  safe cache TTL rollback: missing
```

The team knew the page was slow before it knew why. That cost nine minutes.

@plane z=4 label="Fix Stack"
# Fix Stack

```
1. Add pool-wait SLO
2. Pin promo TTL floor at 10m
3. Fail closed on replica lag over 10s
4. Add one-command TTL rollback
5. Load test promo profile weekly
```

The fix is a stack, too: detect earlier, prevent the risky combination, and
make rollback boring.

3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: Fireworks Burst
fps: 24
loop: false
palette: gold-on-navy
---
A single aerial shell, drawn frame by frame. Read the planes in `z` order to
watch one chrysanthemum break: the shell climbs, the charge ignites, a sphere of
gold stars expands, gravity bends the trails into a willow, and the embers fade
to smoke. Each plane is one keyframe; the `x`/`y` offsets track the burst center
as it drifts on the wind.

@plane z=0 label="Launch" x=0 y=-40
# Frame 0 - Launch

The mortar fires. A single comet of packed black powder leaves the tube and
rises on a thin orange tail. Nothing has broken yet.

- **Altitude:** climbing, roughly 1/3 of apex
- **Visible:** one bright point, a faint smoke trail below it
- **Sound:** the dull *thump* of the lift charge
- **Color:** warm orange, almost white at the head

The fuse is timed to reach the burst charge near the top of the arc. Hold this
point of light steady; everything after this is the break.

Next: the shell coasts to its peak in [[z=1|the apex]].

@plane z=1 label="Apex" x=2 y=10
# Frame 1 - Apex

The comet slows at the top of its climb. For a fraction of a second it hangs,
nearly motionless, the tail thinning to almost nothing. Wind has nudged the
center about two units to the right.

This is the quiet beat before the burst. The timing fuse has just reached the
internal charge.

- **Velocity:** near zero (the still moment)
- **Visible:** a single dim ember, no trail
- **Tension:** maximum, the audience holds its breath

> A good shell breaks at apex, where it is slowest, so the sphere stays round
> instead of smearing into a teardrop.

Jump back to [[z=0|the launch]], or forward to [[z=2|ignition]].

@plane z=2 label="Ignition" x=2 y=12
# Frame 2 - Ignition

The burst charge detonates. The shell's casing splits and the packed stars are
thrown outward in every direction at once. This is the brightest single frame of
the whole sequence.

- **Flash:** a hard white core, blown-out for one frame
- **Stars:** dozens of points launching radially outward
- **Radius:** small but growing fast
- **Sound:** arrives late, the *crack* the crowd feels in the chest

Render this frame with a short, intense highlight. The next frame is all about
the symmetric spread.

On to [[z=3|the full bloom]].

@plane z=3 label="Bloom" x=3 y=12
# Frame 3 - Full Bloom

The chrysanthemum opens. Every star traces a straight radial line from the
center, forming a near-perfect sphere of gold. This is the iconic shape, the
frame you would put on a poster.

| Property   | Value                        |
|------------|------------------------------|
| Shape      | sphere (chrysanthemum break) |
| Star count | ~64 visible trails           |
| Color      | saturated gold, even falloff |
| Radius     | maximum extent               |

Each trail is the same length, which is what makes the burst read as round. The
core has already gone dark; only the expanding shell of stars remains.

From here gravity takes over. See [[z=4|the willow droop]].

@plane z=4 label="Willow" x=4 y=4
# Frame 4 - Willow Droop

Expansion stops. The stars have spent their outward energy, and now gravity
curves every trail downward. The crisp sphere softens into the drooping
"willow" form, golden strands raining toward the ground.

- **Motion:** outward speed gone, downward speed building
- **Shape:** sphere relaxing into a hanging dome
- **Color:** gold cooling toward amber at the trail tips
- **Trails:** longer now, with visible fade from head to tail

This is the lingering, graceful part of the effect, the part that earns the
*ooh* a beat after the *crack*.

Compare with [[z=3|the bloom]], or watch it die in [[z=5|the fade]].

@plane z=5 label="Fade" x=5 y=-6
# Frame 5 - Fade to Smoke

The stars burn out one by one. The bright amber tips wink off, leaving only
faint glowing embers and a drifting cloud of smoke lit from below by the next
shell on the ground.

- **Brightness:** near zero, a few stray sparks
- **Visible:** gray-blue smoke, a couple of dying embers
- **Color:** desaturated, mostly the navy of the night sky again
- **Sound:** silence, then the lift *thump* of the next shell

The frame returns almost to where it began: a dark sky with one small point of
light. If `loop` were true, this is where it would cut back to
[[z=0|the launch]] and start over.

*End of burst.*

---
3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: Adult CPR First Aid
audience: bystander responder, no equipment
standard: 2020 AHA / ERC guidelines (compression rate 100-120/min)
disclaimer: Reference only. Take a hands-on certified course.
---
Hands-only and standard CPR for an unresponsive adult who is not breathing
normally. Move through the planes in order. If you are alone with a phone,
put it on speaker so the dispatcher can coach you while your hands stay free.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Check the scene and the person"
# Step 0: Make sure it is safe, then check responsiveness

Before you touch anyone, look for danger to yourself: traffic, fire, live
wires, water, fumes. You cannot help if you become the second casualty.

Once the scene is safe:

1. Tap the person firmly on both shoulders.
2. Shout, "Are you okay? Can you hear me?"
3. Look at the chest for 5 to 10 seconds. Are they breathing normally?

A single gasp every few seconds is **agonal breathing**. It is not normal
breathing, and it means you should treat the person as not breathing.

- Responsive and breathing: do not start CPR. Keep them comfortable and
  monitor.
- No response and no normal breathing: continue to [[z=1|call for help]].

@plane z=1 x=2 y=0 label="Call 911 and get an AED"
# Step 1: Call emergency services and send for an AED

Time is the enemy. Brain cells start dying within minutes once blood flow
stops, so the call and the AED request happen right now, not later.

- **If others are present:** point at one specific person and say, "You, in
  the red jacket, call 911. You, find an AED and bring it here." Naming
  people prevents the bystander freeze where everyone assumes someone else
  will act.
- **If you are alone:** call 911 yourself, put the phone on speaker, and
  start compressions while you talk. Do not leave an adult to search for an
  AED unless the dispatcher tells you one is very close.

Tell the dispatcher: the exact location, that the person is unresponsive and
not breathing, and that you are starting CPR. Then go straight to
[[z=2|chest compressions]].

@plane z=2 x=4 y=0 label="Chest compressions"
# Step 2: Push hard and fast in the center of the chest

This is the single most important step. Good compressions keep oxygenated
blood moving to the brain and heart.

**Hand placement**

1. Kneel beside the person on a firm, flat surface.
2. Put the heel of one hand on the center of the chest, on the lower half of
   the breastbone (between the nipples).
3. Place your other hand on top and interlace your fingers. Keep fingers off
   the ribs.

**Technique**

- **Depth:** at least 2 inches (5 cm), no more than 2.4 inches (6 cm).
- **Rate:** 100 to 120 compressions per minute. The beat of "Stayin' Alive"
  or "Baby Shark" lands in this range.
- **Recoil:** let the chest rise fully between pushes. Do not lean on the
  chest, or you starve the heart of refill time.
- **Lock your elbows** and drive from your hips and shoulders, not your arms.

Cracking sounds can happen and are not a reason to stop. Minimize pauses:
every break in compressions drops the blood pressure you just built up.

Trained and willing? Add [[z=3|rescue breaths]]. Otherwise keep doing
hands-only compressions without stopping.

@plane z=3 x=6 y=0 label="Rescue breaths (optional)"
# Step 3: Two rescue breaths after every 30 compressions

Rescue breaths are optional. If you are untrained, unwilling, or there is no
barrier device, skip this plane and keep doing continuous compressions from
[[z=2|step 2]]. Hands-only CPR saves lives.

If you choose to give breaths, use a **30:2** cycle:

1. After 30 compressions, open the airway with a head-tilt, chin-lift: one
   hand on the forehead, two fingers lifting the bony part of the chin.
2. Pinch the nose shut, make a seal over the mouth (ideally through a
   pocket mask or face shield), and give 1 breath over about 1 second.
3. Watch for the chest to rise. Give a second breath, then return
   immediately to compressions.

Keep each breath gentle. Two breaths should take less than 10 seconds total
so compressions barely pause. If the first breath does not make the chest
rise, reposition the head and try once more, then continue regardless.

@plane z=4 x=8 y=0 label="Use the AED"
# Step 4: Apply the AED the moment it arrives

An AED (automated external defibrillator) can restart a chaotic heart
rhythm. Modern units talk you through every step out loud.

1. Turn it on and follow the voice prompts.
2. Expose and dry the bare chest. Peel the pads and place them as shown on
   the pad pictures: one upper right, one lower left side.
3. **Stand clear** while it analyzes. Make sure no one is touching the
   person.
4. If a shock is advised, shout "Clear!", confirm no contact, and press the
   flashing shock button.
5. **Immediately resume** compressions from [[z=2|step 2]] for 2 minutes,
   then let the AED reanalyze.

Do not remove the pads. Leave the AED on and attached.

@plane z=5 x=10 y=0 label="Keep going until handoff"
# Step 5: Continue until help takes over

Do not stop. Survival depends on continuous effort until one of these
happens:

- The person starts breathing normally, moves, or wakes up. If they recover
  but stay unconscious, roll them into the **recovery position** (on their
  side, head tilted to keep the airway open) and monitor breathing.
- Trained responders (EMS, paramedics) arrive and take over.
- An AED instructs you to stand clear for analysis or a shock.
- You are too exhausted to continue. **Swap rescuers every 2 minutes** to
  keep compressions strong; trade off during the AED rhythm checks so you
  lose almost no time.

If the person is still down, you go back to [[z=2|compressions]] and repeat
the cycle. The worst CPR is no CPR, so keep your hands on the chest.

A two-bedroom cottage on a compact lot. Each plane below is a horizontal slice
of the building, stacked along the Z (space) axis from the ground up: the
foundation at the bottom, then the main living floor, then the upper floor, and
finally the roof. Dimensions are in feet. Use the cross-plane links to follow
the stair and the plumbing stack between levels.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Foundation & Crawlspace"
# Level 0 - Foundation (slab + crawlspace)

A poured concrete perimeter footing carries the load of both floors. The
crawlspace under the kitchen and bath gives access to the plumbing trunk.

| Feature | Size | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Footing | 16in wide | Below the 36in frost line |
| Crawlspace | 12ft x 14ft | 30in clearance, gravel bed |
| Sump pit | 24in dia. | NW corner, near the downspout |
| Utility pad | 4ft x 4ft | Water heater and electrical panel |

The vertical plumbing stack rises from the crawlspace through the kitchen wall
and continues all the way to the roof vent. Trace it up to the
[[z=1|main floor kitchen]] and on to the [[z=3|roof vent boot]].

Access hatch is in the hallway closet on the [[z=1|main floor]].

@plane z=1 x=0 y=0 label="Main Floor (Ground Level)"
# Level 1 - Main Floor

Total 720 sq ft. Open-plan living and kitchen face south for light; the entry,
bath, and stair are tucked along the north wall.

```
  N
  +----------------+----------+
  |  Kitchen 10x12 | Bath 5x8 |
  |                +----+-----+
  |                | Stair UP |
  +-------+--------+----------+
  | Entry |  Living Room      |
  | 6x6   |  14x16            |
  +-------+-------------------+
              S (porch)
```

- **Entry**: 6 x 6, coat closet plus the crawlspace access hatch.
- **Living room**: 14 x 16, south windows, wood stove on the east wall.
- **Kitchen**: 10 x 12, galley layout; the sink and range share the wet wall
  above the [[z=0|plumbing stack]].
- **Half bath**: 5 x 8, toilet and vanity.
- **Stair**: 3ft run against the north wall, climbing to the
  [[z=2|two bedrooms above]].

Ceiling height is 9ft here, 1ft taller than upstairs.

@plane z=2 x=0 y=0 label="Upper Floor (Bedrooms)"
# Level 2 - Upper Floor

Total 640 sq ft under a sloped ceiling. The stair from the
[[z=1|main floor]] lands in a small landing that doubles as a reading nook.

| Room | Size | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Primary bedroom | 12 x 14 | South dormer, walk-in closet 4x6 |
| Second bedroom | 10 x 11 | North window, built-in desk |
| Full bath | 8 x 8 | Stacked over the kitchen wet wall |
| Landing nook | 5 x 6 | Bookshelf, skylight above |

The full bath sits directly above the kitchen so both share the single
[[z=0|plumbing stack]]. The skylight over the landing is framed into the
[[z=3|roof]] between the two dormers.

Knee walls (4ft tall) run along the east and west eaves, giving low storage.

@plane z=3 x=0 y=0 label="Roof"
# Level 3 - Roof

A 9/12 gable roof with two front dormers over the
[[z=2|primary bedroom and landing]].

- **Pitch**: 9/12 gable, ridge running east to west.
- **Dormers**: two shed dormers on the south face, each 4ft wide.
- **Skylight**: 2ft x 4ft over the landing nook below.
- **Vent boot**: the plumbing stack from the [[z=0|foundation]] terminates
  here, 12in above the shingles, on the north slope.
- **Overhang**: 18in eaves all around, shading the south windows in summer.
- **Material**: architectural asphalt shingles, charcoal; copper drip edge.

Gutters drain to the NW downspout, which feeds the
[[z=0|sump and gravel bed]] below.

3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: Forcing the Seven
view: present
author: A close-up magician
deck: standard Bicycle Rider Back, red
category: Entertainment
---
A card trick in six moves. Your only job is to follow along — *do not skip ahead.*

@plane z=0 label="The Setup"
# Before We Begin

Take a standard deck. Any deck you own.

Give it a **genuine shuffle** — as many times as you like.

---

Now hold it face-down in your left hand.

I need you to do one thing:

> *Think of a number between 1 and 10. Don't say it out loud.*

Got it? Good. Keep it close.

---

*Tap the deck once on the table — just a habit of mine.*

[[z=1|Turn the page when you're ready.]]

@plane z=1 label="The Count"
# Count Down to Your Number

From the top of the deck, deal cards **face-down** into a pile on the table, one at a time, counting silently.

Stop when you reach your number.

---

**Look at that card. Remember it.**

Don't show me. Don't say it. Just hold it in your mind like you're keeping a secret from someone you like.

---

Place it back on top of your pile. Drop the rest of the deck on top of that.

The card is now **somewhere in the middle.** Completely lost.

*I know. Looks hopeless.*

[[z=2|Keep going.]]

@plane z=2 label="The Cut"
# The Hindu Shuffle

Hold the deck face-down. With your right hand, pinch **a third of the deck** from the middle and let the bottom portion fall into your left palm.

Repeat — pinch another clump, let it waterfall down.

Keep going until the deck is gone.

---

This is a Hindu shuffle. You've probably seen it at a card table.

It looks like chaos. It's *almost* chaos.

---

Do it **three times total.** Count them — one, two, three.

Set the deck down.

*Take a breath. You've earned it.*

[[z=3|One more move.]]

@plane z=3 label="The Spell"
# Spell It Out

Here's something strange I want you to try.

Think of your card — the one you peeked at. **Say its full name out loud**, letter by letter, dealing one card face-down per letter.

---

*Example: if your card were the Jack of Clubs, you'd deal:*
`J-A-C-K-O-F-C-L-U-B-S` → 11 cards

---

Your card has a name. Trust it.

Spell it now. Deal the cards face-down as you go.

When you reach the **last letter**, stop.

Do not flip anything yet.

---

*The last card you dealt — hold it face-down between two fingers.*

[[z=4|Almost there.]]

@plane z=4 label="The Misdirection"
# Wait — Did I Forget Something?

Before you flip that card, I owe you an apology.

---

I asked you to think of a number between 1 and 10.

Then I had you shuffle. Then spell your card's name.

By now you've **completely forgotten** what your number was.

---

That was the trick.

Not the card — the forgetting.

You were thinking so hard about your card, your name, your spelling, that the number dissolved.

---

*Seven.*

Your number was seven.

---

[[z=5|Now flip the card.]]

@plane z=5 label="The Reveal"
# Flip It.

---

**Seven of Diamonds.**

---

*There it is.*

---

The deck was never lost. The shuffle was a rhythm, not a randomiser. The spell was a count in disguise.

And while you were busy trusting the letters — I already knew.

---

> "The secret is never the method. It's the moment you stopped watching."

---

*Thank you for playing.*  
Deal the deck again whenever you want to feel this again from the other side.

3md: 1.0
axis: pitch
title: Freerider — El Capitan Free Topo
view: elevator
route: Freerider (VI 5.13a or 5.12d A0)
wall: El Capitan, Yosemite Valley
total_pitches: 33
crux: Enduro Corner (5.13a) or boulder problem to Teflon Corner (5.12d)
rack: double rack to #4, triple #0.5–#2, offset nuts, 2x big bros, 70m ropes
descent: Walk off via East Ledges (rappel 3x, 2hr)
first_free_ascent: Lynn Hill 1993 (complete), Caldwell & Jorgeson 2015 (continuous)
category: Maps
---
33 pitches from Yosemite Valley floor to the summit cap. Each plane is a wall section. Rack hard, rest hard, send it.

@plane z=0 label="Base → Mammoth Terrace (P1–P7)"
# Lower Slabs: Pitches 1–7

**Approach:** Park at El Cap Meadow, hike the base trail 20 min to the East Buttress. Fixed line often in place on P1.

| Pitch | Grade | Length | Character |
|-------|-------|--------|-----------|
| P1 | 5.8 | 35m | Right-facing corner, polished — move fast, gear is spaced |
| P2 | 5.10b | 40m | Slabby face left of the arch, three bolts then cams |
| P3 | 5.11a | 45m | The Freeblast slab — granite friction, trust your feet |
| P4 | 5.11a | 45m | Continue Freeblast, trend left to bolt ladder |
| P5 | 5.10c | 30m | Squeeze chimney, thrutch to good ledge |
| P6 | 5.10d | 40m | Face climbing, thin gear, committing moves at top |
| P7 | 5.10a | 25m | Short traverse right to **Mammoth Terrace** |

**Beta:** P3–P4 (Freeblast) is the crux of the lower wall. Slab technique over power. Bring extra socks — your shoes will be soaked with sweat before Heart Ledges.

**Bivy:** Mammoth Terrace is a good ledge camp for a 2-day approach schedule. Water cache possible here.

@plane z=1 label="Heart Ledges → El Cap Tower (P8–P15)"
# Middle Wall: Pitches 8–15

The route narrows into sustained crack systems. This is where Freerider diverges from the Nose.

| Pitch | Grade | Length | Character |
|-------|-------|--------|-----------|
| P8 | 5.11d | 50m | Hollow flake, rattly pro — place gear confidently |
| P9 | 5.12a | 45m | **Teflon Corner approach** — slick face moves, small wires |
| P10 | 5.11c | 40m | Hand cracks, steep — your best rest pitch in days |
| P11 | 5.12a | 35m | Finger to hand crack up a dark streak |
| P12 | 5.12b | 40m | Long pitch, sustained 5.11 with 5.12 crux at two-thirds height |
| P13 | 5.11d | 30m | Chimney section, wide — break out the #4s |
| P14 | 5.10c | 45m | Wandering face, runout — **look for the knee bar rest** at the small roof |
| P15 | 5.10a | 20m | Short romp to **El Cap Tower** bivy ledge |

**El Cap Tower bivy:** 2m × 4m ledge, good hooks. Most free teams sleep here night 1 or 2. Haul bag anchor bolts are solid.

> "The middle wall is where you find out if your crack technique is real." — Tommy Caldwell

**Rack note:** Triple your 0.75 and 1-inch pieces here. P11–P12 will eat them.

@plane z=2 label="The Boulder Problem (P16–P18)"
# Crux Section: The Boulder Problem

The make-or-break pitches for a free ascent. Most parties yard here (A0) on the first attempt.

---

## Pitch 16 — Teflon Corner (5.12d)
**35m.** Start from El Cap Tower, undercling right, then power up the polished dihedral. Two distinct crux moves:

1. **The pull-in** — undercling to sidepull, high step with left foot, gastons to shoulder height
2. **The top slap** — from a marginal rest, dynamic move to a sloper, match, lock off for the rest jug

**Foot beta:** The Teflon Corner lives up to its name. Smear with maximum rubber contact. Thin-soled shoes (La Sportiva Solution or Scarpa Drago) outperform stiff models here.

---

## Pitch 17 — Boulder Problem (5.13a, or skip via A0)
**15m.** The single hardest move on the route. A V7 boulder problem on a vertical face with no crack.

- **Free:** Sidepull left hand, crimp right, high left foot on a dimple, rock over, throw right to the sloper rail. **One move. Make it.**
- **A0:** Pull the single bolt. No shame. 99% of parties do.

---

## Pitch 18 — Recovery Traverse
**5.11c, 40m.** After the crux, a long traverse left that feels 5.8 on adrenaline. Clip 6 bolts, hand crack finish. Reach **Round Table Ledge** — the best bivy on the wall.

**Round Table Ledge:** Flat, wide, sheltered from afternoon sun. Sleep well. The summit is yours if you made P17 free.

@plane z=3 label="Round Table → Alcove (P19–P25)"
# Upper Wall: Pitches 19–25

Above Round Table the angle lays back slightly. Sustained 5.11–5.12 crack climbing for 250m.

| Pitch | Grade | Length | Character |
|-------|-------|--------|-----------|
| P19 | 5.11b | 50m | Long hand crack, best crack pitch on the route |
| P20 | 5.12a | 45m | Finger crack in a corner, lock-lock-lock |
| P21 | 5.11c | 40m | Undercling flake system, trend right |
| P22 | 5.12b | 35m | **Enduro Corner entry** — begins the long dihedral |
| P23 | 5.13a | 45m | **Enduro Corner** — the real crux for most free parties |
| P24 | 5.11d | 30m | Continue dihedral, sustained, pumpy |
| P25 | 5.10d | 40m | Walk/chimney to **The Alcove** |

## Enduro Corner (P23) — Full Beta
45m of 5.12+–5.13a sustained finger crack in a perfect dihedral. No rest. No shaking out.

**Sequence:**
- First 15m: 5.11 finger crack, find a rhythm
- 15–30m: crux zone, fist-size crack narrows to fingers, back-and-foot stems are your friend
- 30–45m: the corner widens to hands, you're pumped but the holds are better
- **Clip the anchor before you barn-door off the final move**

**Rack:** Leave your #3–4 at the anchor. This pitch wants fingers-to-hands. Double 0.5, 0.75, 1.

@plane z=4 label="Alcove → Pushme-Pullyou (P26–P30)"
# High Wall: Pitches 26–30

You're at ~2700ft. The valley floor is a rumor. Three to five pitches to the summit headwall.

| Pitch | Grade | Length | Character |
|-------|-------|--------|-----------|
| P26 | 5.11a | 45m | Chimney, wide — stuff your body in and breathe |
| P27 | 5.11b | 40m | Face moves on big features, 4 bolts |
| P28 | 5.12a | 35m | **Changing Corners** — route-finding crux, three options |
| P29 | 5.12b | 40m | Long layback flake, 30m of sustained 5.11+ |
| P30 | 5.11c | 25m | Short steep crack to the **Pushme-Pullyou** ledge |

## Changing Corners (P28)
Three lines at the roof band. In order of difficulty:

1. **Left variation** (5.12b): Roof crack, powerful but protectable
2. **Direct** (5.12a): Face traverse to corner above — the standard
3. **Right variation** (5.11d, A0): One bolt pull over the roof — fast option in deteriorating weather

> Read the weather before P28. If clouds are building over Half Dome, take the right variation and move.

**Water note:** Pushme-Pullyou often has seeping water in spring. Fill up. Summit push from here is 2–4 hours.

@plane z=5 label="Summit Headwall → Top (P31–P33)"
# Summit Push: Pitches 31–33

The granite lightens in color here, the angle steepens one last time, and the wind picks up. You're close.

---

## Pitch 31 — Thank God Ledge Bypass (5.11a)
**30m.** The original Nose route traverses the famous Thank God Ledge (walk sideways 20m, trad). Freerider climbs above it. Steep face moves on jugs — surprising after days of cracks. Feels harder than 5.11a at this altitude and fatigue level.

---

## Pitch 32 — The Final Headwall (5.12c)
**40m.** The last hard pitch. Thin crack in a blank orange headwall, 8 bolts supplementing marginal gear. Three crux sequences, the hardest at bolt 5.

**Mental note:** You will feel the wall trying to push you off. It isn't. You're just tired. Breathe. The moves go.

---

## Pitch 33 — Summit Slabs (5.7)
**50m.** Friction slabs to the summit. Laughably easy. Run it out. The valley is 3000ft below you.

---

## Summit
Flat granite, wind, and the best view in North America. Sign the summit register in the metal box (pencil inside). Take 20 minutes before you think about the descent.

**Descent:** East Ledges trail, 3 rappels (70m ropes needed for first rap), 2hr to valley floor. Follow cairns **carefully** — parties get benighted here every season.

**Total free ascent time (fast party):** 3–5 days. Record: 7hr 40min (Honnold & Caldwell, 2018).

[[z=0|Back to the base]] · [[z=2|Review the crux]]

**Avg rent/sf:** $3.31 — slightly below Uptown Oakland median ($3.58)

> Unit 4D vacancy: landlord holding for family member (declared 11/2025). Ellis Act clock **not** triggered. Monitor per Oakland Just Cause Eviction Ordinance §8.22.360.

@plane z=5 label="Roof"
## Roof — Underutilized Infrastructure & Potential

**Roof area:** 4,600 sf | **Structural load:** 40 psf dead + 30 psf live (adequate for rooftop ADU with engineering review)

**Existing:**

---
3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: Assembling the BJORKLUND 3-Drawer Dresser
product: BJORKLUND
sku: 204.118.77
est_time: 35-45 minutes
people_required: 2 (recommended for step 5)
---
Read every step before you start. Lay out all parts on a clean, carpeted
floor or a blanket so panel faces do not get scratched. Tools you supply:
one Phillips screwdriver and a rubber mallet. Everything else is in the bag.

Each plane below is one assembly step on the Z axis. Move forward only when
the current step is fully seated and square.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Step 0 - Inventory and tools"
# Step 0: Check the bag before you build

Open the hardware bag over a bowl so nothing rolls away. Confirm you have
every fastener before driving a single screw, because the count is exact
with no spares.

| Part | Code | Qty |
| --- | --- | ---: |
| Cam lock (silver disc) | 100347 | 12 |
| Cam bolt (double-ended) | 101350 | 12 |
| Wood dowel (38 mm) | 101339 | 16 |
| Drawer screw (black, 25 mm) | 118331 | 24 |
| Back-panel nail | 100001 | 20 |
| Plastic glide foot | 109526 | 4 |

Panels are labelled A through H on a sticker on the inside face. Keep that
face pointing up so you never read it upside down.

- A, B: left and right side panels
- C: top panel
- D: bottom panel
- E: back panel (thin hardboard)
- F, G, H: drawer fronts

If anything is missing, stop here and do not proceed to
[[z=1|the frame assembly]] with an incomplete kit.

@plane z=1 x=0 y=2 label="Step 1 - Build the carcass frame"
# Step 1: Join the sides to the top and bottom

Work on the floor with the back of the unit facing you. This is the box
that everything else hangs off, so take the time to get it square.

1. Tap one wood dowel (101339) into each of the two top holes on side
   panel A. They should sit halfway, by hand, no mallet yet.
2. Press cam bolts (101350) into the threaded holes on the top panel C,
   turning clockwise two full turns with the screwdriver.
3. Stand panel A and lower top panel C onto it so the cam bolts drop into
   panel A. Insert a cam lock (100347) into each round face hole and turn
   it clockwise to about 90 degrees. You will feel it grab.
4. Repeat for side panel B and the bottom panel D.

> Square check: measure both diagonals of the open box. If they match
> within 3 mm, the carcass is square. If not, push the long corner inward
> before the cam locks are fully tight.

Hold off on the back panel. That waits until after
[[z=2|the drawer runners go on]], because you need access from behind.

@plane z=2 x=2 y=2 label="Step 2 - Mount the drawer runners"
# Step 2: Fit the three pairs of runners

The runners decide whether the drawers glide or stick, so alignment here
matters more than torque. Each side panel has three rows of pilot holes.

- Orient every runner with the roller wheel toward the FRONT of the
  carcass and the screw flange toward the back.
- Drive two drawer screws (118331) per runner, snug but not stripped.
- Left and right runners in a pair must sit at the same height. Eyeball
  across the box at each row before moving down.

Row map, top to bottom:

| Row | Holds | Roller faces |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Top | Drawer F | Front |
| Middle | Drawer G | Front |
| Bottom | Drawer H | Front |

That uses 12 of the 24 drawer screws. The other 12 attach the drawer
fronts back in [[z=4|the drawer build]].

@plane z=3 x=2 y=0 label="Step 3 - Close the back and stand it up"
# Step 3: Nail on the back, then tip upright

With the runners mounted, you can finally close the box.

1. Lay back panel E into the rabbet groove around the rear edge. The
   smooth (printed) side faces OUT, into the room.
2. Starting at one corner, drive back-panel nails (100001) every 15 cm or
   so. The nails are short, so angle the hammer flat and tap lightly.
3. The back panel is what keeps the unit square under load. Do not skip
   nails to save time.
4. With a helper, tip the dresser upright onto its base.
5. Thread the four plastic glide feet (109526) into the bottom corners and
   adjust them so the unit does not rock on your floor.

Give the carcass a gentle shove from the top corner. If it racks side to
side, a back-panel nail is missing or a cam lock from
[[z=1|Step 1]] needs another quarter turn.

@plane z=4 x=4 y=0 label="Step 4 - Build the three drawers"
# Step 4: Assemble each drawer box

All three drawers build the same way, so set up a small production line and
do the same operation on all three before moving on.

For each drawer:

1. Cam-lock the two drawer sides to the drawer back, same disc-and-bolt
   method from [[z=1|the carcass step]].
2. Slide the thin drawer bottom into its groove. Do not nail or glue it;
   it floats so the box can flex.
3. Attach the drawer front (F, G, or H) from the inside using four drawer
   screws (118331) through the pre-drilled front rail. Leave them slightly
   loose for now.
4. With the front held square, hand-tighten the four screws in a cross
   pattern.

Match each front to its row: F is the shallowest front and goes on top,
H is the deepest and goes on the bottom.

@plane z=5 x=4 y=2 label="Step 5 - Fit drawers and final tune"
# Step 5: Hang the drawers and align the gaps

This is the two-person step. One person steadies the carcass while the
other seats each drawer.

1. Tilt a drawer up slightly, set its runners onto the carcass rollers,
   and push until it clicks past the stop. Pull it out once to confirm it
   glides freely.
2. Load top to bottom: F, then G, then H.
3. Look at the three gaps between the fronts. They should be even, around
   3 mm each. If a front sits crooked, loosen its four screws from
   [[z=4|Step 4]], nudge it level, and retighten.

Final safety:

- Use the supplied wall anchor and strap to fix the dresser to a stud.
  A loaded dresser can tip, and this single strap is the difference.
- Go back and give every cam lock a final snug, working from
  [[z=1|the frame]] outward.

Done. Wipe the panels with a dry cloth and recycle the flat-pack carton.
