3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: Homicide on Lake Meridian — Case 2024-0447
view: present
case: 2024-0447
detective: Sgt. M. Vasiliev, King County ME
victim: John Aldred Moss, 54, retired civil engineer
scene: Lake Meridian Park, Auburn WA — Pier 3 access road
date-found: 2024-09-14 06:22 PDT
status: closed — conviction 2025-03-11
category: Information
---
Reconstruct the timeline. Each step reveals the next layer of evidence.
Tap through in order — this is how the case broke open.

@plane z=0 label="Scene Discovery"
# 06:22 — Body Found

A jogger, **Darla Fitch, 31**, calls 911 from the Lake Meridian boat launch.

> "He's just lying there at the end of the gravel road. I thought he was sleeping."

**Initial observations (first officer on scene):**
- Male, prone, face-down, no visible trauma
- Fully dressed: khaki work pants, fleece pullover
- Hands underneath torso
- No wallet, no phone, no keys

**Weather that morning:** 11°C, heavy dew, no rain since 09-12

One anomaly noted immediately: **the dew pattern on the victim's back is uniform** — no disturbance, no scuff marks in gravel within 2 meters.

→ He was placed here. Not where he died. [[z=1|See lividity findings]]

@plane z=1 label="Lividity & PMI"
# 08:47 — ME On Scene

**Dr. Claire Touma** rolls the body at 08:47. The finding is immediate.

**Lividity:** Fixed, posterior — meaning blood pooled in the *back* of the body within 6–8 hours of death.

But Moss was found **face-down**.

| Finding | Implication |
|---|---|
| Fixed posterior lividity | Died lying on his back |
| Found face-down | Moved after lividity fixed |
| PMI estimate | Death between **09-13 21:00 – 23:00** |
| Core temp at scene | 28.4°C → ~9 hrs since death |

**Petechial hemorrhaging** in the conjunctiva. Sub-dermal bruising at the neck — two parallel lines, 3.1 cm apart.

Preliminary CoD: **ligature asphyxiation**.

The killer had 7+ hours to move the body before discovery. [[z=2|Follow the phone data]]

@plane z=2 label="Digital Trail"
# 11:30 — Phone Carrier Subpoena

Moss's iPhone 14 (recovered from Duwamish River, 09-15) had been powered off — but **carrier tower pings** don't lie.

**Last 12 hours of tower hits:**

```
09-13 18:04  Tower 7A  — Home (46th Ave SE, Auburn)
09-13 19:47  Tower 7A  — Home
09-13 21:12  Tower 12C — Lake Meridian area ← arrives at scene
09-13 21:19  Tower 12C — [LAST PING — phone powered off]
09-14 03:44  Tower 8F  — 167th Ave SE, 2.1 mi north ← phantom ping
```

The 03:44 ping isn't Moss's phone. It's **the suspect's phone pinging the same tower** while transporting the body — logged because Moss's carrier used the same tower ID in their CDR export.

Cross-referencing that tower hit against all devices: **37 phones active**. Narrow by cell plan, prior criminal, known associate of victim → [[z=3|One name surfaces]]

@plane z=3 label="The Associate"
# 14:15 — Victor Greiss, 51

**Victor Greiss** — Moss's former business partner. Civil dispute over a land surveying contract, **$214,000 judgment** against Greiss filed 09-01-2024.

- His T-Mobile number in the 03:44 tower hit batch: ✓
- Address: 9 minutes from dump site: ✓
- 2012 domestic violence conviction (strangulation): ✓

**Search warrant executed 09-15, 16:00:**

- Garage: **Honda Ridgeline, 2021** — rear cargo liner recently bleached
- Luminol test on liner: **positive, two zones**, consistent with body drag
- On workbench: **paracord spool**, cut end, diameter 3.1 mm
- In trash (not taken out yet): **disposable nitrile gloves**, size L

Greiss's alibi: "I was home all night, ask my wife."

Wife's interview: [[z=4|She hesitated exactly once]]

@plane z=4 label="Alibi Fracture"
# 09-16, 10:05 — Interview: Sandra Greiss

Det. Vasiliev notes the hesitation on the question: *"Did Victor leave the house at all on the night of the 13th?"*

> **Sandra:** "No. He — no, he was home. We watched a movie."
> **Vasiliev:** "What movie?"
> **Sandra:** *(3.2 second pause)* "I don't remember the title. Something on Netflix."

Netflix account subpoena (09-17):

| Date | Title | Start | End | Device |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 09-13 | *The Gentlemen* Ep 4 | 20:11 | 21:03 | Samsung TV |
| 09-13 | *The Gentlemen* Ep 4 | 21:03 | 21:03 | Samsung TV — **paused, never resumed** |

Victor left the house at **~21:05**. Sandra knew. Her attorney contacted the DA at 09-19.

Plea deal: immunity for full cooperation. [[z=5|The cord matches]]

@plane z=5 label="Physical Match"
# 09-21 — Trace & Toolmark Lab

**Paracord from workbench spool** vs. **ligature furrow dimensions from autopsy:**

- Diameter: **3.1 mm** — consistent ✓
- Nylon braid pattern (8-strand diamond): lifted from furrow via SEM imaging — **matches spool batch** ✓
- Partial fingerprint on spool core: **Greiss, right index, 12-point match** ✓

**Cargo liner DNA:**
- Zone 1: Moss (victim) — 99.97% profile match
- Zone 2: Greiss — 94.3% profile match (partial, degraded by bleach)

**Touch DNA on Moss's collar:**
- Greiss epithelials, inside right collar fold

At this point, Det. Vasiliev's case summary reads:

> *"Physical, digital, and testimonial evidence converge. Request DA review for first-degree murder warrant."*

Warrant issued 09-22. Greiss arrested 09-22 at 07:48. [[z=6|Verdict]]

@plane z=6 label="Verdict"
# 2025-03-11 — King County Superior Court

**Jury deliberation: 4 hours 17 minutes.**

**Verdict: Guilty — Murder in the First Degree**

**Sentence: 26 years, Washington State Corrections**

---

**What broke the case, in order:**

1. - [x] Dew pattern → body was moved
2. - [x] Lividity → died on his back, found face-down
3. - [x] Tower ping at 03:44 → suspect's phone placed at scene
4. - [x] Paracord diameter → physical link
5. - [x] Netflix subpoena → alibi collapsed in 3.2 seconds
6. - [x] Sandra's testimony → placed Greiss out of house at time of death

> "Forensics doesn't catch killers. It catches liars."
> — Sgt. Vasiliev, post-trial press statement

3md: 1.0
axis: floor
title: Mercy General Hospital - Floor Guide
building: Tower A (Main Pavilion)
elevators: Bank 1 (west lobby), Bank 2 (east garage link)
emergency: Dial 4444 from any house phone for a Code Blue
updated: 2026-06-23
---
Welcome to Tower A. Each plane below is one floor, stacked from the ground-level
Lobby (z=0) up to the Surgical floor (z=4). Use the west elevator bank for
visitor floors and the east bank for the parking garage link. Wayfinding tiles
in the floor follow the same color you see on each floor's header below.

@plane z=0 label="Ground - Lobby & Emergency" x=0 y=0
# Ground Floor - Lobby & Emergency (Blue Zone)

You are here. Information desk is straight ahead past the revolving doors.

## Key destinations
- **Emergency Department (ED)** - follow the red line on the floor, south corridor
- **Admitting / Registration** - desks 1 through 6, immediately right of the lobby
- **Gift shop & cafe** - open 6:00 AM to 9:00 PM, left of the fountain
- **Pharmacy (outpatient pickup)** - window 3, behind the information desk
- **Chapel & quiet room** - north alcove, open 24 hours

## Getting around
- Visitor elevators: take **Bank 1 (west)** for floors 1-4
- Need surgery check-in? Go up to [[z=4|Surgical Services on Floor 4]]
- Garage link and valet: head to the east doors near Admitting

> After 9:00 PM all visitors enter through the **Emergency entrance** and sign in
> at the security desk. Day passes are not valid overnight.

@plane z=1 label="Floor 1 - Imaging & Labs" x=0 y=0
# Floor 1 - Imaging & Diagnostics (Green Zone)

Outpatient testing lives here. Check in at the green kiosk when you arrive.

## Departments
- **Radiology** - X-ray, CT, ultrasound (rooms 110-118)
- **MRI suite** - room 120, ring the bell; remove all metal before entering
- **Phlebotomy / Blood draw** - rooms 101-104, walk-ins 7:00 AM to 5:00 PM
- **Cardiac testing** - EKG and stress lab, room 130

## Before your scan
- Fasting bloodwork: nothing but water for 8 hours prior
- Contrast CT: arrive 30 minutes early to drink the prep
- Bring your photo ID and the order from your referring physician

Heading to inpatient care afterward? The wards begin on
[[z=2|Floor 2 - Medical & Surgical Units]]. Pre-op patients should continue to
[[z=4|Floor 4 - Surgical Services]].

@plane z=2 label="Floor 2 - Inpatient Wards" x=0 y=0
# Floor 2 - Medical & Surgical Units (Amber Zone)

General inpatient rooms. Quiet hours are 10:00 PM to 6:00 AM.

## Unit map
- **2 West (rooms 201-224)** - General medicine
- **2 East (rooms 225-248)** - Post-surgical recovery
- **Nurses' station** - center hub, staffed 24/7, call button reaches here

## Visitor notes
- Two visitors per patient at a time
- Wash or sanitize hands at every doorway dispenser
- Flowers are welcome; latex balloons are not (allergy safety)
- Meal service runs 7:30 AM, 12:00 PM, and 5:30 PM

For higher-acuity patients, the intensive care unit is one floor up on
[[z=3|Floor 3 - Critical Care]]. Discharge paperwork is processed back down at
[[z=0|Ground Floor Admitting]].

@plane z=3 label="Floor 3 - Critical Care" x=0 y=0
# Floor 3 - Critical Care & Maternity (Purple Zone)

Restricted access. Press the intercom at the double doors and wait to be buzzed in.

## Units
- **Intensive Care Unit (ICU)** - 12 beds, rooms 301-312
- **Cardiac Care Unit (CCU)** - rooms 313-320
- **Labor & Delivery / Maternity** - secured wing, rooms 330-345
- **Neonatal step-down nursery** - room 348

## Access rules
- ICU and CCU: immediate family only, please check with the charge nurse
- Maternity badge required; partners receive a wristband at check-in
- No children under 12 in the ICU corridors
- Phones on silent, calls taken in the family lounge near room 305

Need a procedure or operation? The operating rooms are directly above on
[[z=4|Floor 4 - Surgical Services]]. Imaging orders are filled down on
[[z=1|Floor 1 - Imaging & Diagnostics]].

@plane z=4 label="Floor 4 - Surgical Services" x=0 y=0
# Floor 4 - Surgical Services (Red Zone)

The top floor of Tower A. Surgical check-in, operating rooms, and recovery.

## Areas
- **Pre-op / Surgical check-in** - desk 4A, arrive 2 hours before your scheduled time
- **Operating rooms** - OR 1 through OR 8 (staff only past the red doors)
- **Post-Anesthesia Care Unit (PACU)** - recovery bays, family is paged here
- **Surgical waiting lounge** - room 410, live status board updates by case number

## On the day of surgery
- Nothing to eat or drink after midnight unless your surgeon says otherwise
- Leave jewelry, contacts, and valuables at home
- A family member should stay reachable in the [[z=4|surgical waiting lounge]]
- After recovery, most patients move to [[z=2|Floor 2 - Post-surgical Recovery]]

> Surgeons update families through the status board and in person after each case.
> If you step away, leave a phone number with the lounge volunteer.

A guide to amenities at The Larkspur, sliced floor by floor along the Z (floor)
axis. The lobby sits at ground level (z=0), guest floors stack above it, and the
rooftop bar caps the building. Each slice lists what you will find on that level,
how to reach it, and where to go next. Use the cross-plane links to ride the
elevator between floors without hunting through a binder.

Two elevator banks serve the building: the main glass cabs run from the
[[z=0|lobby]] to the [[z=8|rooftop]], and a single service elevator (key-card
only) reaches the [[z=-1|parking and back-of-house]] level.

@plane z=-1 x=0 y=0 label="Parking & Back-of-House (P1)"
# Level P1 - Parking and Back-of-House

One level below the street. Self-parking and valet drop, plus the staff areas
guests rarely see but often need.

| Amenity | Location | Hours |
|---|---|---|
| Self-parking | Bays 1-60, ramp off Cedar St | 24 hours |
| Valet stand | By the elevator landing | 6:00 AM - 1:00 AM |
| EV charging | Bays 55-60, Level 2 chargers | 24 hours |
| Luggage storage | Bell closet near valet | Staffed 7:00 AM - 11:00 PM |
| Laundry & housekeeping | Locked corridor, staff only | - |

- **Parking rates**: $28 self-park, $42 valet, both per night with in/out
  privileges. Charge it to your room at the [[z=0|front desk]].
- **EV charging**: free for hotel guests; ask the valet to plug in if the bay
  is taken.
- **Accessibility**: two van-accessible bays by the elevator; the cab reaches
  every guest floor up to the [[z=8|rooftop bar]].

Take the elevator up one level to reach the [[z=0|lobby and check-in]].

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Lobby & Restaurant (Ground)"
# Level 0 - Lobby, Front Desk, and Restaurant

Street level. This is where you arrive, check in, eat, and get help at any hour.

```
  Cedar St entrance
  +---------------------------+
  | Front Desk | Concierge    |
  +------------+--------------+
  | Lobby Lounge (fireplace)  |
  +------------+--------------+
  | Cafe Marlo | Elevators    |
  +------------+--------------+
       Garden patio (rear)
```

- **Front desk & concierge**: staffed 24 hours, dial ext. 0. Theater tickets,
  dinner reservations, and luggage runs to [[z=-1|the bell closet]].
- **Cafe Marlo**: breakfast 6:30 - 10:30 AM, dinner 5:30 - 10:00 PM. Coffee and
  pastries all day in the lobby lounge.
- **Lobby lounge**: gas fireplace, free seating, fast Wi-Fi (network "Larkspur
  Guest", password on your key sleeve).
- **Garden patio**: heated in cool months, open until 10:00 PM.

For a workout or a swim, ride up to the [[z=3|fitness and pool floor]]. Meeting
planners should continue to the [[z=2|conference level]].

@plane z=1 x=0 y=0 label="Standard Guest Rooms (Floors 1-2)"
# Level 1 - Standard Guest Rooms

Floors 1 and 2 hold the standard king and double-queen rooms. Quiet, simple, and
close to the elevators.

| In-room amenity | Detail |
|---|---|
| Bed | King or two queens, premium linens |
| Coffee | Pour-over kit and electric kettle |
| Minibar | Stocked; honor pricing on the card by the tray |
| Wi-Fi | Free, same "Larkspur Guest" network as the [[z=0|lobby]] |
| Climate | In-room thermostat, blackout curtains |

- **Ice and vending**: alcove by the service elevator, each floor.
- **Housekeeping**: daily by 2:00 PM; hang the card or dial ext. 0.
- **Connecting rooms**: rooms 112/114 and 210/212 connect for families.

Need more space or a soaking tub? The [[z=4|suites]] are four floors up. For a
workout first, stop at the [[z=3|pool and fitness floor]].

@plane z=2 x=0 y=0 label="Conference & Events (Floor 3)"
# Level 2 - Conference and Event Space

A full floor of flexible meeting space, named for local rivers.

| Room | Capacity (theater) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Willamette Ballroom | 200 | Divisible into three sections |
| Rogue Room | 40 | Built-in 4K display, video conferencing |
| Deschutes Room | 24 | Boardroom table, natural light |
| Pre-function foyer | - | Coffee service, registration desks |

- **A/V**: house sound, wireless mics, and a dedicated tech on call; arrange
  through the [[z=0|concierge]].
- **Catering**: menus from Cafe Marlo on the [[z=0|ground floor]]; plated or
  buffet, dietary options on request.
- **Business center**: printing and a private phone booth off the foyer, open
  24 hours with a room key.

After the meeting, unwind at the [[z=8|rooftop bar]] or take a lap in the
[[z=3|pool]].

@plane z=3 x=0 y=0 label="Pool, Spa & Fitness (Floor 4)"
# Level 3 - Pool, Spa, and Fitness

The wellness floor, with windows on the city.

- **Lap pool**: heated saltwater, 4 lanes, open 6:00 AM - 10:00 PM. Towels at
  the deck stand.
- **Hot tub**: poolside, seats eight, same hours as the pool.
- **Fitness center**: 24 hours with a room key; Peloton bikes, free weights to
  50 lb, two treadmills, and a stretching area.
- **Spa Larkspur**: massage and facials, 9:00 AM - 8:00 PM by appointment;
  book through the [[z=0|front desk]].
- **Sauna and steam**: in each locker room, off by 10:00 PM.

| Service | Hours | How to book |
|---|---|---|
| Lap pool & hot tub | 6:00 AM - 10:00 PM | Walk-in, room key |
| Fitness center | 24 hours | Walk-in, room key |
| Spa treatments | 9:00 AM - 8:00 PM | Ext. 0 or the spa desk |

Guests in the [[z=4|suites]] get robes and slippers delivered; everyone else can
borrow a robe at the towel stand. Finish the day with a drink on the
[[z=8|rooftop]].

@plane z=4 x=0 y=0 label="Suites & Club Lounge (Floors 5-7)"
# Level 4 - Suites and Club Lounge

The top guest floors, floors 5 through 7, hold the corner suites and the members'
Club Lounge.

| Suite type | Size | Highlights |
|---|---|---|
| Junior Suite | 480 sq ft | Sitting area, rain shower |
| Corner Suite | 720 sq ft | Soaking tub, city-and-river view |
| Larkspur Suite | 1,050 sq ft | Two rooms, dining table for six |

- **Club Lounge** (Floor 7): continental breakfast 6:30 - 10:00 AM, evening
  hors d'oeuvres 5:00 - 7:00 PM, all-day coffee and soft drinks. Suite and Club
  guests only; ask the [[z=0|front desk]] to activate your key.
- **Turndown service**: nightly, with sparkling water and a forecast card.
- **Pillow menu**: six options; dial ext. 0 by 8:00 PM.
- **Robes and slippers**: provided, plus priority booking at the
  [[z=3|spa]].

Suites include two passes to the [[z=8|rooftop bar]] each night, no reservation
needed. Standard rooms are down on [[z=1|floors 1 and 2]].

@plane z=8 x=0 y=0 label="Rooftop Bar & Terrace (Floor 8)"
# Level 8 - Rooftop Bar and Terrace

The top of the building: a glass-walled bar that opens onto an open-air terrace.

- **Aerie Bar**: cocktails, local wine and beer, small plates from Cafe Marlo's
  kitchen. Open 4:00 PM - midnight (until 1:00 AM Fri/Sat).
- **Terrace**: fire pits, heaters, and lounge seating; last call follows the
  bar.
- **Sunset hour**: half-price oysters and a featured spritz, 4:00 - 6:00 PM
  daily.
- **Live music**: acoustic sets Thursday through Saturday, 8:00 PM.

| Detail | Note |
|---|---|
| Dress code | Smart casual after 6:00 PM |
| Reservations | Walk-in for hotel guests; others via [[z=0|concierge]] |
| Age policy | 21+ after 9:00 PM |
| Weather | Terrace closes in high wind; bar stays open |

Suite guests, your two nightly passes come from the [[z=4|Club Lounge]]. When you
are done, the glass elevator drops straight back to the [[z=0|lobby]] or down to
[[z=-1|parking]].

3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: API Gateway Outage - On-Call Runbook
category: Engineering
schema: runbook/1
entrypoint: "0"
resolved: "7"
service: api-gateway
severity: sev2
view: single
---
One file, two readers. A human on-call follows the prose and clicks the branch
that matches reality. A program parses the same planes as a decision graph and
can dry-run, lint for dead ends, or drive an auto-remediation. Each [[z=N|...]]
link is a branch; step 0 is the entrypoint, step 7 is resolution.

@plane z=0 label="DETECT" kind=entry signal="5xx_rate>5%"
# Detect

PagerDuty fired: `5xx rate > 5% for 3m` on `api-gateway`. Open the dashboard and
confirm it is real, not one bad host or a deploy still rolling.

- error rate truly elevated -> [[z=1|TRIAGE]]
- single host or already recovering -> [[z=7|RESOLVED]]

@plane z=1 label="TRIAGE" kind=decision
# Triage

Find the blast radius. `kubectl get pods -n prod | grep -v Running` and pull the
last deploy time. Decide the most likely cause.

- a deploy shipped in the last 30m -> [[z=3|ROLLBACK]]
- DB latency or connection errors in the logs -> [[z=2|DB_CHECK]]
- cause unclear after 10m -> [[z=6|ESCALATE]]

@plane z=2 label="DB_CHECK" kind=decision tool="psql"
# Database check

Check the primary: `SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity;`, replication lag,
slow-query log.

- DB is saturated or lagging -> [[z=4|MITIGATE_DB]]
- DB looks healthy -> [[z=3|ROLLBACK]]

@plane z=3 label="ROLLBACK" kind=action cmd="kubectl rollout undo"
# Roll back the last deploy

`kubectl rollout undo deploy/api-gateway -n prod`. Watch the error rate for 5m.

- errors dropping -> [[z=5|VERIFY]]
- no improvement -> [[z=6|ESCALATE]]

@plane z=4 label="MITIGATE_DB" kind=action
# Mitigate the database

Kill long-running queries, fail over to the replica if the primary is wedged,
then widen the connection pool.

- recovered -> [[z=5|VERIFY]]
- still degraded -> [[z=6|ESCALATE]]

@plane z=5 label="VERIFY" kind=decision
# Verify recovery

Error rate under 1% for 5m, latency normal, the request queue drained.

- all green -> [[z=7|RESOLVED]]
- still red -> [[z=6|ESCALATE]]

@plane z=6 label="ESCALATE" kind=action
# Escalate

Page the incident commander and the service owner. Post the timeline in
`#incident`. Take the directed action, then re-verify.

- mitigation applied -> [[z=5|VERIFY]]

@plane z=7 label="RESOLVED" kind=terminal accept=true
# Resolved

Metrics stable. Close the incident, snapshot the timeline, schedule the
postmortem. The on-call's job is done.

3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: Installing Sparrow CLI
product: Sparrow CLI
version: 2.4.0
platforms: macOS, Linux, Windows
---
This guide walks through installing the Sparrow CLI from a clean machine to a
verified, working setup. Each plane is one step on the `step` axis. Follow them
in order; if a step fails, the troubleshooting links point you to the right
place to recover. Estimated time: about ten minutes.

@plane z=0 label="Check prerequisites"
# Step 0: Check prerequisites

Before you install anything, confirm your machine meets the minimum bar.

- **Operating system:** macOS 12+, a glibc-based Linux (Ubuntu 20.04+, Fedora 36+), or Windows 10 21H2+.
- **Architecture:** x86_64 or arm64. Run `uname -m` (macOS/Linux) to check.
- **Disk:** at least 250 MB free for the binary and its cache.
- **Network:** outbound HTTPS to `dl.sparrowcli.dev` on port 443.

Verify your shell knows where user binaries live:

```bash
echo "$PATH" | tr ':' '\n' | grep -q "$HOME/.local/bin" && echo "ok" || echo "missing"
```

If it prints `missing`, you will add that directory in [[z=1|Step 1]] before
installing. Once the checks above pass, continue to [[z=1|the installer]].

@plane z=1 x=0 y=1 label="Run the installer"
# Step 1: Run the installer

The fastest path is the official install script, which detects your OS and
architecture and drops the binary into `~/.local/bin`.

```bash
curl -fsSL https://dl.sparrowcli.dev/install.sh | sh
```

Prefer a package manager? Pick your platform:

- **macOS (Homebrew):** `brew install sparrow-cli`
- **Debian/Ubuntu:** `sudo apt-get install ./sparrow_2.4.0_amd64.deb`
- **Windows (winget):** `winget install Sparrow.CLI`

If `~/.local/bin` was reported as missing back in [[z=0|the prerequisites]],
add it to your shell profile now:

```bash
echo 'export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.profile
```

Open a new terminal so the updated `PATH` takes effect, then move on to
[[z=2|configuring authentication]].

@plane z=2 x=0 y=2 label="Configure credentials"
# Step 2: Configure credentials

Sparrow talks to your account over an API token. Generate one in the web
dashboard under **Settings > Access Tokens**, then store it locally:

```bash
sparrow auth login --token sk_live_xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
```

This writes a config file to `~/.config/sparrow/config.toml` with permissions
`0600` (readable only by you). To confirm the token was saved:

```bash
sparrow auth whoami
```

You should see your account email and the active workspace. If you manage
several environments, set a default workspace so later commands do not need a
flag:

```bash
sparrow config set workspace prod-eu
```

With credentials in place, run the [[z=3|verification check]] to make sure
everything works end to end.

@plane z=3 x=0 y=3 label="Verify the install"
# Step 3: Verify the install

Confirm the binary is on your path and reports the expected version:

```bash
sparrow --version
# sparrow 2.4.0 (build 2026-05-18, arm64-apple-darwin)
```

Run the built-in self-check, which pings the API, validates your token, and
exercises the local cache:

```bash
sparrow doctor
```

A healthy run ends with `All checks passed`. The most common warnings are:

| Symptom | Likely cause | Fix |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `command not found` | `PATH` not updated | revisit [[z=1|Step 1]] |
| `401 unauthorized` | bad or expired token | redo [[z=2|Step 2]] |
| `timeout reaching api` | firewall blocks 443 | recheck [[z=0|prerequisites]] |

Once `sparrow doctor` is clean, you are ready for [[z=4|your first command]].

@plane z=4 x=0 y=4 label="First run and next steps"
# Step 4: First run and next steps

Kick the tires with a harmless read-only command:

```bash
sparrow projects list
```

You should see a table of projects in your default workspace. From here:

- **Create your first project:** `sparrow projects create demo`
- **Enable shell completion:** `sparrow completion zsh > ~/.sparrow-completion.zsh`
- **Read the handbook:** `sparrow help` or visit the docs site.

To upgrade later, rerun the installer from [[z=1|Step 1]] or use your package
manager; Sparrow keeps your config in `~/.config/sparrow`, so credentials from
[[z=2|Step 2]] survive upgrades. To remove it entirely, run `sparrow uninstall`
and delete the config directory.

That is the full path from a bare machine to a verified, working Sparrow CLI.
Welcome aboard.

axis: step
title: Welwyn North Interlocking — Down Fast to Platform 2
view: present
signalbox: Welwyn North SB (NX panel, 1959)
route: 1D77 King's Cross → Cambridge, Down Fast → Platform 2
timetable: 14:23 pass, 0 min late
operator: Leif H., Monday turn
category: Engineering
---
You are setting road for 1D77 through Welwyn North. Seven interlocking steps, one keystroke at a time. Each plane is a discrete state the frame must pass through before the next can be entered.

@plane z=0 label="Idle — Line Clear"

## 🟩 All Normal, Line Clear

The panel is quiet. Berth track circuits show white. Signal WN15 (Down Fast home) stands at **Danger — Red**.

```
 WN13  WN15        WN17
  🟥    🟥          🟥
   |     |           |
===⬛====⬛===[23]===⬛===
         DF          P2
         ^
      1D77 approaching (1.2 miles)
```

- All points: normal (N)
- Track circuits: clear
- Lock: no routes set

> Bell received from Hitchin: **2-1** (Express Passenger approaching). Acknowledged with **2-1**.

---
**Next action:** Set points 23A/23B to Platform 2 road. [[z=1|Step 1 →]]

@plane z=1 label="Points Normal → Reverse"

## ⚙️ Points 23A and 23B — Calling Over

Operate point switches **23A** (trailing) then **23B** (facing) on the NX panel.

| Point | Normal position | Required | Movement |
|-------|----------------|----------|----------|
| 23A   | Down Fast through | Reverse | Swing trailing end toward P2 |
| 23B   | Down Fast through | Reverse | Swing facing end toward P2 |

```
Before:          After:
 DF ──────────    DF ──────┐
         P2 │              P2
            │
```

- Points motor runs: **4.2 seconds**
- Detection lights: red (moving) → **white (detected, reversed)**
- Facing point lock energised: **FPL 23B confirmed**

> **Warning:** If 23B detection fails, route cannot be set. Check point heaters — last frost 06:14 this morning.

[[z=0|← Back]] · [[z=2|Step 2 →]]

@plane z=2 label="Overlap Check"

## 🔒 Overlap — TC24 Must Be Clear

The interlocking will not release the proceed aspect until the **overlap track circuit TC24** (80 metres beyond WN17, the starting signal) is proved clear.

```
WN17 (starting)
  |
  |——— 80m overlap ———|
  |                   |
 [23]               [TC24]
                       ^
               Must show WHITE (clear)
               before WN15 can clear
```

- TC24 status: **CLEAR** ✓
- Overlap route locked in rear of WN17
- Conflicting route via WN19 (Up Slow cross-over): **locked out** automatically

> The 14:19 Up Slow (2C44) cleared TC24 at 14:21:08. Two minutes and counting — plenty of room.

[[z=1|← Back]] · [[z=3|Step 3 →]]

@plane z=3 label="Route Set — Signals Clear"

## 🟢 Route Proved, Signals Clear

Interlocking logic satisfied. Press **WN15 signal button** — route lights up on the panel, signal clears.

```
 WN13  WN15        WN17
  🟥    🟢          🟡
   |     |           |
===⬛====⬛===[23R]==⬛===
         DF          P2
                ^
           route lit (yellow)
```

- WN15: **Proceed — Double Yellow** (two-aspect clear)
- WN17: **Caution — Single Yellow** (platform starter, held at danger until 1D77 booked stop)
- Points 23A/23B: **locked reversed** for duration of route
- Route indicator at WN15: **"2"** (feather, lit)

> The driver will see double yellow at WN15 and ease off for the platform starter.

[[z=2|← Back]] · [[z=4|Step 4 →]]

@plane z=4 label="Train Entering Section"

## 🚆 1D77 Fouling the Berth

14:23:41 — 1D77 hits **TC20** (approach berth). Signal description populates automatically on the panel.

```
 TC20  TC21   TC22  [23R] TC23
  🟫    ⬛      ⬛           ⬛
         |
      1D77 here
      Class 387, 8-car, 163m
      Driver: reported on time
```

- TC20: **occupied** (red)
- WN15 clears to single yellow as train passes (track-circuit replacement)
- Route remains locked — **cannot be cancelled** while train is on route

> Standard. You watch the berths walk the train down the panel. Satisfying, like a bead on a wire.

[[z=3|← Back]] · [[z=5|Step 5 →]]

@plane z=5 label="Platform Occupied"

## 🛑 Platform 2 — Train Standing

14:24:09 — 1D77 comes to a stand. TC23 occupied, TC22 occupied. WN17 holds at **Danger**.

```
         [23R]  TC23    P2 platform
                 🟫🟫🟫🟫🟫🟫🟫🟫
                 |← 163m Class 387 →|

WN17: 🟥  (platform starter — DANGER)
```

- Route automatically **replaced** once all berths behind WN17 re-clear
- Points 23A/23B: still locked reversed
- Station call: **1 min 30 sec** scheduled

> Station supervisor presses the "right away" plunger at 14:25:38. One pip on the bell, driver acknowledges two toots.

[[z=4|← Back]] · [[z=6|Step 6 →]]

@plane z=6 label="Departure — Route Released"

## 🟢 Right Away — WN17 Clears

14:25:40 — "Right Away" received. Clear WN17 (Down Platform 2 starter) for onward section to Knebworth.

```
WN17 (starter, P2)
  🟢  PROCEED
   |
   |——— TC24 overlap still held ———|
   |
  TC24: CLEAR ✓
  Section to Knebworth: LINE CLEAR (received 14:24:55)
```

- WN17: **Proceed — Green** (line clear to Knebworth home)
- Train departs 14:25:42 (**2 minutes late** — boarding overrun)
- As 1D77 clears TC23: points 23A/23B **release**, motor swings back to **Normal**
- Route extinguishes on panel — back to [[z=0|idle state]]

> Log entry: `14:23 1D77 arr P2, 14:25 dep, 2L. Pts 23 op x2. No irregularities. WNS.`

The panel goes quiet again. Next working: 14:31, the Up Fast empty stock from Cambridge.

3md: 1.0
axis: slide
title: Building for the Edge - Conference Keynote Outline
event: DevSummit 2026
speaker: Mara Voss, VP Platform Engineering
duration: 40 minutes plus 10 for Q and A
---
Speaker outline for the closing keynote. Each plane is one slide. The `z` value
is the slide number, and the small `x`/`y` offsets group the three-slide demo arc
(slides 3, 4, 5) slightly to the right so a spatial viewer can see them as a set.
Read top to bottom, or jump straight to [[z=5|the live demo]].

@plane z=1 x=0 y=0 label="Title and Hook"
# Building for the Edge

**Why your next bottleneck is 3,000 miles away**

Mara Voss, VP Platform Engineering

Opening hook (90 seconds, no slides advance):

> "Last month a single config change cut our checkout latency by 280ms. We did
> not buy faster servers. We moved the work closer to the people doing it."

- Land the promise: by the end, you will know when edge compute pays off and when
  it quietly burns money.
- Set the frame: three numbers - latency, cost, and operational blast radius.
- Promise a live demo at the end (see [[z=5|the demo]]); ask folks to hold
  questions until then.

@plane z=2 x=0 y=0 label="The Problem"
# The distance tax

Every request to a central region pays for the speed of light. You cannot
optimize your way out of physics.

- A round trip from Sydney to us-east-1 is ~200ms before your code runs a line.
- Users feel anything above 100ms; above 300ms they start abandoning carts.
- We measured it: 41% of our p99 latency was pure network transit, not compute.

Three forces pushing work outward:

1. **Regulation** - data residency rules now cover 70+ jurisdictions.
2. **Cost** - egress bandwidth is the line item nobody forecasts correctly.
3. **Experience** - real-time features (typing indicators, live cursors) die at
   distance.

This is the gap the rest of the talk closes. Next we look at [[z=3|the shift]]
that makes edge practical today.

@plane z=3 x=2 y=0 label="The Shift / Demo Setup"
# What changed: the platform caught up

Edge used to mean a CDN that could only cache static files. That is no longer
true.

- Compute runtimes now boot in under 5ms at hundreds of locations.
- State is the hard part, and it finally has answers: regional read replicas,
  CRDT-backed stores, and durable per-object coordinators.
- The mental model shifts from "one big region" to "many small ones that agree
  eventually, and sometimes immediately."

**Demo setup (what you are about to see):**

- A toy order service deployed two ways - one central, one edge-first.
- Same code, two configs.
- We will watch p50 and p99 side by side from three continents.

Continue to [[z=4|the architecture]] before we run it.

@plane z=4 x=2 y=0 label="Reference Architecture"
# A pattern that actually ships

Not "rewrite everything." A seam you can adopt incrementally.

```
client -> edge function (auth, read, render)
            |
            +-- regional cache (hot reads)
            |
            +-- central core (writes, billing, audit)
```

Rules of thumb that keep teams out of trouble:

- **Reads at the edge, writes to the core.** Most traffic is reads; route them
  close.
- **Keep the source of truth singular.** Eventual consistency is a feature, not
  an accident, but money and audit logs stay central.
- **Make failure boring.** If the edge cannot reach the core, degrade to
  read-only, do not error.

Now the part you came for: [[z=5|the live demo]].

@plane z=5 x=2 y=0 label="Live Demo"
# Live demo: same code, two worlds

Switching to the terminal. If the wifi cooperates, this is the whole talk in 4
minutes.

- **Run 1 (central):** order placed from a Singapore client.
  - p50: 312ms, p99: 740ms. Audible "oof" expected from the room.
- **Run 2 (edge-first):** identical request, edge read path enabled.
  - p50: 38ms, p99: 96ms.
- **The kill switch:** I pull the core connection mid-demo. Reads keep serving
  from the regional cache; writes queue and drain when it returns.

Fallback if the demo fails: I have a recording, and I will own the irony of a
latency talk being blocked by conference wifi.

This sets up the takeaways on [[z=6|the closing slide]].

@plane z=6 x=0 y=0 label="Takeaways and Close"
# Three things to do Monday

Bring it home (2 minutes, slow down here):

1. **Measure your distance tax.** Pull p99 by region this week. You cannot fix
   what you have not split out.
2. **Find one read-heavy path.** Move just that to the edge. One seam, not a
   rewrite. Revisit [[z=4|the architecture]] for the seam.
3. **Plan the failure mode first.** Decide what "degraded" means before you ship,
   not during the incident.

Closing line:

> "The fastest request is the one that never had to cross an ocean. Go find yours."

- Thank the team and the conference organizers.
- Slides, demo source, and benchmarks: github.com/maravoss/edge-keynote.
- Open the floor for Q and A (10 minutes).

3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: Kickflip: Frame by Frame
view: play
fps: 7
deck: 8.0" Powell-Peralta
trick: Kickflip
filmed: Manual pad, Pier 7
category: Animation
---
One kickflip, 8 frames, ~0.4 seconds of air.

@plane z=0 label="Setup"
## Pop Stance

```
    O          <- head
    |
   /|\         <- arms loose, weight centered
   / \
  /   \
 /     \
f-------h      <- feet: f=front (toes over bolts), h=heel (tail pocket)
=======[]      <- board flat on ground
```

**Front foot:** balls of toes just behind front bolts, angled ~45°
**Back foot:** heel on tail, ready to pop
**Knees:** bent ~30°, coiled

---

@plane z=1 label="Pop"
## Tail Strike

```
    O
    |
   \|/         <- arms swing up for momentum
    |
   / \
  /   \
 f     H       <- back foot drives tail into ground
      /|
     / |
    /  |       <- tail kisses concrete
===---/
```

**Back foot snaps tail** — 100% of your weight loads the tail for ~0.05 sec
**Sound:** that sharp *crack* you can hear from 30 feet away
**Board angle:** ~15° nose-up as tail hits

---

@plane z=2 label="Flick"
## The Flick

```
      O
     \|/
      |
    f~          <- front foot slides up and OUT off the nose edge
      \
       \
        board rotating ↻
        =======
```

**Front foot action:** slide up the deck, let the outside edge of your shoe catch the nose pocket, then FLICK outward
**This is the whole trick.** Bad flick = late flip, no flip, or a vampire.
**Rotation target:** 360° along the board's long axis

---

@plane z=3 label="Flight-Low"
## Board Leaves Feet (Frame 1)

```
      O
     /|\        <- arms wide for balance
    / | \
   /  |  \
  /   |   \
        [====]  <- board rising, ~15° into flip, nose still high
```

**Board height:** ~6 inches off ground
**Flip progress:** 60° rotated
**Feet:** both in the air, front foot pulling back from the flick, back foot tucking up

---

@plane z=4 label="Flight-Peak"
## Peak of the Pop

```
       O
      /|\
     / | \
    /  |  \
   /   |   \
        |
       [=]      <- board vertical — 90° flip point
        |
```

**This is where you COMMIT.** Board is perfectly vertical.
**Height:** ~14 inches — your highest point
**Eyes:** watching the grip tape, waiting for it to come around
**Do not bail here.**

---

@plane z=5 label="Flight-Catch"
## Board Comes Around

```
       O
      /|\
     /   \
    f     h     <- feet dropping DOWN toward board
        [====]  <- board 270° through flip, grip facing up
```

**Grip tape is visible again** — this is your cue to catch
**Front foot:** drops first, aiming for the front bolts
**Back foot:** follows to the tail bolts
**Common bail:** catching too early (over the nose) = nose catch

---

@plane z=6 label="Land"
## Lock In

```
      O
      |
     \|/
      |
    f---h       <- both feet over bolts, knees soft
    =======[]   <- board back on ground, trucks absorb impact
```

**Both feet land simultaneously** over the bolts — not the nose, not the tail
**Knees:** bend to absorb ~1.5x bodyweight on landing
**Weight:** centered, equal on both feet
**Roll away.** That's it.

---

@plane z=7 label="Slow-Mo Stats"
## By the Numbers

| Phase | Duration | Board Rotation |
|---|---|---|
| Pop (tail strike) | 0.05 sec | 0° → 0° |
| Flick | 0.04 sec | 0° → 60° |
| Flight low | 0.06 sec | 60° → 90° |
| Peak | 0.08 sec | 90° → 270° |
| Catch | 0.06 sec | 270° → 360° |
| Land | 0.05 sec | 360° → locked |

**Total air time:** ~0.34 seconds
**Max height (deck):** ~14 inches
**Flip axis:** longitudinal (nose-to-tail)

> "The kickflip isn't magic. It's geometry with consequence." — some dude at a park

3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: Kilauea 2018: Hour by Hour
view: present
event: Lower East Rift Zone eruption, May 3–4 2018
location: Leilani Estates, Puna District, Hawaii
eruption_type: Fissure/effusive (basaltic)
source: USGS Hawaiian Volcano Observatory
category: Information
---
The Lower East Rift Zone awakens. May 3, 2018. These are the hours.

@plane z=0 label="00:00 — Tremor"
# 00:00 — First Tremor Detected

HVO seismometers pick up a **continuous low-frequency tremor** signal beneath Leilani Estates. Amplitude: 0.4 µm/s. Below the threshold most residents would feel.

The magma column in Pu'u 'O'o crater has been dropping for two days — a drainage signal every volcanologist dreads.

> "The edifice is deflating. Something is moving east."
> — HVO duty scientist field log, 04:12 HST

**Instruments watching:**
- 14 tiltmeters across the rift zone
- GPS baseline on Pu'u 'O'o shortening at 3 cm/hr
- SO₂ flux from summit: **3,200 tonnes/day** (baseline: 800)

*The magma is already in the pipe. It has been traveling since April 30.*

[[z=1|Next: ground cracking]]

@plane z=1 label="02:30 — Cracking"
# 02:30 — Ground Cracks Open on Mohala Street

A row of hairline fractures splits asphalt on **Mohala Street**, running NE–SW — parallel to the rift axis, exactly as the stress model predicted.

Steam rises from cracks. Temperature at the fracture: **94 °C**. Not lava yet — groundwater flashing to steam as the dike approaches from below.

**Dike geometry (estimated):**
| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Depth to top | ~1.5 km |
| Width | ~1 m |
| Propagation rate | ~0.8 km/hr east |
| Overpressure | ~12 MPa |

Civil Defense issues **voluntary evacuation** for Zone 9. Most residents stay.

The dike is now directly below residential streets. It is 1,500 meters from the surface.

[[z=2|Next: fissure opens]]

@plane z=2 label="04:45 — Fissure 1"
# 04:45 — Fissure 1 Breaks the Surface

A 500-meter-long fissure opens along **Leilani Avenue**. Initial lava fountains reach **30 meters**. The magma temperature at vent: **1,150 °C**.

The spatter is olivine-rich basalt, phenocrysts up to 4 mm — this is **stored rift zone magma**, not fresh summit material. It has been sitting in the system since 1955.

**Sound:** A low roar, like a waterfall, audible 2 km away. This is gas escaping at 40 m/s through a 1-meter-wide crack in the earth.

**Lava chemistry (first sample, 06:00):**
- SiO₂: 51.2%
- MgO: 7.1%
- TiO₂: 2.8%
- SO₂ at vent margin: **125 ppm** (mask required at 50 ppm)

Within 20 minutes, the first house on Leilani Avenue is consumed.

[[z=3|Next: laze and vog]]

@plane z=3 label="09:00 — Laze Plume"
# 09:00 — Lava Reaches Vacationland Road

The first lava flow, moving at **180 meters/hour**, reaches a drainage ditch. When basalt at 1,100 °C contacts standing water, the reaction is immediate.

**Laze:** Lava haze. The plume contains:
- Hydrochloric acid (HCl) — from seawater minerals in the soil
- Steam
- Fine glass shards (Pele's hair) at **< 10 µm** — respirable
- Hydrogen sulfide

> Wind direction: NE at 12 knots. The plume is moving toward Pohoiki.

Meanwhile, **vog** (volcanic smog) from SO₂ reacting with atmospheric moisture drifts across Hilo. Air Quality Index in Hilo: **168 (Unhealthy)**.

Fissures 2 and 3 have opened overnight. The rift is activating sequentially eastward, like a zipper.

[[z=4|Next: peak fountaining]]

@plane z=4 label="16:00 — Fissure 8 Roars"
# 16:00 — Fissure 8 Becomes the Main Vent

**Fissure 8** — later named *Ahu'ailā'au* — has consolidated all activity. Lava fountains now reach **80 meters**. The channel leaving the vent is **30 meters wide**, flowing at **7 km/hour** toward the coast.

This is a **Hawaiian-style** eruption at its most photogenic and most dangerous.

**Channel cross-section:**
```
  ~~ lava surface (1,070°C) ~~
  ========================= 
  |  molten channel core   |  ← 30 m wide, 2–4 m deep
  =========================
     solid levees (crust)
```

The effusion rate: **100 m³/second**. For scale — that is filling an Olympic swimming pool every 25 seconds.

SO₂ emissions peak at **100,000 tonnes/day**. The summit caldera is collapsing in response, triggering M5.3 earthquakes every 6–8 hours.

[[z=5|Next: ocean entry]]

@plane z=5 label="20:00 — Ocean Entry"
# 20:00 — The Flow Reaches the Sea

The channel crosses Highway 132 and enters the Pacific at **Kapoho Bay** — a popular snorkeling reef that has existed for 500 years.

**Delta formation begins.** As lava meets seawater:
1. Instant steam explosion (littoral explosion) — ejects lava fragments 100 m
2. Pillow lava forms where flow submerges
3. New land accretes at **~2 hectares/hour**
4. The reef is buried under 10–25 m of basalt within 8 hours

> Kapoho Bay — 0.4 km² of protected reef, home to 50+ coral species — is gone by midnight.

The new land added to Hawaii's coastline tonight: **0.8 km²**.

Geologists will be studying these flow lobes for decades. You are watching an island grow.

[[z=6|Next: ashfall and close]]

@plane z=6 label="36:00 — Ash and Silence"
# 36:00 — Summit Collapse, Ashfall, Quiet

At **02:00 on May 5**, the Halemaumau crater floor collapses 300 meters. The implosion sends an ash column **6 km** into the atmosphere.

Ashfall deposits at Volcano Village: **1.2 mm** of fine basaltic tephra. Gray dust on every surface. The smell of sulfur, metallic and cold.

**The eruption fingerprint — what you take home:**
- 35 km² of new lava field
- 716 homes destroyed
- 875 million m³ of lava erupted over 3 months
- ~3.5 km² of new land added to Hawaii Island
- 24 active fissures (Fissure 8 dominant)

And then — over the weeks that follow — silence. The fountains die. The channel drains. Fissure 8 goes cold. The new field steams in the rain for months.

> The rift does not announce when it is finished. One morning you check the seismograms and the tremor is gone, and you realize it has been quiet for two days, and that was the end.

*Field season opens. The mapping begins.*

3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: Simple Ribbed Beanie (Knit Flat, Seamed)
designer: Corvid Knitworks
yarn: Worsted weight, approx 110 yards
needles: US 8 (5.0 mm) straight
gauge: 18 sts and 24 rows to 4 inches in stockinette
size: Adult medium, 20 inch finished circumference
---
A beginner-friendly beanie worked flat in rows, then seamed up the back. Read
each step in order: every plane below is one block of rows. Abbreviations used
throughout: `k` = knit, `p` = purl, `k2tog` = knit two together, `st(s)` =
stitch(es), `RS` = right side, `WS` = wrong side.

Cast on 90 stitches loosely. Jump to [[z=1|the ribbed brim]] when ready.

@plane z=0 label="Cast On" x=0 y=0
# Step 0: Cast On

Using the long-tail cast-on, cast 90 stitches onto a single US 8 needle. Leave a
tail of about 18 inches; you will use it later to [[z=5|sew the back seam]].

- Total stitches: 90
- Tip: cast on over two needles held together if your cast-on tends to be tight.
  A loose edge keeps the brim stretchy.

This is row 0. Every odd row below is a wrong-side (WS) row and every even row is
a right-side (RS) row.

@plane z=1 label="Ribbed Brim" x=0 y=1
# Step 1: 2x2 Ribbed Brim (Rows 1 to 16)

The brim is a stretchy 2x2 rib worked over all 90 stitches for 16 rows (about 2.5
inches).

- **RS rows (1, 3, 5 ... 15):** `*k2, p2*` repeat from `*` to last 2 sts, k2.
- **WS rows (2, 4, 6 ... 16):** `*p2, k2*` repeat from `*` to last 2 sts, p2.

Knitting the knits and purling the purls as they face you keeps the columns
aligned. After row 16 the brim will fold nothing; it sits flat. Continue to
[[z=2|the stockinette body]].

@plane z=2 label="Stockinette Body" x=0 y=2
# Step 2: Stockinette Body (Rows 17 to 44)

Switch to stockinette and work straight until the piece measures about 7 inches
from the cast-on edge.

- **RS rows (odd):** knit all 90 sts.
- **WS rows (even):** purl all 90 sts.

That is 28 rows of stockinette. The fabric will want to curl at the edges; this
is normal and disappears once seamed. When the piece reaches 7 inches, stop on a
completed WS row and move to [[z=3|the crown decreases]].

@plane z=3 label="Crown Decreases" x=0 y=3
# Step 3: Crown Shaping (Rows 45 to 58)

Decrease evenly to draw the top of the hat closed. Work these on RS rows only;
purl all WS rows in between.

| Decrease row | Instruction | Sts remaining |
|---|---|---|
| Row 45 | `*k8, k2tog*` to end | 81 |
| Row 47 | `*k7, k2tog*` to end | 72 |
| Row 49 | `*k6, k2tog*` to end | 63 |
| Row 51 | `*k5, k2tog*` to end | 54 |
| Row 53 | `*k4, k2tog*` to end | 45 |
| Row 55 | `*k3, k2tog*` to end | 36 |
| Row 57 | `*k2, k2tog*` to end | 27 |

Each listed row removes 9 stitches. Purl rows 46, 48, 50, 52, 54, and 56 plain.
After row 57 you have 27 sts on the needle. Proceed to [[z=4|closing the crown]].

@plane z=4 label="Close The Crown" x=0 y=4
# Step 4: Final Decrease and Gather (Rows 58 to 59)

- **Row 58 (WS):** purl all 27 sts.
- **Row 59 (RS):** `*k1, k2tog*` to end, leaving 18 sts.

Cut the working yarn, leaving a 10 inch tail. Thread the tail through a tapestry
needle, then pass it through all 18 live stitches and slip them off the knitting
needle. Pull the tail snug to gather the crown into a tight circle, and secure it
with a small stitch on the inside. Finish at [[z=5|the seam]].

@plane z=5 label="Seam and Finish" x=0 y=5
# Step 5: Seam and Finish

You now have a flat trapezoid gathered at the top. Fold it right sides together so
the two side edges meet.

1. Using the long cast-on tail from [[z=0|the cast on]] and a tapestry needle,
   sew the back seam with mattress stitch from the brim up to the crown.
2. Weave in all remaining ends on the wrong side, working each end through at
   least 2 inches of fabric so it does not pop out.
3. Turn the hat right side out. Block lightly by misting with water and laying
   flat to dry.

Optional: fold the [[z=1|ribbed brim]] up for a double-thick cuff. Your beanie is
finished.

---
3md: 1.0
axis: scene
title: Last Train to Oslo
view: present
author: Leif
genre: literary thriller
word_count: 680
draft: 2
category: Story
---
A scene told in five locked rooms, each one smaller than the last.

@plane z=0 label="The Platform"
# 23:47. Nationaltheatret Station.

The last Oslo-bound train idles at Track 3. Marta Veen drags her suitcase through the gap between the closing doors, not quite gracefully. The wheels catch. She yanks. The doors seal behind her.

She is alone in Car 7.

Outside the window, the platform empties the way platforms always do — quickly, as if the people were never there.

She finds a seat. Row 14, window side. Places her bag on the overhead rack. Pulls her phone from her coat pocket.

Twelve unread messages from **Kristoffer**.

She turns the screen face-down on the table.

The train shudders and begins to move.

@plane z=1 label="The Bag"
# She almost didn't pack it.

The suitcase is a dark blue Samsonite, 68 litre, bought in 2019 for a holiday in Dubrovnik that they took *together*. There is still a luggage tag on the handle. Kristoffer's handwriting: *K + M, forever*.

She had peeled it halfway off, then stopped.

Inside the suitcase, folded between two sweaters she will not need in August:

- Her mother's letters, rubber-banded, the oldest dated 1987
- A USB drive, 32GB, labelled **DO NOT** in red Sharpie
- Her manuscript, printed double-sided, 214 pages

She has not told anyone about the manuscript.

She has not told anyone about what is *on* the USB drive.

The train enters the tunnel under Drammensveien. The window goes black.

@plane z=2 label="The Other Passenger"
# At Skøyen, someone gets on.

She hears him before she sees him — wheels on the floor of the carriage, the deliberate drag of someone who is *not* rushing, who has nowhere urgent to be, or who wants her to think that.

He chooses Row 12.

Two rows ahead.

She watches the back of his head in the dark glass of the window. Grey coat. No luggage. He sets something on the table — a small rectangular object, difficult to make out.

A book, maybe.

Or a phone propped flat.

Or a recorder.

He has not looked at her once. That is the thing about people who are watching you: they don't look.

The train curves north. Lysaker. Sandvika. The dark between stations stretches longer than it should.

@plane z=3 label="The Message"
# She turns the phone over.

Twelve messages have become thirteen.

The last one is not from Kristoffer.

The number is Finnish — she can tell from the prefix. The message is four words:

> **Leave it on the train.**

No context. No signature. No prior conversation.

She looks up.

Row 12 is empty. The grey coat is gone. She did not hear him stand, did not hear the door between carriages open. She has been watching the window the whole time.

On the table where he sat: nothing.

But the fluorescent strip above Row 12 is still swinging slightly, as if something passed beneath it very recently, as if the air in this carriage has not yet decided to be still.

She puts her hand on the suitcase handle.

@plane z=4 label="The Twist"
# Oslo S. 00:31.

She leaves the suitcase on the train.

Not because of the message. Not because of the man in the grey coat.

Because when she finally opened the bag at Asker to check the USB drive, she found the sweaters, she found the letters, she found the 214 pages of her manuscript.

But she found something else too, slipped between pages 107 and 108 — a photograph she has never seen, printed on cheap glossy paper, slightly damp:

**Kristoffer. Smiling. Standing in her kitchen. Dated three weeks from now.**

She walks the length of Platform 9 in the empty station, coat pulled close, phone still in her pocket.

Behind her, the train closes its doors and pulls away toward Gardermoen, carrying everything she cannot explain.

The city receives her without comment.
---

3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Brief in Opposition to Summary Judgment
court: United States District Court, Northern District of California
case: Vela v. Meridian Logistics, Inc., No. 3:24-cv-01187-WHA
party: Plaintiff Daniela Vela
counsel: Okafor & Reyes LLP
---
Read top down: the foreground plane (z=0) is the bare holding a busy judge
needs first. Each plane behind it adds depth, ending at the full doctrinal
reasoning at z=4. Drill from any layer using the cross-plane links.

@plane z=0 label="Holding" x=0 y=0
# Conclusion

Summary judgment must be DENIED. A reasonable jury could find that Meridian
classified Ms. Vela as an independent contractor while exercising the control
of an employer, so the central factual question (control over the manner and
means of her work) is genuinely disputed and cannot be resolved on the papers.

Drill down for the supporting structure: [[z=1|standard of review]],
[[z=2|the disputed facts]], [[z=3|the controlling test]], and the
[[z=4|full doctrinal analysis]].

@plane z=1 label="Standard" x=0 y=-1
# Standard of Review

Summary judgment is proper only where there is no genuine dispute as to any
material fact. Fed. R. Civ. P. 56(a). The court views the evidence in the light
most favorable to the non-moving party and draws all reasonable inferences in
her favor. Tolan v. Cotton, 572 U.S. 650, 657 (2014).

The moving party bears the initial burden. Credibility determinations and the
weighing of evidence are jury functions, not those of a judge on a Rule 56
motion. Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242, 255 (1986).

Because the [[z=2|record facts]] are disputed on the question that
[[z=3|the governing test]] makes dispositive, the [[z=0|denial]] follows.

@plane z=2 label="Disputed Facts" x=1 y=-2
# The Material Facts Are Disputed

Drawing all inferences for Ms. Vela, the record shows:

- **Scheduling.** Meridian assigned her routes through its dispatch app and
  penalized drivers who declined more than two assignments per week (Vela Decl.
  paragraph 7; Dispatch Policy 4.2).
- **Supervision.** A route manager tracked her in real time and sent corrective
  messages about speed and rest stops (Ex. 12, message logs).
- **Equipment.** She was required to lease a Meridian-branded van and run
  company software; she could not substitute her own vehicle (Lease Agr. 1-3).
- **Integration.** Last-mile delivery is Meridian's core business, not a
  peripheral service.

Meridian's contrary evidence (a signed contractor agreement and her separate
LLC) goes to weight, not to the absence of a dispute. That conflict is exactly
what [[z=1|Rule 56]] reserves for trial, and it is the conflict the
[[z=3|Borello factors]] turn on.

@plane z=3 label="Governing Test" x=1 y=-3
# The Controlling Legal Test

California applies the Borello multi-factor test to common-law employment
status, with the right to control the manner and means of work as the
principal factor. S. G. Borello & Sons, Inc. v. Dept. of Industrial Relations,
48 Cal. 3d 341, 350 (1989).

Secondary factors include:

1. Whether the worker is engaged in a distinct occupation or business.
2. Whether the work is part of the principal's regular business.
3. Who supplies the instrumentalities and place of work.
4. The method of payment (by time versus by the job).
5. Whether the parties believe they are creating an employment relationship.

No single secondary factor is decisive; they are weighed together against the
control inquiry. Applying them to the [[z=2|disputed record]] is a jury task.
The detailed weighing appears in the [[z=4|full analysis]].

@plane z=4 label="Full Analysis" x=2 y=-4
# Full Doctrinal Analysis

**Control is the heart of the dispute.** The Borello court framed control over
the manner and means of work as the most significant consideration. 48 Cal. 3d
at 350. Meridian's real-time tracking, route assignment, and corrective
messaging (see [[z=2|the supervision facts]]) are classic indicia of an
employer's control. A jury could reasonably find Meridian directed not just the
result but the method.

**The secondary factors do not rescue the motion.** Three of the five point
toward employment on this record:

- *Regular business.* Delivery is Meridian's regular business, weighing toward
  employee status (factor 2).
- *Instrumentalities.* Meridian supplied the branded van and software (factor
  3), again favoring employment.
- *Method of payment.* Per-route pay is consistent with either status and is
  therefore neutral (factor 4).

The contractor agreement and the LLC (factor 5, the parties' belief) cut the
other way, but Borello is explicit that a label the parties adopt does not
control where the economic reality differs. Where the factors point in
different directions, the weighing belongs to the jury.

**Meridian's authorities are distinguishable.** Each granted summary judgment
on an *undisputed* record where the hiring party exercised no day-to-day
control. Here the [[z=1|Rule 56 standard]] forbids resolving the live control
dispute on the papers.

**Disposition.** For these reasons the motion should be denied. This is the
reasoning that supports the [[z=0|holding]] stated at the foreground.

---
3md: 1.0
axis: slide
title: Why Does the Moon Change Shape?
subject: Grade 5 Earth Science
duration: 50 minutes
standard: NGSS 5-ESS1-2
teacher: Ms. Okafor
---
Lesson plan for a single 50-minute class period. Each plane is one slide in the deck,
projected in order. The Hook ([[z=0|slide 1]]) sets up a question the Wrap-Up
([[z=5|slide 6]]) answers, and the model on [[z=2]] is the anchor for the whole lesson.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Hook"
# Why Does the Moon Change Shape?

**Bell-ringer (5 min).** Slide shows four photos of the Moon taken on different nights:
a thin crescent, a half Moon, a gibbous Moon, and a full Moon.

Prompt on the board:
> "These are all the same Moon, photographed in one month. What is going on? Write one
> guess in your notebook before we talk."

Teacher moves: stay quiet for the first 60 seconds, then take three hands. Do not
correct wrong guesses yet, just record them on chart paper. Common ones to expect:
- "Clouds cover part of it."
- "The Earth's shadow eats it."
- "It actually shrinks and grows."

We will come back to these exact guesses in the [[z=5|Wrap-Up]].

@plane z=1 x=1 y=0 label="Prior Knowledge"
# What We Already Know

**Quick review (5 min).** Anchor today's lesson on what students locked in last week.

Cold-call review questions:
1. Where does the Moon's light come from? (The Sun. The Moon does not glow on its own.)
2. How long does the Moon take to orbit the Earth once? (About one month.)
3. Does the Moon spin? (Yes, once per orbit, so we always see the same face.)

Write the key sentence on the board and have the class read it aloud:

> "The Moon does not make light. It reflects sunlight, and we only see the lit part
> that faces us."

That single idea is the lever for the [[z=2|model demo]]. Half the Moon is always lit;
the question is how much of the lit half points toward Earth.

@plane z=2 x=2 y=0 label="Model Demo"
# Modeling the Phases

**Hands-on demonstration (15 min).** This is the heart of the lesson.

Materials per group of four:
- 1 polystyrene ball on a pencil (the Moon)
- 1 desk lamp with the shade removed (the Sun)
- The student's own head (the Earth)

Procedure:
1. One student holds the ball at arm's length and slowly turns in a full circle while
   the lamp stays fixed.
2. The rest of the group watches how much of the lit side of the ball they can see as
   it travels around.
3. Each student records the shape they see at four stations: lamp behind them, ball to
   the left, lamp behind the ball, ball to the right.

Safety note: lamps get hot, no touching the bulb, cords taped to the floor.

What they should notice: the lit half never changes, but our *view* of it does. When
the ball is between the student and the lamp, we see the dark side (new Moon). Connect
this straight back to the [[z=1|key sentence]] from review.

@plane z=3 x=3 y=0 label="Guided Practice"
# Naming the Phases

**Guided practice (10 min).** Now attach vocabulary to what they just observed.

Project a phase diagram and fill it in together. Students label a printed handout as
the class goes:

| Position of Moon | What we see | Name |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Between Sun and Earth | Dark face | New Moon |
| Quarter way around | Right half lit | First Quarter |
| Opposite the Sun | Full face lit | Full Moon |
| Three-quarters around | Left half lit | Third Quarter |

Teach "waxing" (getting bigger, right side lit) versus "waning" (getting smaller,
left side lit) with a hand gesture: right hand cups a growing Moon, left hand cups a
shrinking one.

Check for understanding: hold up the foam ball at a new position and ask the class to
name the phase with a thumbs signal before anyone speaks.

@plane z=4 x=4 y=0 label="Independent Work"
# Your Turn: Moon Journal

**Independent practice (8 min in class, then homework).**

In class: each student sketches the four main phases in order and writes one sentence
explaining why the new Moon looks dark, using the words "lit" and "facing."

Homework (the Moon Journal):
- [ ] Look for the Moon at the same time each night for the next week.
- [ ] Draw what you see, even if it is hidden by clouds (write "cloudy").
- [ ] Note whether it is waxing or waning.

Differentiation:
- **Support:** sentence starter provided ("The new Moon looks dark because...").
- **Stretch:** predict the phase exactly seven nights from tonight and explain.

Rubric lives on the back of the handout: 2 points for ordered phases, 2 for the
explanation, 1 for correct waxing/waning labels.

@plane z=5 x=5 y=0 label="Wrap-Up"
# So, Why Does the Moon Change Shape?

**Closure (2 min).** Return to the chart paper from the [[z=0|Hook]].

Read each original guess aloud and have the class vote thumbs-up or thumbs-down now
that they have the model:
- "Clouds cover part of it." (No, the shape is sharp and curved, not patchy.)
- "The Earth's shadow eats it." (No, that is a lunar eclipse, which is rare.)
- "It shrinks and grows." (No, only our view of the lit half changes.)

Exit ticket (one index card):
> "In one sentence, tell me why the Moon looks different shapes during the month."

Collect cards at the door. Tomorrow's lesson on eclipses builds directly on the model
from [[z=2|the demo]], so flag any exit tickets that still mention Earth's shadow.

3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: The Anatomy of a Letterform
view: stack
subject: lowercase 'g' (double-storey), set in Garamond Premier Pro, 72pt
designer: Claude Garamond, c. 1540
category: Education
---
Peel back the layers of a single glyph — the double-storey **g** — from its skeleton to its surface.

@plane z=0 label="Skeleton"
# The Skeleton

Everything begins with an **invisible armature** — the ductus, the path a broad-nib pen would trace.

```
     ___
    /   \
   |  +  |   ← optical center (not geometric)
    \___/
      |
    __|__
   /     \
  |   +   |  ← counter (lower bowl)
   \_____/
```

The double-storey g descends from humanist manuscript hands. Its two loops mirror the **binocular structure of the eye** — which is why it feels familiar even before you name it.

- **Stress angle:** ~15° (oblique, matching the pen hold)
- **Joins:** thin strokes at the northwest and southeast
- No curves are geometric arcs; every path is **optically corrected**

@plane z=1 label="Proportions"
# Proportions & Metrics

| Zone | Name | Garamond value (UPM 1000) |
|---|---|---|
| Top of ascender | **Ascender line** | 720 |
| Top of uppercase | **Cap height** | 660 |
| Top of x | **x-height** | 430 |
| Bottom of roman | **Baseline** | 0 |
| Bottom of g's ear | **Descender line** | −220 |

The upper bowl of the g sits **entirely within the x-height zone** (430 → 0). The link — that sinuous neck — drops through the baseline. The lower bowl and ear extend into the descender zone.

> **Ear:** the small terminal stroke projecting from the upper-right of the bowl. In Garamond it is a teardrop, not a spur — a marker of Renaissance origin.

@plane z=2 label="Strokes"
# Named Strokes

Every line has a name. Learn them and you can **diagnose any typeface** in seconds.

- **Bowl** — the closed, rounded form. The g has two: *upper bowl* (smaller) and *lower bowl* (larger, open at base in some cuts).
- **Link** — the curved stroke connecting the two bowls. Its weight and angle define the typeface's personality.
- **Ear** — terminal at the upper-right of the upper bowl. Garamond's ear curves gently clockwise; Gill Sans omits it entirely.
- **Loop** — the lower, sometimes open, counter. In italic g, it frequently remains open.
- **Spine** — not present in g, but its absence here distinguishes g from s.
- **Counter** — the enclosed (or partially enclosed) white space *inside* each bowl. White space is as designed as the black ink.

Stroke width follows **thick-thin contrast**: stems are ~140 units wide; hairlines ~30 units.

@plane z=3 label="Terminals"
# Terminals & Finishing Details

The moment a stroke ends is where the designer's hand shows most clearly.

**Garamond Premier Pro lowercase g terminals:**

1. **Ear terminal** — a ball terminal with slight leftward tilt, ~40 units diameter
2. **Link-to-lower-bowl join** — a cupped, concave junction; the ink trap prevents fill at small sizes
3. **Lower bowl opening** — in the roman, the loop closes; in italic, it opens into a flag terminal pointing ~40° left

### Ink Traps

At the junction of the link and lower bowl, Garamond cuts a tiny notch — an **ink trap** — visible only under magnification. At 8pt, ink floods the junction; the trap pre-compensates so the join reads clean.

> "The eye does not see type. The eye sees the spaces type creates." — Adrian Frutiger

@plane z=4 label="Spacing"
# Side Bearings & Spacing

The g is not an island. Its metrics reach into the words around it.

```
  |← LSB →|← glyph width →|← RSB →|
           gggggggggggggggg
```

- **Left side bearing (LSB):** ~30 units — tight, because the upper bowl's curve provides optical distance
- **Right side bearing (RSB):** ~35 units — slightly wider; the ear's horizontal projection demands breathing room
- **Advance width:** ~530 units total

**Kerning pairs (Garamond, selected):**

| Pair | Kern value | Why |
|---|---|---|
| `Ag` | −18 | A's right leg tucks under g's bowl |
| `gy` | −12 | y's left arm echoes g's right curve |
| `fg` | +8 | f's arm and g's ear collide without space |

Spacing is **not optical illusion correction** — it is optical illusion *construction*. The goal is equal apparent whitespace, not equal measured whitespace.

@plane z=5 label="Rendering"
# From Outline to Pixel

The Bézier outline is only halfway to the reader's eye.

### Hinting

At 16px on a 96dpi screen, the g's lower bowl is ~7 pixels tall. Without hints, the counter disappears. **TrueType delta hints** snap stems to pixel boundaries:

- Stem width forced to 2px at 12–14px
- Bowl counter forced open with a minimum 1px gap
- Link thinned to 1px below 18px

### Variable Font Axes (Garamond Premier Variable, hypothetical)

| Axis tag | Range | Effect on g |
|---|---|---|
| `wght` | 300–700 | Link thickens; ear becomes ball vs. flag |
| `opsz` | 6–72 | At 6pt: wider counters, open loop; at 72pt: tighter spacing, refined ear |
| `ital` | 0–1 | Loop opens; link angle shifts from 15° to 8° |

> The double-storey g is the **canary of type rendering** — if your g looks good, your rasterizer is working.

@plane z=6 label="Recognition"
# The g as Diagnostic Glyph

Typographers use the g (and the a) to fingerprint unknown typefaces.

**Field guide — what the g tells you:**

- **Double-storey g with open loop** → Probably a text face designed for optical sizes (Garamond, Minion, Arno)
- **Double-storey g with closed loop** → Classical, upright roman (Times New Roman, Palatino)
- **Single-storey g** → Humanist sans or geometric (Gill Sans, Futura, Optima)
- **Single-storey g with large loop** → Contemporary neutral (Inter, DM Sans)
- **Rotated-ear g** → Early digital constraints or deliberate retro (ITC Garamond — controversial)

> Adobe's Robert Slimbach recut Garamond in 1989 after studying specimens at the Plantin-Moretus Museum in Antwerp. He corrected the ear angle that had drifted in earlier revivals. That correction is visible — to a trained eye — in a single glyph.

See the skeleton that started it all: [[z=0|back to skeleton]]

3md: 1.0
axis: floor
title: Maplewood Central Library - Floor Guide
building: Maplewood Central Library
address: 412 Birchwood Avenue
hours: Mon-Thu 9am-9pm, Fri-Sat 9am-6pm, Sun 12pm-5pm
elevators: 2 (north lobby, south stair core)
---
Welcome to Maplewood Central Library. This guide stacks the building floor by
floor, lowest to highest. Each floor lists its collections, service desks, and
quiet ratings. Use the elevators in the north lobby or take the south stair
core. Restrooms are on every floor near the elevators.

@plane z=-1 x=0 y=0 label="Lower Level - Archives and Parking"
# Lower Level (B1)

The lower level holds the climate-controlled archives and the staff workroom.
Patron access is by appointment at the [[z=0|Welcome Desk]] on the ground floor.

## Collections
- Local history archives (1851 to present)
- Microfilm: regional newspapers, census records
- Map and atlas vault (oversized, flat storage)

## Services
- Archivist office, room B1-04 (request items 24 hours ahead)
- Two microfilm reader stations
- Underground parking entrance (40 spaces, accessible bays nearest the elevator)

## Quiet rating
Silent. No phone calls. Gloves provided for handling original materials.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Ground Floor - Welcome and Popular Reads"
# Ground Floor

The front entrance, the busiest service point, and the popular collections. Start
here, then ride up to [[z=1|Adult Nonfiction]] or down to [[z=-1|the Archives]].

## Service desks
- Welcome Desk (left of the main doors): library cards, directions, lost and found
- Holds pickup shelves (self-service, alphabetized by last name)
- Self-checkout kiosks (four units, card or app)

## Collections
- New and popular fiction
- Large print and audiobooks
- DVDs, board games, and the Library of Things (tools, kits, ukuleles)
- Daily newspapers and current magazines

## Amenities
- Cafe cart (coffee, tea, pastries) open until 4pm
- Friends of the Library used-book nook

## Quiet rating
Lively. Conversation welcome. Phone calls fine near the entrance.

@plane z=1 x=0 y=0 label="Second Floor - Adult Nonfiction and Reference"
# Second Floor

Nonfiction, reference, and the reading room. Quieter than [[z=0|the ground floor]];
for group work head up to [[z=2|the Learning Commons]].

## Collections
- Nonfiction (Dewey 000 to 999), shelved north to south
- Reference (in-library use only)
- Genealogy and government documents

## Services
- Reference Desk (center of the floor): research help, interlibrary loan
- Two scan-to-email stations and a black-and-white printer
- The Reading Room: 30 individual carrels with task lighting

## Quiet rating
Quiet study. Headphones required for audio. Calls only in the stair landing.

@plane z=2 x=0 y=0 label="Third Floor - Youth and Learning Commons"
# Third Floor

The children's and teen wings, plus collaborative spaces. The noisiest upper
floor by design. For silence, return to [[z=1|the Reading Room]].

## Children's wing (west)
- Picture books and early readers
- Story-time alcove (sessions Tue and Thu, 10:30am)
- Play-and-learn area with low shelving and bean bags

## Teen wing (east)
- Young adult fiction and graphic novels
- Gaming consoles (booking required at the desk)
- Homework help table (volunteer tutors, weekday afternoons)

## Learning Commons (center)
- Six bookable group study rooms (seats 4 to 8, reserve online)
- Maker bench: 3D printer, vinyl cutter, sewing machine
- Open laptop bar with 20 charging seats

## Quiet rating
Active. Talking and collaboration encouraged. Maker tools run during open hours.

@plane z=3 x=0 y=0 label="Fourth Floor - Rooftop Reading Terrace"
# Fourth Floor

The top floor opens onto the rooftop terrace, the calmest spot for reading with
a view. Bring a book up from any floor; food from the [[z=0|ground-floor cafe]] is
welcome on the terrace.

## Spaces
- Glass-walled quiet lounge with armchairs
- Covered outdoor terrace (open Apr through Oct, weather permitting)
- Community meeting room (seats 60, booked through the Welcome Desk)

## Amenities
- Water bottle filling station
- Accessible terrace ramp and shaded seating

## Quiet rating
Restful. Low conversation indoors, quiet enjoyment on the terrace.

3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: Intro to Film Photography
course_code: PHOT 101
meeting: Tuesdays 18:00 to 20:30
instructor: J. Okafor
weeks: "6"
---
A six-week course syllabus. The axis is time: each plane is one week of class,
scrub the Z axis to move through the term. Every plane lists the topic, the
in-class work, the assignment due, and what to bring. Camera required by week 1; a
35mm SLR loaner is available on request.

@plane z=0 label="Week 1 - The Camera"
# Week 1: The Camera and the Frame
Topic: how a film SLR works. Aperture, shutter, ISO, and the exposure triangle.

- In class: load a roll, dry-fire metering drills, composition walk outside
- Assignment due: none
- Bring: your camera, one roll of ISO 400 black and white film
- Read: handout on the exposure triangle

The shooting habits you build now pay off when we develop in [[z=2|week 3]].

@plane z=1 label="Week 2 - Light and Exposure"
# Week 2: Reading Light
Topic: metering modes, the zone system basics, exposure compensation.

- In class: bracket the same scene across five stops, predict the results
- Assignment due: shoot a full roll exploring hard versus soft light
- Bring: your exposed roll from this week, light meter app on your phone
- Read: chapter 2, "Previsualization"

@plane z=2 label="Week 3 - Developing"
# Week 3: Developing Film
Topic: the darkroom chemistry workflow, developer, stop, fix, wash.

- In class: develop the roll you shot in [[z=1|week 2]], step by step
- Assignment due: bring two exposed rolls ready to develop
- Bring: notebook, an old shirt, your exposed rolls
- Safety: closed-toe shoes required in the darkroom

@plane z=3 label="Week 4 - Printing"
# Week 4: The Enlarger
Topic: contact sheets, test strips, dodging and burning.

- In class: make a contact sheet, then one fine 8x10 print
- Assignment due: a contact sheet from your best roll so far
- Bring: your developed negatives in sleeves
- Read: chapter 4, "From Negative to Print"

@plane z=4 label="Week 5 - The Project"
# Week 5: Studio and Critique Prep
Topic: building a cohesive series, sequencing, and self-editing.

- In class: edit your negatives down to a six-image series, peer feedback
- Assignment due: a working edit of 12 candidate prints
- Bring: all prints made so far, pushpins
- Note: final series due next week

@plane z=5 label="Week 6 - Final Critique"
# Week 6: Final Critique
Topic: present and defend your finished series.

- In class: gallery walk and group critique of every student's final six prints
- Assignment due: final mounted series of six prints plus a one-paragraph statement
- Bring: your matted prints ready to hang
- Looking back: the strongest work usually traces to habits set in [[z=0|week 1]].

3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: Backyard Vegetable Garden Plan
zone: USDA 7b
plot: 4 raised beds, 4ft by 8ft each
sun: full sun, 8 hours
---
A season-by-season plan for a four-bed raised garden. The axis is time: each plane
is a season, scrub the Z axis to walk through the gardening year. Bed numbers (B1
to B4) are consistent across seasons so you can practice crop rotation. The layout
below shows the beds.

```
   [ B1 ]   [ B2 ]
   [ B3 ]   [ B4 ]
   (path runs north-south down the middle)
```

@plane z=0 label="Early Spring"
# Early Spring (March to April)
Cool weather. Last frost expected mid-April; protect tender starts.

- B1: peas (sugar snap), spinach, radishes
- B2: lettuce mix, arugula, scallions
- B3: prep and amend with compost, plant garlic if not already in
- B4: cover crop (crimson clover) turned under late April

Tasks: build pea trellis, start tomato and pepper seeds indoors. The starts you
sow now go into the ground in [[z=1|summer]].

@plane z=1 label="Summer"
# Summer (May to August)
Peak growth. Water deeply in the early morning.

- B1: tomatoes (Sungold, San Marzano) on stakes
- B2: peppers and basil, cucumbers on the trellis from spring
- B3: bush beans, zucchini, marigolds for pest control
- B4: sweet corn block, with squash at the base

Tasks: mulch heavily, side-dress tomatoes with compost mid-June, harvest beans
every other day. Plan the fall sowings on [[z=2|autumn]] before the corn finishes.

@plane z=2 label="Autumn"
# Autumn (September to October)
Cooling off. Second cool-season crop and cleanup.

- B1: clear tomatoes, sow garlic for next year
- B2: fall lettuce, kale, Swiss chard
- B3: turnips, beets, more radishes
- B4: broccoli and cauliflower transplants

Tasks: pull spent summer plants, top up compost, sow cover crop where beds are
empty.

@plane z=3 label="Winter"
# Winter (November to February)
Rest and plan. Most beds dormant.

- B1: garlic overwintering under straw mulch
- B2: kale and chard under a low tunnel, harvest as needed
- B3: bare, cover-cropped, resting the soil
- B4: bare, sheet-mulched with leaves

Tasks: order seeds, sharpen and oil tools, sketch next year's rotation so heavy
feeders move off [[z=1|the summer tomato bed]]. The cycle restarts at
[[z=0|early spring]].
