3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: Seed Germination: Frame-by-Frame
view: play
fps: 4
species: Phaseolus vulgaris (common bean)
days_elapsed: 0–10 DAP (days after planting)
depth_cm: 5
category: Animation
---
*Phaseolus vulgaris* — watch hypocotyl emergence through soil break, rendered at 4 fps.

@plane z=0 label="Day 0 — Dry Seed"
```
  soil surface
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
                                
  [ seed ]                      
   (  )                         
   ~~~~                         
                                
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```
**Day 0 — Imbibition begins.**
Seed coat absorbs ~150% its dry weight in water within 6 h. Metabolic rate: near zero. Axis embryo still compressed. No radicle visible.

@plane z=1 label="Day 2 — Radicle Emerges"
```
  soil surface
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
                                
  [ seed ]                      
   (  )                         
     |   ← radicle              
                                
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```
**Day 2 — Radicle protrusion.**
Primary root punches through micropyle end. Gravitropism kicks in; auxin redistributes to lower flank. Root cap secretes mucilage to lubricate soil passage.

@plane z=2 label="Day 3 — Hypocotyl Hook"
```
  soil surface
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
                                
   ( )                          
    |                           
    |  ← hypocotyl elongating   
    |                           
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```
**Day 3 — Hypocotyl straightens upward.**
Hook unfolding driven by differential ethylene on concave side. Root hairs begin forming 2–4 mm behind root tip. Stored cotyledon starch mobilising via amylase.

@plane z=3 label="Day 5 — Soil Break"
```
  soil surface
  ┌─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─┐
  ↑ breaking through           
   \                           
    | ← hypocotyl              
    |                           
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```
**Day 5 — Emergence (VE stage).**
Apical hook breaches surface. Cotyledons still folded, protecting the shoot apical meristem. Chloroplast differentiation begins on light exposure. The seedling is now autotrophic-adjacent.

@plane z=4 label="Day 7 — Cotyledons Open"
```
 \\   //  ← cotyledons spread  
  \\ //                        
   \\/                         
    |                          
    |                          
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```
**Day 7 — Cotyledons unfurl.**
Chlorophyll a/b ratio ~2.8. Stomata open; transpiration begins. First photosynthetic CO₂ fixation detectable. Epicotyl elongating between cotyledon axils toward first true leaves.

@plane z=5 label="Day 10 — First True Leaves"
```
   _/ \_   ← true leaf 1 (V1)  
  /     \                      
 \\     //  ← cotyledons       
   \\ //                       
    ||                         
    ||                         
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```
**Day 10 — V1 stage.**
Unifoliate primary leaves fully expanded (~4 cm²). Cotyledons yellowing as reserves depleted. Trifoliate leaves initiating in SAM. Root system: taproot 8 cm + lateral network forming. Seedling is fully established.

3md: 1.0
axis: slide
title: Seed Pitch: CorvidLabs Developer Tools
view: present

category: Business
---
One idea per slide. Raise $1.2M to become the Swift toolchain every indie and studio team installs on day one.

@plane z=0 label="Problem"
# Developers ship slow because the Swift ecosystem has no opinionated toolkit

Every team reinvents the same 12 wheels:

- **Retry logic** written from scratch in every network layer
- **Validation** scattered across models with no shared protocol
- **Caching** duct-taped together from UserDefaults + NSCache
- **Environment config** loaded differently on every platform

> "We spent two sprints on infra before writing a single feature."
> — iOS Lead, Series B startup (2025 customer interview)

The gap: Apple ships the language. Nobody ships the **standard library for apps**.

@plane z=1 label="Solution"
# CorvidLabs: zero-dependency Swift micro-libraries that compose

Eight focused packages, one coherent system:

| Package | One-liner |
|---|---|
| `swift-retry` | Exponential backoff with typed throw support |
| `swift-cache` | Actor-safe, generics-first LRU cache |
| `swift-valid` | Composable validation pipelines |
| `swift-env` | Type-safe environment loading, all platforms |
| `swift-parse` | Lightweight parser combinators |
| `swift-color` | Cross-platform Color ↔ hex ↔ HSL |
| `swift-music` | Note, scale, and MIDI primitives |
| `swift-qr` | Pure-Swift QR generation, no Vision needed |

**Philosophy:** strict concurrency on by default, async/await only, zero force-unwraps.

@plane z=2 label="Traction"
# 14 months in — real signal

- **2.1M** cumulative Swift Package Index downloads
- **4,300** GitHub stars across repos (organic, zero ad spend)
- **38** paying Studio tier subscribers at $49/mo = **$1,862 MRR**
- `swift-retry` added to 3 YC W25 companies' production stacks
- Featured in **Swift by Sundell** newsletter (April 2026, 42K readers)

**NPS: 71** — surveyed 110 active users, June 2026

> The funnel: open-source adoption → Studio tier (CI integrations + SLAs) → Enterprise contracts.

@plane z=3 label="Market"
# $4.1B developer tools TAM, Swift is the wedge

- **6.9M** registered Apple developers (App Store Connect, 2025)
- **~800K** professional Swift developers shipping production apps
- Adjacent: Swift on server (Vapor, Hummingbird ecosystems) adding 60K+ devs/yr

**Comparable exits:**
- Fastlane → Google: $172M (2017) — 1 tool, huge mindshare
- Sentry → $\$225M ARR (2025) — SDK-led growth same motion

Our beachhead: **iOS indie + small studio** (50K teams), expand to enterprise via `swift-env` + `swift-cache` (audit logs, on-prem builds).

@plane z=4 label="Business Model"
# Three tiers, SDK-led growth

**Free / Open Source**
All packages, MIT license. This is the acquisition engine.

**Studio — $49/mo per team**
- Private Slack channel with Leif
- SLA: critical bug fix within 48h
- CI-optimized binary caches (saves ~4 min/build)
- Early access to unreleased packages

**Enterprise — $1,200/mo**
- Custom forks, compliance docs, on-prem hosting support
- Named support engineer
- Currently: 2 pilots in contract negotiation ($28.8K ARR each)

**12-month target:** 200 Studio + 5 Enterprise = **$189.6K ARR**

@plane z=5 label="Ask"
# $1.2M Seed — 18-month runway to $500K ARR

**Use of funds:**

```
Engineering (2 hires)     $540K   45%
```

**Milestones this money buys:**

**Lead check size:** $400K–$800K. Targeting close: August 29, 2026.

3md: 1.0
axis: slide
title: The Prodigal Son - Coming Home
speaker: Pastor J. Reyes
passage: Luke 15:11-32
service: Sunday Morning
duration: 32 minutes
---
Sermon outline for the message on Luke 15:11-32, "The Prodigal Son."
Advance one slide per section. Speaker notes live under each heading.

@plane z=0 label="Title" x=0 y=0
# Coming Home

### The Prodigal Son - Luke 15:11-32

> "But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and was
> filled with compassion for him; he ran to his son, threw his arms
> around him and kissed him." (v. 20)

**Big idea:** No distance is too far for the Father's welcome.

Open with the question: where do you picture God standing when you finally
turn back toward home? Move to [[z=1|the younger son's demand]] to begin.

@plane z=1 label="The Demand" x=-2 y=1
# 1. The Demand (vv. 11-13)

- The younger son asks for his share of the estate **while the father is
  still living** - in that culture, a wish that the father were dead.
- The father grants it. Love does not cage; it gives room to choose.
- The son "set off for a distant country" and squandered everything in
  reckless living.

**Application:** Sin is rarely a single leap. It is a slow drift toward
a country far from the Father's table.

Next: what the far country actually costs in [[z=2|The Famine]].

@plane z=2 label="The Famine" x=2 y=1
# 2. The Famine (vv. 14-16)

- A severe famine strikes; the son ends up feeding pigs - for a Jewish
  listener, the lowest, most defiling work imaginable.
- "He longed to fill his stomach with the pods... but no one gave him
  anything." (v. 16)
- The far country always over-promises and under-delivers.

**Application:** Emptiness is not the enemy here. Sometimes the famine is
the mercy that turns us back toward home.

@plane z=3 label="The Turning" x=0 y=2
# 3. The Turning (vv. 17-20a)

- "When he came to his senses..." (v. 17) - repentance begins with seeing
  clearly again.
- He rehearses a speech: "I have sinned against heaven and against you."
- Then the decisive verb: "So he got up and went to his father." (v. 20)

**Application:** Repentance is not just feeling sorry. It is standing up
and walking in a new direction - toward the Father, not away.

Set up the surprise that meets him in [[z=4|The Welcome]].

@plane z=4 label="The Welcome" x=2 y=3
# 4. The Welcome (vv. 20b-24)

- The father **runs** - undignified for an elder in that day - and embraces
  him before the speech is even finished.
- Robe, ring, sandals, fattened calf: full restoration, not probation.
- "This son of mine was dead and is alive again; he was lost and is found."

**Application:** Grace runs to meet you. You do not clean yourself up first;
you come home, and the cleaning happens in the Father's arms.

@plane z=5 label="The Invitation" x=0 y=4
# 5. The Invitation (vv. 25-32)

- The older brother is angry and will not go in. He has obeyed for years
  yet never felt like a son.
- The father goes out to him too: "You are always with me, and everything
  I have is yours." (v. 31)
- The story ends unresolved - an open door, and the question hanging in the
  air: will you come in?

**Closing call:** Whether you ran to the far country or stayed home and grew
bitter, the Father is outside, pleading. Come to the table.

Return to [[z=0|the opening question]] and pray.

```
░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░
```

3md: 1.0
axis: floor
title: MS Corvid Aurora - Deck Plan
vessel: MS Corvid Aurora
gross_tonnage: "142,000 GT"
guest_capacity: "3,560"
length_overall: 330 m
---
Deck plan for the **MS Corvid Aurora**, read from the keel upward. Each plane is one
deck; the `z` value is the deck number. Public spaces sit low and amidships, cabins
stack in the middle, and the open recreation decks crown the ship.

Muster stations are on Deck 4 (see [[z=4|the Promenade muster map]]). For dining,
jump to [[z=5|Deck 5]].

@plane z=1 label="Deck 1 - Crew and Machinery" x=0 y=0
# Deck 1 - Crew and Machinery

The lowest guest-adjacent deck. Most of this level is off-limits to passengers.

- **Engine control room** (frames 40-72): twin medium-speed diesel-electric plants.
- **Crew mess and galley** (amidships): three sittings, 0600 to 2200.
- **Provision stores**: refrigerated and dry holds, restocked at every turnaround port.
- **Laundry**: 14,000 kg of linen processed per sea day.

Guest access is limited to the **medical center** forward (frames 12-20), staffed
around the clock with two physicians and four nurses. The infirmary connects by a
dedicated lift to the gangway on [[z=4|Deck 4]] for shoreside transfers.

@plane z=4 label="Deck 4 - Promenade and Muster" x=0 y=0
# Deck 4 - Promenade and Muster

The teak **Promenade** wraps the full hull at this level, a continuous 0.4 km loop
(three laps to the mile). This is the ship's safety hub.

## Muster stations

| Station | Location | Assembly area |
|---------|----------|---------------|
| A | Forward, port | Aurora Theater stalls |
| B | Forward, starboard | Aurora Theater balcony |
| C | Amidships | Atrium, decks 4-6 |
| D | Aft, port | Horizon Lounge |
| E | Aft, starboard | Card Room and Library |

Lifeboats hang from davits along both sides at this deck. The **gangway** is here
at frame 16, port side, used for tendering and port calls.

Take the central stair up one level to reach the restaurants on [[z=5|Deck 5]],
or continue to the cabins beginning on [[z=7|Deck 7]].

@plane z=5 label="Deck 5 - Dining and Atrium" x=0 y=0
# Deck 5 - Dining and Atrium

The social spine of the ship. A three-story **Atrium** rises through decks 4 to 6,
crowned by a sculptural glass chandelier.

## Where to eat

- **The Meridian** (aft, frames 64-88): main dining room, two seatings plus open
  flexible dining. Floor-to-ceiling stern windows.
- **Saffron** (port, midship): pan-Asian a la carte, reservations suggested.
- **Helm & Anchor** (starboard, midship): grill and raw bar.
- **The Pantry** (forward): casual marketplace buffet, open 24 hours.

The **Guest Services** and **Shore Excursions** desks ring the Atrium base. For the
show after dinner, the Aurora Theater spans down to the muster level on
[[z=4|Deck 4]]; head up to the cabins on [[z=7|Deck 7]] to turn in.

@plane z=7 label="Deck 7 - Staterooms" x=0 y=0
# Deck 7 - Staterooms

The first of the accommodation decks. Cabin numbers run **7001-7088** port and
**7501-7588** starboard, lowest forward.

## Cabin categories on this deck

- **Interior** (no window): frames 8-28, forward.
- **Oceanview** (picture window): along the hull, amidships.
- **Balcony** (private veranda): aft two-thirds of the deck.

Odd numbers are port, even numbers are starboard. A self-service **launderette**
sits at the midship lift lobby, along with ice and a vending alcove.

Three banks of guest lifts connect every passenger deck. From the lobby you can
drop to dining on [[z=5|Deck 5]] or ride up to the pools on [[z=14|Deck 14]].

@plane z=14 label="Deck 14 - Pool and Lido" x=0 y=0
# Deck 14 - Pool and Lido

The open-air recreation deck, wind shadowed by the superstructure forward.

## Layout, bow to stern

1. **Sanctuary** (forward, adults only): shaded loungers and a quiet bar.
2. **Main pool and two whirlpools** (amidships): retractable glass roof for cool weather.
3. **The Galley Grill** and **Scoops** ice cream (poolside).
4. **Wave Stage** (aft): live music and the evening movie screen.

A jogging track and the **Vista Spa** sun terrace sit one half-level up at the
forward mast house. Loungers fill fast on sea days, so claim one early. To return
to your room, the aft lifts run straight down to [[z=7|Deck 7]]; for a late bite,
The Pantry on [[z=5|Deck 5]] stays open all night.

3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: Sicilian Najdorf: First 6 Moves
view: play
fps: 2
opening: Sicilian Defense, Najdorf Variation
eco: B90
players: Fischer, Kasparov, Polgar — all loved this
category: Games
---
The sharpest, most deeply analyzed opening in chess history. Used by Fischer and Kasparov to squeeze every ounce of imbalance out of the position. Watch it unfold move by move.

@plane z=0 label="Start"
## Starting Position

```
  a b c d e f g h
8 ♜ ♞ ♝ ♛ ♚ ♝ ♞ ♜
7 ♟ ♟ ♟ ♟ ♟ ♟ ♟ ♟
6 · · · · · · · ·
5 · · · · · · · ·
4 · · · · · · · ·
3 · · · · · · · ·
2 ♙ ♙ ♙ ♙ ♙ ♙ ♙ ♙
1 ♖ ♘ ♗ ♕ ♔ ♗ ♘ ♖
  a b c d e f g h
```

**White to move.** Pieces set, tension zero, everything still possible.

> "The most important feature of the chess position is the activity of the pieces." — Garry Kasparov

@plane z=1 label="1. e4"
## 1. e4 — White Occupies the Center

```
  a b c d e f g h
8 ♜ ♞ ♝ ♛ ♚ ♝ ♞ ♜
7 ♟ ♟ ♟ ♟ ♟ ♟ ♟ ♟
6 · · · · · · · ·
5 · · · · · · · ·
4 · · · · ♙ · · ·
3 · · · · · · · ·
2 ♙ ♙ ♙ ♙ · ♙ ♙ ♙
1 ♖ ♘ ♗ ♕ ♔ ♗ ♘ ♖
  a b c d e f g h
```

**e4** — the King's Pawn. Controls d5 and f5, opens lines for the bishop and queen. Bobby Fischer called it "best by test."

- Controls: **d5, f5**
- Opens: **d1–h5 diagonal**, **f1 bishop**
- Principle: *Occupy the center with pawns early*

@plane z=2 label="1… c5"
## 1… c5 — The Sicilian!

```
  a b c d e f g h
8 ♜ ♞ ♝ ♛ ♚ ♝ ♞ ♜
7 ♟ ♟ · ♟ ♟ ♟ ♟ ♟
6 · · · · · · · ·
5 · · ♟ · · · · ·
4 · · · · ♙ · · ·
3 · · · · · · · ·
2 ♙ ♙ ♙ ♙ · ♙ ♙ ♙
1 ♖ ♘ ♗ ♕ ♔ ♗ ♘ ♖
  a b c d e f g h
```

**c5** — Black fights for d4 without mirroring White's center. Asymmetry is the whole point: Black gets queenside counterplay while White attacks the king.

- Controls: **d4** (contests it without occupying)
- Black's plan: **…d6, …Nf6, …a6**, then queenside expansion
- This imbalance is why the Sicilian scores *so many decisive games*

@plane z=3 label="2. Nf3 d6"
## 2. Nf3 d6 — Development and Preparation

```
  a b c d e f g h
8 ♜ ♞ ♝ ♛ ♚ ♝ ♞ ♜
7 ♟ ♟ · · ♟ ♟ ♟ ♟
6 · · · ♟ · · · ·
5 · · ♟ · · · · ·
4 · · · · ♙ · · ·
3 · · · · · ♘ · ·
2 ♙ ♙ ♙ ♙ · ♙ ♙ ♙
1 ♖ ♘ ♗ ♕ ♔ ♗ ♘ ♖
  a b c d e f g h
```

**Nf3** develops a piece and eyes d4 and e5. Black plays **d6**, supporting c5 and preparing …Nf6 without allowing e4–e5.

- Nf3 threatens nothing yet — it develops with **tempo options** later
- d6 is slightly passive but rock solid; it says "I'll defend before I attack"
- Alternative: **2… Nc6** (Classical), **2… e6** (Scheveningen) — both good

@plane z=4 label="3. d4 cxd4"
## 3. d4 cxd4 — The Trade That Defines Everything

```
  a b c d e f g h
8 ♜ ♞ ♝ ♛ ♚ ♝ ♞ ♜
7 ♟ ♟ · · ♟ ♟ ♟ ♟
6 · · · ♟ · · · ·
5 · · · · · · · ·
4 · · · ♟ ♙ · · ·
3 · · · · · ♘ · ·
2 ♙ ♙ ♙ · · ♙ ♙ ♙
1 ♖ ♘ ♗ ♕ ♔ ♗ ♘ ♖
  a b c d e f g h
```

White opens the center; Black captures. After **4. Nxd4**, White has a *pawn majority in the center* and space. Black has an *open c-file* and no doubled pawns.

> The entire Sicilian is built on this structural imbalance.

- White's extra central pawn → **kingside attack potential**
- Black's open c-file → **queenside counterplay**
- Material is equal; the *dynamic* imbalance is everything

@plane z=5 label="4. Nxd4 Nf6 5. Nc3 a6"
## 5… a6 — The Najdorf Move

```
  a b c d e f g h
8 ♜ ♞ ♝ ♛ ♚ ♝ · ♜
7 · ♟ · · ♟ ♟ ♟ ♟
6 ♟ · · ♟ · ♞ · ·
5 · · · · · · · ·
4 · · · ♘ ♙ · · ·
3 · · ♘ · · · · ·
2 ♙ ♙ ♙ · · ♙ ♙ ♙
1 ♖ · ♗ ♕ ♔ ♗ · ♖
  a b c d e f g h
```

**a6** — Miguel Najdorf's patent. A quiet pawn move that prevents Nb5, prepares …b5–b4, and keeps maximum flexibility.

- Stops **Nb5** (which would harass d6 and c7)
- Prepares **…b5**, launching queenside counterplay
- Keeps options: **…e5** (English Attack), **…e6** (Classical Najdorf)
- Used by Fischer in his 1972 World Championship win over Spassky

@plane z=6 label="6. Bg5 — The Main Line"
## 6. Bg5 — Richter-Rauzer / Classical Attack

```
  a b c d e f g h
8 ♜ ♞ ♝ ♛ ♚ ♝ · ♜
7 · ♟ · · ♟ ♟ ♟ ♟
6 ♟ · · ♟ · ♞ · ·
5 · · · · · · ♗ ·
4 · · · ♘ ♙ · · ·
3 · · ♘ · · · · ·
2 ♙ ♙ ♙ · · ♙ ♙ ♙
1 ♖ · ♗ ♕ ♔ · · ♖
  a b c d e f g h
```

**Bg5** — the sharpest reply. White pins the f6-knight immediately, making …e5 awkward and threatening to double Black's f-pawns.

- Pin on f6 cramps Black's coordination
- Black's classic response: **6… e6** (entering the Poisoned Pawn with 7. f4 Qb6?!)
- Alternative: **6. Be3** (English Attack), **6. f3** (English Attack), **6. Bc4** (Fischer's favorite)

> From here, theory runs 25+ moves deep. Every grandmaster has an opinion. Welcome to the Najdorf.

[[z=0|Reset to start]]

3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Sillage Descent
view: elevator
perfumer: Leif
accord: Vetiver Nocturne No. 7
drydown: 6 hours on skin at 37°C
category: Creative
---
A fragrance pyramid you can walk through. Descend from the bright opening to the warm, animal base — each layer as it lives on skin, minute by minute.

@plane z=0 label="Top — 0–15 min"
# First Impression

The cap lifts. A cold green flash, then warmth.

- **Bergamot** *(Calabrian, cold-pressed)* — sharp citrus zest, almost medicinal edge
- **Pink peppercorn** — cracked on white linen, slight metallic bite
- **Cardamom CO₂** — eucalyptol and camphor, gone in eight minutes

> *"The top is the lie you tell. Seductive, brief, entirely intentional."*

Volatility: ████████░░ high  
Tenacity on cotton: 12 min  
Tenacity on skin: 18 min

Blending note: bergamot at **3%** in the final formula — any higher and the aldehydes war with the peppercorn.

@plane z=1 label="Heart Emerging — 15–45 min"
# The Character

The citrus burns off. Something warmer takes the air.

- **Orris butter** *(Florence, 2023 root)* — violet, powdery, faintly carrot
- **Jasmine sambac absolute** — indolic, white-flower-at-night richness
- **Iso E Super** — 2%, the subliminal cedar that no one names but everyone remembers

The heart is where a perfume earns its keep.

| Note | Concentration | Character |
|------|-------------|-----------|
| Orris | 0.8% | powdery anchor |
| Jasmine sambac | 1.2% | indolic warmth |
| Iso E Super | 2.0% | aura, not presence |
| Black tea accord | 0.4% | tannin edge |

→ [[z=2|See the base taking shape beneath this]]

@plane z=2 label="Full Heart — 45 min–2 hr"
# Peak Radiance

The formula blooms. This is the version strangers notice across a room.

**The sillage moment:** jasmine and orris fuse with skin temperature, the Iso E Super expands. The accord is no longer notes — it is a *thing*.

Evaluation checklist:
- [ ] No sharp seams between top residue and heart
- [ ] Orris not reading as laundry
- [ ] Jasmine indoles present but not overripe
- [ ] Projection: 60–80 cm from wrist at arm's length

*Tested on three skin types: oily (amplifies jasmine), dry (shortens peak by 20 min), neutral (reference skin).*

**Formula version:** v7 — increased jasmine from 0.9% to 1.2%, dropped ylang to trace after ylang dominated v6 heart completely.

@plane z=3 label="Drydown — 2–4 hr"
# The Descent

The heart softens. The base rises through it like smoke through gauze.

- **Vetiver** *(Haitian)* — smoky, earthy, slightly rubbery at first
- **Labdanum absolute** — warm resin, fig-skin, animalic without musk
- **Sandalwood** *(Mysore-type, synthetic)* — creamy, soft, the hand underneath everything

The accord is shifting from *perfume you wear* to *scent you leave behind*.

> Spray a strip. Come back in three hours.  
> That's the truth of the formula.

Vetiver to labdanum ratio locked at **1.8 : 1** after ratio testing across five months.

@plane z=4 label="Dry Base — 4–6 hr"
# What Remains

Only the base survives. It has been there the whole time, waiting.

**Vetiver Nocturne No. 7 dry base:**

```
Haitian vetiver           18.0%
Labdanum absolute          10.0%
Ambrette seed (musk)        4.0%
Iso E Super                 2.0%
Sandalwood (Javanol)        8.0%
Benzyl benzoate (fixative)  5.0%
Ethanol (96%) to           100%
```

Wrist strip at hour six: **warm, smoky, skin-close**.  
No green, no citrus, no jasmine. Clean extinction.

Longevity score: **8.5 / 10**  
Sillage score: **6 / 10** (intimate, deliberate)

@plane z=5 label="The Formula Story"
# Why This Accord Works

Vetiver Nocturne No. 7 is built on a **tension principle**: cold (bergamot, peppercorn) against warm (labdanum, vetiver), fleeting (top) against permanent (base).

The jasmine heart is the mediator — it reads as both cold-white and warm-indolic depending on skin chemistry and which layer the wearer is in.

**Three things that almost broke it:**

1. Ylang at 0.5% in v6 — drowned the orris entirely
2. Vetiver at 22% in v3 — base was legible in the top notes, ruined the reveal
3. Real Mysore sandalwood tried in v4 — extraordinary but $2,400/kg, replaced with Javanol

**The name:** *sillage* (French) — the trail a fragrance leaves in the air behind you, the ghost of presence.

*This is a document about a perfume that is also the perfume.*

The Stormcaller tree spends from the surface inward. Tier 1 sits at the
foreground (the skills you reach first), and each deeper plane gates behind the
one above it. Read from `z=0` down to `z=3`; deeper tiers cost more SP and demand
prerequisites from the tier above.

Total to fully clear: 31 SP across four tiers.

@plane z=0 label="Tier 1 - Foundations" x=0 y=0
# Tier 1: Foundations

No prerequisites. Available at level 1. Spend here first.

| Skill        | Cost | Effect                                            |
|--------------|------|---------------------------------------------------|
| Spark        | 1 SP | Bolt for 12 damage, 4m range, no cooldown         |
| Static Field | 2 SP | Standing still builds a charge that shocks on hit |
| Quick Hands  | 1 SP | Cast speed +8%                                     |

Spend a point in Spark to open the path to [[z=1|Tier 2: Conduction]].

> Tip: Static Field pairs with kiting. Stop, soak a charge, then move.

@plane z=1 label="Tier 2 - Conduction" x=2 y=0
# Tier 2: Conduction

Requires 3 SP spent in [[z=0|Tier 1]] and character level 8.

| Skill        | Cost | Prerequisite | Effect                                  |
|--------------|------|--------------|-----------------------------------------|
| Chain Bolt   | 2 SP | Spark        | Lightning arcs to 2 nearby enemies      |
| Grounding    | 2 SP | Static Field | -20% damage taken while channeling      |
| Overload     | 3 SP | Quick Hands  | Crits refund 1 charge of Static Field   |

Chain Bolt is the backbone of every later build. Take it before anything else
here, then descend to [[z=2|Tier 3: Tempest]].

@plane z=2 label="Tier 3 - Tempest" x=4 y=0
# Tier 3: Tempest

Requires 7 SP spent across Tiers 1 and 2, plus Chain Bolt, and level 16.

| Skill         | Cost | Prerequisite | Effect                                       |
|---------------|------|--------------|----------------------------------------------|
| Thunderhead   | 4 SP | Chain Bolt   | Summon a cloud that strikes every 2 seconds  |
| Insulation    | 3 SP | Grounding    | Immune to chain damage from your own bolts   |
| Resonance     | 4 SP | Overload     | Each chain jump adds +15% damage to the next |

Two viable splits from here:

- **Burst:** Thunderhead plus Resonance for stacking arc damage.
- **Sustain:** Thunderhead plus Insulation for safer crowd clears.

The capstone at [[z=3|Tier 4: Apex]] unlocks once Thunderhead is maxed.

@plane z=3 label="Tier 4 - Apex" x=6 y=0
# Tier 4: Apex (Capstone)

Requires Thunderhead maxed, 14 SP spent in the tree, and level 24. Pick exactly
one of the two capstones below. The choice is permanent until a respec.

## Stormlord (offense)

- Cost: 6 SP
- Thunderhead becomes mobile and follows you.
- Resonance stacks no longer reset between targets.
- Builds on [[z=2|Resonance]] for runaway scaling against packs.

## Tempest Warden (defense)

- Cost: 6 SP
- Grounding and Insulation now apply to your whole party within 8m.
- Killing a shocked enemy grants the party a 3-second damage shield.
- Builds on [[z=2|Insulation]] for raid survivability.

Either capstone closes the tree. Respec at any shrine for 500 gold to walk back
up to [[z=0|Tier 1]] and rebuild.

axis: level
title: Sky Citadel Run
view: parallax
legend: .=· #=🟫 ^=🧱 ~=🌊 P=🧍 G=💎 S=🌿 C=🪨 M=⛓️ T=🌲 _=▪️ e=👾 b=🦇 k=🔑 d=🚪 X=💥 @=☁️

A crumbling citadel floats above an endless sea. Three layers: distant sky, midground ruins, and the deadly foreground floor. Navigate right to reach the gate — but the chains move.

@plane z=0 label="Sky — Far Background"

```
@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@
@  @@@    @@@      @@@    @@   @@@@@     @@    @@@   @@@
@       @@      @@      @@         @@        @@      @@@
@@   @@      @@@    @@      @@@         @@@       @@  @@
@       @@@      @@    @@@       @@   @@      @@@    @@@
@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@
```

> The sky never stops breathing. Clouds drift east. You drift right.

@plane z=1 label="Ruins — Midground Hazards"

```
........................................................
....T.......b......b..........b..b..................T...
..T...S.S.S...S.S....S.S.S.S....S.S.S.S.S...S.S.S....
^^^^^...^^^^^...^^^^...^^^^^...^^^^^...^^^^^...^^^^^^^
```

**Bats** patrol the ruin arches. **Vines** (S) slow your jump. **Stone battlements** (^) are solid platforms.

- 🦇 **Bat** — swoops in a figure-eight. Duck under on the return stroke.
- 🌿 **Vine** — climbable but cuts horizontal speed by half.
- 🧱 **Battlement** — land on top; hitting the side bounces you back.

@plane z=2 label="Citadel Floor — Main Play Layer"

```
.......................................................
....G..........k.....G.............................G..
.P.....C.....M...M.....C.C.....M...M.M.....C......e..
###^##...##^###...##^####...##^##...##^##...##^#####^
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```

**You** (P) start left. Reach the door (d) on the far right.

| Symbol | Meaning | Tip |
|--------|---------|-----|
| 💎 G | Gem — 100 pts each | Safe to skip |
| 🔑 k | Key — required | Grab before the door |
| ⛓️ M | Swinging chain | Time your jump to the arc |
| 🪨 C | Crumble block | Falls 1 s after first step |
| 👾 e | Guard — patrols segment | Stomp or dodge under |
| 🌊 ~ | Sea — instant death | Never touch |

> **Pro move:** ride a crumble block (C) leftward off its edge — it gives you a free half-tile of extra height on the next jump.

@plane z=3 label="Secret Ceiling Layer"

```
_________________________________________________
__G_____G__________G________G___________G_______
_________________________________________________
^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
```

Hidden above the main floor: a ceiling run unlocked after collecting the 🔑 key. Wall-jump the right edge of any battlement (^) to reach it.

- Three bonus 💎 gems spaced across the ceiling path
- No enemies — pure momentum puzzle
- Rejoins the main layer at the door platform

> **Secret:** the ceiling path skips the final chain (M) entirely. Worth it if you're going for S-rank time.

@plane z=4 label="Boss Vestibule — The Gate"

```
..................................................
......................................G...G...G...
..............................................d...
##################^###########^##########^#######
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```

The door (d) requires the key. Three final gems guard the approach — each one sits over a crumble block above the sea.

**Gate conditions:**
- [ ] Key collected from z=2
- [ ] Guard (e) defeated or bypassed
- [ ] Chains cleared without touching the sea

```
LEVEL CLEAR conditions
──────────────────────
Base time target : 90 s
S-rank           : 55 s  (ceiling route required)
All gems         : 8 total across all planes
No-damage bonus  : +500 pts
```

> *The gate grinds open. Below, the sea is very far down. Ahead: World 5.*

3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Solar System by Depth
view: elevator
unit: AU
scale: logarithmic
source: JPL Horizons, IAU 2006
category: Information
---
Descend outward from the Sun. Each plane is a world — real distances, real textures.

@plane z=0 label="☀ Sun · 0 AU"
# The Sun

**Diameter:** 1,392,700 km — 109 Earths side by side  
**Surface temp:** 5,778 K (photosphere) · 15,000,000 K (core)  
**Composition:** 74% H, 24% He, 2% heavier elements  
**Rotation:** 25 days at equator, 36 days at poles — differential rotation

> The Sun contains **99.86%** of all mass in the solar system. Everything else is rounding error.

The solar wind streams outward at 400–800 km/s, carving the heliosphere — a bubble 120 AU across that shields us from interstellar cosmic rays.

**Light travel time from here:** 0 seconds, obviously.

@plane z=1 label="☿ Mercury · 0.39 AU"
# Mercury · 0.39 AU

**Diameter:** 4,879 km  
**Orbital period:** 87.97 Earth days  
**Day length:** 58.65 Earth days — 3:2 spin-orbit resonance  
**Surface temp:** −180 °C (night) to +430 °C (day)

Mercury has almost **no atmosphere** — exospheric pressure ~10⁻¹⁵ bar. Without a blanket, heat escapes instantly into space each night.

Its enormous iron core is ~85% of its radius — proportionally the largest in the solar system. One hypothesis: a giant impact stripped the mantle early on.

**Light travel time from Sun:** 3 min 13 sec at perihelion

- [ ] MESSENGER orbited 2011–2015, mapping 100% of surface
- [ ] BepiColombo en route, arrival 2025

@plane z=2 label="♀ Venus · 0.72 AU"
# Venus · 0.72 AU

**Diameter:** 12,104 km  
**Orbital period:** 224.7 days  
**Day length:** 243 Earth days — *longer than its year*  
**Surface temp:** 465 °C (mean) — hotter than Mercury

Venus rotates **retrograde** — the Sun rises in the west. No one knows why for certain. A massive ancient impact is the leading candidate.

The atmosphere is 96.5% CO₂ at 92 bar — the pressure of 900 m underwater on Earth. Lead would melt on the surface.

> Clouds of **sulfuric acid** float at 45–70 km altitude, permanently hiding the surface from visible light. Radar sees through them.

**Light travel time from Sun:** 6 min at mean distance  
**Closest approach to Earth:** 38.2 million km — our nearest planetary neighbor

@plane z=3 label="♁ Earth · 1.00 AU"
# Earth · 1.00 AU

**Diameter:** 12,742 km  
**Orbital period:** 365.25 days  
**Axial tilt:** 23.44° (gives us seasons)  
**Surface temp:** −88 °C to +58 °C (mean: +15 °C)

The **1 AU** standard: 149,597,870.7 km, defined by the distance light travels in 499.0 seconds.

One moon. Unusually large — mass ratio 1:81 vs Earth. The Moon stabilizes Earth's axial tilt to within ~2.4°, making our climate predictable over geological time. Mars, with two tiny moons, wobbles 0–60° over millions of years.

**Water:** 71% of surface. Liquid water requires the right pressure *and* temperature — only possible in the habitable zone. We're in it by ~500 km margin on the inner edge.

- **Magnetic field:** 25–65 µT, generated by a liquid iron outer core
- **Atmosphere:** 78% N₂, 21% O₂, 1% Ar — the O₂ is entirely biological

@plane z=4 label="♂ Mars · 1.52 AU"
# Mars · 1.52 AU

**Diameter:** 6,779 km — half of Earth's  
**Orbital period:** 686.97 days  
**Axial tilt:** 25.19° — nearly Earth-like  
**Surface temp:** −153 °C to +20 °C (mean: −63 °C)

Olympus Mons rises **21.9 km** above the mean datum — the tallest volcano in the solar system. Valles Marineris stretches 4,000 km, 7 km deep — the Grand Canyon is a crack in a sidewalk by comparison.

Mars lost its magnetic field ~4 billion years ago. Without it, the solar wind stripped the atmosphere. Today: 0.6% of Earth's sea-level pressure, 95.3% CO₂.

> **Did Mars have liquid water?** Yes — river deltas, lake beds, and hydrated minerals confirm it persisted for ~1 billion years early in Martian history.

**Moons:** Phobos (27 km) and Deimos (15 km) — captured asteroids, or debris from a giant impact. Phobos orbits in 7 h 39 min — faster than Mars rotates. It will collide or break apart in ~50 million years.

@plane z=5 label="⚶ Asteroid Belt · 2.2–3.2 AU"
# Asteroid Belt · 2.2–3.2 AU

**Total mass:** ~4% of the Moon — less than you'd think  
**Largest body:** Ceres, 940 km, a dwarf planet  
**Object count:** ~1.1–1.9 million bodies > 1 km

The belt is **not dense**. Every spacecraft that has flown through it passed without incident. Average spacing between kilometer-class rocks: ~1 million km.

Jupiter's gravity prevents this material from accreting into a planet — orbital resonances (Kirkwood gaps at 2.50, 2.82, 2.95 AU) eject asteroids into planet-crossing orbits.

**Composition gradient:**
- Inner belt: silicate S-type (rocky, dry)
- Outer belt: carbonaceous C-type (dark, water-rich, primordial)

> C-type asteroids are chemically similar to the Sun minus volatiles — nearly unchanged since 4.56 billion years ago.

Ceres has water ice beneath its surface and **bright salt deposits** (sodium carbonates) in Occator Crater — remnants of an ancient briny ocean.

@plane z=6 label="♃ Jupiter · 5.20 AU"
# Jupiter · 5.20 AU

**Diameter:** 139,820 km — 11 Earths wide  
**Mass:** 318 × Earth — more than all other planets combined × 2  
**Orbital period:** 11.86 years  
**Day length:** 9 h 56 min — fastest rotation in the solar system

The **Great Red Spot** is a storm 1.3× Earth's diameter that has persisted for at least 350 years. It has been shrinking since the 1800s; current size: ~16,000 km wide.

Jupiter is a **failed star** in the sense that it is mostly H and He, but it would need ~80× more mass to ignite hydrogen fusion. It does radiate 1.67× more heat than it receives from the Sun — residual heat from gravitational contraction.

**Moons:** 95 known. The Galilean four are worlds in their own right:
- **Io:** most volcanically active body in the solar system (tidal heating)
- **Europa:** 10–30 km ice shell over a liquid water ocean — prime astrobiology target
- **Ganymede:** larger than Mercury, has its own magnetosphere
- **Callisto:** ancient, heavily cratered, barely geologically active

**Light travel time from Sun:** 43 min 16 sec

@plane z=7 label="♄ Saturn · 9.58 AU"
# Saturn · 9.58 AU

**Diameter:** 116,460 km (equatorial) — oblate due to fast spin  
**Bulk density:** 0.687 g/cm³ — less than water  
**Orbital period:** 29.46 years  
**Day length:** 10 h 33 min

The rings span **282,000 km** in diameter but are on average only **~10 meters thick** — the thinnest structure relative to its width of any object known. If scaled to a sheet of paper (0.1 mm), the rings would be 2.8 km wide.

Ring material is **93% water ice**, with trace silicates. The rings are geologically young — likely 10–100 million years old, formed when a moon wandered too close and broke apart.

> Cassini (1997–2017) revealed that ring material rains onto Saturn at ~10,000 kg/sec. The rings may be gone in 100 million years.

**Titan:** 5,150 km diameter. The only moon with a dense atmosphere (1.5 bar, mostly N₂). Lakes and rivers of liquid methane and ethane pool near the poles. Surface pressure and temperature mimic early Earth — in a deep-freeze analog.

**Light travel time from Sun:** 1 h 20 min at mean distance

---
3md: 1.0
axis: space
title: The Solar System by Distance
subtitle: Eight planets, ordered outward from the Sun
units: astronomical units (1 AU is the Earth-Sun distance)
author: CorvidLabs Gallery
---
A walk outward from the Sun. Each plane sits at the planet's mean orbital
distance, mapped so that `z` equals the distance in astronomical units (AU).
The inner rocky worlds are packed close together near `z=1`, while the outer
gas and ice giants spread out across tens of AU. Start at [[z=0.39|Mercury]]
and travel to the edge at [[z=30.07|Neptune]].

@plane z=0.39 x=0 y=0 label="Mercury"
# Mercury (0.39 AU)

The smallest planet and the closest to the Sun. Mercury races around its orbit
in just 88 Earth days, the fastest year in the system.

- **Type:** rocky (terrestrial)
- **Day length:** 59 Earth days (a single rotation)
- **Surface:** scorched by day, frozen by night, swinging from +430 C to -180 C
- **Moons:** none

No atmosphere worth the name means no blanket to trap heat, so the temperature
swing between the sunlit and shadowed sides is the most extreme of any planet.

Next stop outward: [[z=0.72|Venus]].

@plane z=0.72 x=0.33 y=0 label="Venus"
# Venus (0.72 AU)

Earth's near-twin in size, and the hottest planet in the system. A thick
carbon-dioxide atmosphere traps heat so efficiently that the surface sits at
about 465 C, hotter than Mercury despite being farther from the Sun.

- **Type:** rocky (terrestrial)
- **Atmosphere:** 96 percent CO2, surface pressure about 92 times Earth's
- **Spin:** retrograde (the Sun rises in the west) and very slow
- **Moons:** none

A day on Venus (243 Earth days) is longer than its year (225 Earth days).

Inward to [[z=0.39|Mercury]], or onward to [[z=1.0|Earth]].

@plane z=1.0 x=0.66 y=0 label="Earth"
# Earth (1.00 AU)

The reference point. One AU is defined by Earth's average distance from the Sun,
about 150 million kilometers, which is why this plane sits at exactly `z=1`.

- **Type:** rocky (terrestrial)
- **Atmosphere:** 78 percent nitrogen, 21 percent oxygen
- **Surface:** the only known world with stable liquid water and life
- **Moons:** 1 (the Moon, at roughly 384,000 km)

Sunlight takes about 8 minutes and 20 seconds to reach here.

The next world out is the red one: [[z=1.52|Mars]].

@plane z=1.52 x=1.0 y=0 label="Mars"
# Mars (1.52 AU)

The red planet, colored by iron oxide dust. Mars is half Earth's diameter and
hosts the tallest volcano in the system, Olympus Mons, rising about 22 km.

- **Type:** rocky (terrestrial)
- **Day length:** 24 hours 37 minutes (close to Earth's)
- **Atmosphere:** thin CO2, about 0.6 percent of Earth's surface pressure
- **Moons:** 2 (Phobos and Deimos, both small and lumpy)

Beyond Mars lies the asteroid belt, the boundary between the rocky inner system
and the giants. Cross it to reach [[z=5.2|Jupiter]].

@plane z=5.2 x=2.5 y=0 label="Jupiter"
# Jupiter (5.20 AU)

The giant. Jupiter holds more than twice the mass of every other planet
combined, and its Great Red Spot is a storm wide enough to swallow Earth.

- **Type:** gas giant
- **Day length:** under 10 hours (the fastest spin in the system)
- **Composition:** mostly hydrogen and helium, no solid surface
- **Moons:** 95 confirmed, including the four large Galilean moons (Io, Europa,
  Ganymede, Callisto)

Note the jump in scale: Jupiter sits more than three times farther out than
Mars, and the spacing only grows from here.

Onward to the ringed world: [[z=9.58|Saturn]].

@plane z=9.58 x=4.5 y=0 label="Saturn"
# Saturn (9.58 AU)

Famous for its rings, a vast disk of ice and rock chunks only tens of meters
thick but spanning hundreds of thousands of kilometers. Saturn is the least
dense planet, lighter than water on average.

- **Type:** gas giant
- **Day length:** about 10.7 hours
- **Rings:** seven main groups, mostly water ice
- **Moons:** 146 confirmed, including Titan (thicker atmosphere than Earth's)

From here the system turns cold and blue. Continue to [[z=19.22|Uranus]].

@plane z=19.22 x=8 y=0 label="Uranus"
# Uranus (19.22 AU)

The tilted ice giant. Uranus rotates almost on its side, tipped about 98
degrees, so each pole spends roughly 42 years in sunlight and 42 in darkness.

- **Type:** ice giant
- **Color:** pale cyan from methane in the upper atmosphere
- **Day length:** about 17 hours, retrograde
- **Moons:** 28 confirmed, named for characters from Shakespeare and Pope

Nearly twice as far out as Saturn. One step remains to the edge: [[z=30.07|Neptune]].

@plane z=30.07 x=12 y=0 label="Neptune"
# Neptune (30.07 AU)

The outermost planet, a deep-blue ice giant with the fastest winds in the
system, reaching about 2,000 km/h. Sunlight here is roughly 900 times fainter
than on Earth, and a single Neptune year lasts about 165 Earth years.

- **Type:** ice giant
- **Discovery:** found in 1846 by mathematical prediction before it was seen
- **Day length:** about 16 hours
- **Moons:** 16 confirmed, including Triton, which orbits backward

This is the end of the planetary road. Beyond lies the Kuiper Belt, home to
Pluto and other dwarf planets.

Jump back to the start at [[z=1.0|Earth]] or all the way in to [[z=0.39|Mercury]].

3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: "Midnight Driver - Stem Breakdown"
artist: The Sodium Lamps
bpm: 102
key: A minor
time_signature: 4/4
sample_rate: 48 kHz
session_length: 3:48
mix_engineer: R. Okafor
---
"Midnight Driver" is a 102 BPM track in A minor, printed here as separate
instrument stems instead of a single flat mix. Each plane is one stem: solo it
to study a part, stack several to rebuild the arrangement. The planes are ordered
bottom to top the way an engineer thinks about a mix, from the low-end foundation
up to the vocal that sits on top.

A few shared facts hold across every stem: the tempo is steady at 102 BPM (no
click drift), the song stays in A minor throughout, and all stems are exported
at 48 kHz, 24-bit, peak-normalized to -1 dBFS with no master-bus processing so
they sum cleanly. To hear the rhythmic core on its own, start with the
[[z=0|Drums]]; to hear what everything is built to support, jump to the
[[z=4|Lead Vocal]].

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Drums"
# Drums (the floor)

The rhythmic foundation. Recorded as a full kit, then bounced to one stem with
the room mics folded in so the groove keeps its natural ambience.

- **Pattern:** straight backbeat, kick on 1 and 3, snare on 2 and 4
- **Hi-hats:** eighth notes in the verse, opening to quarters in the chorus
- **Fills:** two-bar crash fill into each chorus, a longer tom run into the bridge
- **Processing baked in:** light bus compression (4:1, slow attack) for glue

The kick is tuned low and short so it leaves room underneath. Everything else in
the song is timed against this stem, so it is the natural reference when lining up
parts. The next thing to lock to the kick is the [[z=1|Bass]].

@plane z=1 x=0 y=-1 label="Bass"
# Bass (locks to the kick)

A fingered electric bass that doubles the kick pattern and outlines the chord
roots. This is the bridge between rhythm and harmony.

- **Tone:** rounded, slight high-mid bump so it reads on small speakers
- **Note choice:** roots on the downbeats, walking eighths into chord changes
- **Root motion (verse):** Am - F - C - G, two bars each
- **Performance note:** muted ghost notes on the "and" of beat 2 to push the groove

Solo the drums and bass together and you already have the whole rhythm section.
On top of that foundation we stack the harmony, played by the
[[z=2|Rhythm Guitars and Keys]].

@plane z=2 x=-1 y=1 label="Harmony"
# Rhythm Guitars and Keys (the harmonic bed)

The chord layer that fills the middle of the spectrum. Two parts share this stem:
a clean electric guitar panned left and an electric piano panned right, so the
harmony spreads across the stereo field.

- **Guitar:** muted eighth-note strums, capo at the 2nd fret, panned 40 left
- **Electric piano:** sustained chords with a slow tremolo, panned 35 right
- **Chords (chorus):** F - C - G - Am, brighter voicings than the verse
- **Arrangement trick:** the piano drops out in verse 2 to make space, then
  returns for the final chorus to lift the energy

This bed is deliberately quiet under the vocal and louder in the instrumental
sections. It leans on the [[z=1|Bass]] for its low notes and clears the high end
for the [[z=4|Lead Vocal]]. The hooks on top of it come from the
[[z=3|Lead Instruments]].

@plane z=3 x=1 y=1 label="Leads"
# Lead Instruments (the hooks)

The melodic counter-lines that answer the vocal: a saturated lead guitar and a
short synth motif. They mostly play in the gaps between vocal phrases.

- **Lead guitar:** the chorus riff, a four-note hook on the high E and B strings
- **Synth:** a plucky arpeggio in the intro and outro, doubled an octave up
- **Solo:** sixteen bars over the bridge, lead guitar with a long delay tail
- **Mix placement:** centered but pushed back with reverb so it sits behind vocals

When the [[z=4|Lead Vocal]] rests, this stem steps forward; when the vocal sings,
it tucks back. Mute it and the song still works but loses its signature riff.

@plane z=4 x=0 y=2 label="Lead Vocal"
# Lead Vocal (the top)

The focal point. A single performer, double-tracked only in the choruses for
width, sitting on top of everything else in the mix.

- **Range:** roughly A3 to E5, comfortable chest voice with a few falsetto lifts
- **Verses:** single take, dry and close, intimate
- **Choruses:** double-tracked and panned slightly wide, with a harmony a third up
- **Effects baked in:** plate reverb and a slap delay on phrase endings only

This is the part every other stem is arranged to support: the [[z=0|Drums]] set
the pace, the [[z=1|Bass]] and [[z=2|Rhythm Guitars and Keys]] hold the harmony,
and the [[z=3|Lead Instruments]] fill the spaces around the words. Solo this stem
to study phrasing; unmute the rest to hear it back in context.

3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: 3-Day Sourdough: Stage by Stage
view: present
slug: sourdough-three-day-rise
category: Home
---

A living timeline of your loaf — from flour and water to a crackling crust. Each plane is one stage. Tap through when you're ready to move on.

@plane z=0 label="Day 1 · Feed the Starter"

# Feed the Starter

**Time:** Evening, Day 1 — about 5 minutes of work

Your starter has been sleeping in the fridge. Wake it up 12–24 hours before baking.

## What you need
- 50 g active starter (discard the rest)
- 50 g whole wheat flour
- 50 g bread flour
- 100 g room-temperature water (78°F / 26°C)

## Steps
- [ ] Discard all but 50 g of starter
- [ ] Whisk in flours and water until no dry clumps remain
- [ ] Cover loosely and leave at room temperature (68–72°F)
- [ ] Mark the jar with a rubber band so you can track the rise

## How to know it's ready
> Your starter should **double in size** and show a domed top with bubbles throughout — usually 8–12 hours. A ripe starter smells tangy and faintly fruity, not acetone-sharp.

Proceed to **[[z=1|Day 2 Morning: Autolyse & Mix]]** when your starter passes the float test: drop a small spoonful in water — if it floats, it's ready.

---
*Hydration so far: starter only*

@plane z=1 label="Day 2 · Autolyse & Mix"

# Autolyse & Mix

**Time:** Morning, Day 2 — 30 minutes hands-on + 1 hour rest

## Autolyse (1 hour before mixing)
- [ ] Combine **450 g bread flour** + **50 g whole wheat flour** in a large bowl
- [ ] Pour in **360 g warm water (90°F)** and mix until no dry flour remains
- [ ] Cover and rest **45–60 minutes** — gluten forms without kneading

## Mix the Dough
- [ ] Add **100 g ripe starter** and **10 g fine sea salt** dissolved in 15 g warm water
- [ ] Dimple the salt water into the dough with your fingers
- [ ] Squeeze and fold until fully incorporated (3–5 minutes)
- [ ] Dough will feel sticky and slightly shaggy — this is correct

## Bulk Fermentation Begins
Transfer to a straight-sided container. Mark the level.

**Target:** dough increases **50–75%** over the next 4–5 hours at 76°F.

| Hour | Action |
|------|--------|
| 0:30 | Stretch & fold set 1 |
| 1:00 | Stretch & fold set 2 |
| 1:30 | Stretch & fold set 3 |
| 2:00 | Stretch & fold set 4 |
| 2:00–5:00 | Undisturbed rest |

> **Stretch & fold:** Wet your hand, grab one edge of the dough, stretch it up as high as it goes without tearing, then fold it over the center. Rotate the bowl 90° and repeat 4 times total.

Continue to **[[z=2|Day 2 Afternoon: Shape & Cold Proof]]** when the dough is puffy, jiggly, and has visible bubbles on the sides.

@plane z=2 label="Day 2 · Shape & Cold Proof"

# Shape & Cold Proof

**Time:** Afternoon, Day 2 — 20 minutes hands-on, then overnight

## Pre-shape
- [ ] Lightly flour your work surface
- [ ] Gently turn the dough out — do not deflate it
- [ ] Using a bench scraper, fold the edges toward the center and flip seam-side down
- [ ] Let it rest uncovered **20–30 minutes** (bench rest)

## Final Shape — Batard (oval)
- [ ] Lightly flour the top of the dough
- [ ] Flip it over so the floured side faces down
- [ ] Fold the bottom third up, the sides in, then roll tightly toward you
- [ ] Use the bench scraper to drag the loaf toward you to build tension
- [ ] The surface should feel taut, like a drum

## Cold Proof
- [ ] Line a banneton or bowl with a well-floured linen cloth
- [ ] Place the loaf **seam-side up** in the banneton
- [ ] Pinch the seam lightly to close
- [ ] Cover with a shower cap or plastic wrap
- [ ] Refrigerate **12–16 hours** (or up to 36 hours for deeper flavor)

> Cold fermentation slows yeast activity and lets flavor-producing bacteria catch up. The loaf will barely grow in the fridge — that's fine.

Your dough is patient now. Rest. **[[z=3|Day 3 Morning: Bake]]** is the payoff.

@plane z=3 label="Day 3 · Bake"

# Bake

**Time:** Morning, Day 3 — 1 hour (mostly oven time)

## Preheat — Do This First
- [ ] Place your Dutch oven (lid on) in the oven
- [ ] Set oven to **500°F / 260°C**
- [ ] Preheat **at least 1 hour** — the cast iron must be scorching

## Score & Load
- [ ] Cut a sheet of parchment paper to fit your Dutch oven
- [ ] Turn the cold dough out onto the parchment (seam-side down now)
- [ ] Dust lightly with rice flour for contrast
- [ ] Score swiftly with a lame or sharp razor at **30–45°** angle, one confident slash
- [ ] Lower dough + parchment into the screaming-hot Dutch oven using the paper as a sling
- [ ] Put the lid on immediately

## Bake Schedule

| Time | Temp | Lid | What's Happening |
|------|------|-----|-----------------|
| 0–20 min | 500°F | On | Steam trapped inside — rapid oven spring |
| 20–25 min | 450°F | Off | Crust sets and colors |
| 25–40 min | 450°F | Off | Deep color, crust crisps, moisture escapes |

- [ ] Remove lid at 20 minutes and lower temp to 450°F
- [ ] Bake until crust is **deep mahogany** — not golden, not tan: *dark*
- [ ] Internal temp should read **205–210°F / 96–99°C**

## Cool — The Hardest Part
- [ ] Transfer to a wire rack immediately
- [ ] **Wait at least 1 hour before cutting** (the crumb is still setting inside)

> The crack and sing of the crust as it cools is your reward. Slice open to find an open, glossy crumb — mild tang from the cold proof, caramelized crust from the heat.

**[[z=4|Notes & Next Time]]** to record what worked.

@plane z=4 label="Notes & Next Time"

# Notes & Next Time

**Date baked:** _______________

## Observations

**Starter health**
- Float test passed at hour: ___
- Smell: ○ Fruity  ○ Tangy  ○ Acetone  ○ Flat

**Bulk fermentation**
- Room temp: ___°F
- Time to 75% rise: ___ hours
- Dough feel at end: ○ Jiggly  ○ Tight  ○ Overproofed

**Crust & Crumb**
- Crust color: ○ Pale  ○ Golden  ○ Dark  ○ Very dark
- Crumb: ○ Tight  ○ Medium  ○ Open  ○ Gummy
- Ear lifted: ○ Yes  ○ No

**Flavor**
- Sourness: ○ Mild  ○ Medium  ○ Sharp
- Overall: ___/10

## Adjustments for Next Bake

| Variable | This Bake | Try Next Time |
|----------|-----------|---------------|
| Hydration | 75% | |
| Bulk time | | |
| Cold proof | | |
| Bake time | | |

> Sourdough is a relationship. Each loaf teaches you something the last one couldn't. Write it down here while the crust is still singing.

3md: 1.0
axis: side
title: Ser vs. Estar: The One That Always Trips You Up
view: single
deck: Spanish Flashcards
level: A2–B1
author: Leif
category: Education
---
Flip through 6 cards. Each card has a **front** (z=even) and a **back** (z=odd). Try to answer before you flip. ([[z=1|→ flip card 1]])

@plane z=0 label="Card 1 — Front"
## ¿Cómo **___ ** tú hoy?

*Fill in the blank: ser or estar?*

---

> Hint: think about whether "today" makes it temporary or permanent.

[[z=1|→ flip to see answer]]

@plane z=1 label="Card 1 — Back"
## ¿Cómo **estás** tú hoy?

✅ **estar** — because mood and health are *temporary states*

---

**The rule:** ESTAR covers **H**ow you feel, **A**ppearance right now, **L**ocation, **T**emporary conditions — remember **HALT**.

> *"Estoy cansado."* — I'm tired (right now, not forever)
> *"Estoy en Madrid."* — I'm in Madrid (location)

[[z=2|→ next card]]

@plane z=2 label="Card 2 — Front"
## Ella **___ ** médica.

*Fill in the blank: ser or estar?*

---

> Hint: is being a doctor a temporary condition or a defining identity?

[[z=3|→ flip to see answer]]

@plane z=3 label="Card 2 — Back"
## Ella **es** médica.

✅ **ser** — profession is a *defining characteristic*

---

**The rule:** SER covers **D**escriptions, **O**rigin, **C**haracteristics, **T**ime, **O**ccupation, **R**elationships — remember **DOCTOR**.

> *"Soy de Seattle."* — I'm from Seattle (origin)
> *"Son las tres."* — It's three o'clock (time)

[[z=4|→ next card]]

@plane z=4 label="Card 3 — Front"
## La puerta **___ ** abierta.

*Fill in the blank: ser or estar?*

---

> Hint: *abierta* (open) — is this a characteristic of the door, or its current condition?

[[z=5|→ flip to see answer]]

@plane z=5 label="Card 3 — Back"
## La puerta **está** abierta.

✅ **estar** — the door is *currently open*, not permanently open by nature

---

**The trap:** Many past participles change meaning with ser vs. estar:

| | ser | estar |
|---|---|---|
| aburrido | boring (personality) | bored (right now) |
| listo | clever | ready |
| malo | evil | sick |
| rico | wealthy | delicious |

> *"El café **es** rico."* — Coffee is delicious (characteristic)
> *"Estoy listo."* — I'm ready (state)

[[z=6|→ next card]]

@plane z=6 label="Card 4 — Front"
## La boda **___ ** en la iglesia.

*Fill in the blank: ser or estar?*

---

> Hint: events have locations — but which verb do you use for an *event's* location vs. a *thing's* location?

[[z=7|→ flip to see answer]]

@plane z=7 label="Card 4 — Back"
## La boda **es** en la iglesia.

✅ **ser** — events use *ser* for location, not estar!

---

**The exception that breaks your brain:**

- *Things and people* use **estar** for location → *El libro **está** en la mesa.*
- *Events* use **ser** for location → *La fiesta **es** aquí.*

> Think of it this way: events *happen at* a place (defining attribute), while objects are merely *found at* a place (temporary).

[[z=8|→ next card]]

@plane z=8 label="Card 5 — Front"
## El cielo **___ ** azul.

*Fill in the blank: ser or estar?*

---

> Hint: is the sky being blue a permanent truth or today's weather?

[[z=9|→ flip to see answer]]

@plane z=9 label="Card 5 — Back"
## El cielo **es** azul.

✅ **ser** — the sky is *inherently, characteristically* blue

---

**But wait** — context can flip it:

> *"Hoy el cielo **está** muy azul."*
> Today the sky is very blue. (unusually vivid — a temporary observation)

This is the advanced nuance: both can be grammatically valid, but they express **different meanings**. Ser = inherent truth. Estar = the speaker's observation in this moment.

[[z=10|→ final card]]

@plane z=10 label="Card 6 — Front"
## Translate: *"She used to be happy, but now she's sad."*

*Use the correct form of ser or estar for each clause.*

---

> Hint: happiness — is it a personality trait or a temporary state? Does your answer change the meaning?

[[z=11|→ flip to see answer]]

@plane z=11 label="Card 6 — Back"
## Two valid answers, two meanings:

**"Ella **era** feliz, pero ahora **está** triste."**
→ She *used to be* a happy person by nature, but now she's (temporarily) sad.

**"Ella **estaba** feliz, pero ahora **está** triste."**
→ She was feeling happy earlier, but now she's feeling sad.

---

> The first sentence implies a *personality change* — something significant happened.
> The second is just mood shifting through the day.

**You've finished the deck.** 🎉

- [[z=0|↩ start over]] from card 1
- Or grab a notebook: write 3 sentences today using *estar* for a temporary feeling, and check yourself tomorrow.

3md: 1.0
axis: frame
legend: o=●
title: ASCII Jump Sprite Sequence
fps: 12
loop: false
direction: rightward
gravity: 9.8 (stylized)
artist: CorvidLabs
---
A five-frame character jump drawn in monospace ASCII, the kind you would wire to
the jump button in a side-scrolling platformer. Play the frames in order
(0, 1, 2, 3, 4), holding [[z=2|the apex]] for a beat or two of hang time, then
either snap back to [[z=0|the idle stance]] on landing or chain into a run.

Each plane is one drawn cel plus the engine notes (vertical velocity, collision
box, and which input or trigger advances to the next frame). Render at 12 fps for
a snappy arcade feel; stretch frames 1 and 3 by a frame each for a floatier,
moon-gravity jump. The `x`/`y` offsets trace the arc of the body's center so a
spatial viewer can lay the cels out along the jump path.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Crouch / Anticipation"
# Frame 0 - Crouch (Anticipation)

The jump button is pressed and the character coils down for one frame before
leaving the ground. This squash is the windup that sells the launch: the more
the knees bend here, the higher the jump reads.

```
   ___
  (o.o)
  /[ ]\
 _|   |_
```

- Vertical velocity: 0 (still grounded)
- Pose: knees bent, arms drawn back, weight low over the feet
- Collision box: full width, short height (crouched)
- Advance on: jump input held for 1 frame, then release into [[z=1|the launch]]

Tip: hold this frame an extra tick if the player taps jump while moving fast, so
a running jump still gets its windup.

@plane z=1 x=3 y=-4 label="Launch / Push-off"
# Frame 1 - Launch (Push-off)

Legs snap straight and the body stretches upward as the feet leave the floor.
This is the fastest-moving frame, so a slight vertical smear or a single-pixel
trail sells the speed. Velocity is at its peak and positive (rising).

```
  \o/
   |
  / \
   ' '
```

- Vertical velocity: maximum upward (initial jump impulse)
- Pose: arms thrown up, legs extending, toes pointing down
- Collision box: tall and narrow (stretched)
- Advance on: one frame elapsed, gravity begins to subtract from velocity

Coming up off the floor leads into the slowing rise of [[z=2|the apex]].

@plane z=2 x=6 y=-7 label="Apex / Hang Time"
# Frame 2 - Apex (Hang Time)

The top of the arc. Vertical velocity has decayed to roughly zero, so this is the
frame to hold for hang time. Tucking the knees here reads as control and gives
the player a clear "now I am floating" beat before the fall.

```
   o
  /|\
  / \
```

- Vertical velocity: near 0 (rising has stopped, falling has not begun)
- Pose: knees tucked, arms out for balance, body compact at the peak
- Collision box: compact (tucked)
- Advance on: hold 1 to 3 frames, then let gravity pull into [[z=3|the fall]]

This is the only frame you should ever pause on; everything before it is
[[z=1|the rise]] and everything after is the drop.

@plane z=3 x=9 y=-4 label="Fall / Descent"
# Frame 3 - Fall (Descent)

Gravity wins and the character drops. The body unfolds and the legs reach down,
anticipating the ground. Velocity is now negative and growing as the fall
accelerates, the mirror of the launch frame.

```
  \ /
   |
  /o\
  | |
```

- Vertical velocity: increasing downward (accelerating fall)
- Pose: arms lifting from the drop, legs extending toward the floor
- Collision box: tall again, feet leading the descent
- Advance on: collision with ground detected, snap to [[z=4|the landing]]

If the player holds a direction here, carry horizontal momentum so the landing
lands forward of [[z=0|the original crouch]].

@plane z=4 x=12 y=0 label="Land / Recovery"
# Frame 4 - Land (Recovery)

The feet hit and the body absorbs the impact with a deep squash, the heaviest
pose of the sequence. A small dust puff or a one-frame screen nudge here makes
the landing feel weighty. After this recovery the character either eases back to
neutral or buffers straight into a run.

```
  ___
 (-.-)
 /[ ]\
=|   |=
```

- Vertical velocity: 0 (grounded, impact absorbed)
- Pose: deep crouch, arms low, dust kicking out at the feet
- Collision box: widest and shortest of the sequence (impact squash)
- Advance on: 1 to 2 recovery frames, then return to [[z=0|the idle crouch]]

Loop note: this jump does not auto-repeat. Recovery resolves to idle and waits
for the next jump input, so playback ends here unless the player presses again.

3md: 1.0
axis: space
title: A Doll's House, Act III - Stage Set Design
production: Henrik Ibsen, revival
venue: Proscenium house, 38-foot opening
designer: scenic department
scale: 1/2 inch = 1 foot
---
A depth-staged scenic plan for the Helmer drawing room. Each plane is a slice of the
set measured upstage from the plaster line (the front edge of the playing space).
The `z` value is feet of depth: z=0 sits at the apron, larger `z` recedes away from
the audience. Read it front to back to walk the eye from the orchestra rail to the
painted backing behind the rear window.

@plane z=0 label="Apron and Footlights" y=-2
# Apron (z = 0 ft, plaster line)

The thrust of bare stage between the audience and the proscenium arch. Nothing is
built here; it is the actor's closest approach to the house.

- **Floor:** scrubbed pine planking, stained warm honey, raked 1:24 toward the seats
- **Footlight trough:** a row of shielded warm-white LEDs, dimmed to 15 percent for
  the final confrontation
- **Sightline note:** anyone standing forward of this line is lit hard from below;
  keep blocking upstage of the arch except for Nora's exit

The whole room beyond reads as a box framed by the arch. The set proper begins at
[[z=4|the parlor furniture]] one stride upstage.

@plane z=4 label="Parlor Furniture" x=-3
# Downstage parlor (z = 4 ft)

The intimate playing area, where most of Act III is staged. Furniture is real and
load-bearing because actors sit, lean, and slam things.

| Piece | Position | Note |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Round table | stage left of center | the letter is read here |
| Two armchairs | flanking the table | upholstered horsehair, period-correct |
| Small sofa | stage right | Nora's "doll" corner |

- The stove is practical: a flicker-bulb behind mica glass throws orange onto the
  rug when the room dims.
- A porcelain lamp on the table is the warm key light for the tarantella aftermath.

Sightlines from the side seats are tight here; nothing taller than 30 inches lives
between the sofa and [[z=8|the rear wall]].

@plane z=8 label="Rear Wall and Doors" x=0 y=1
# Rear wall (z = 8 ft)

The architectural backbone of the box set: a papered flat wall pierced by two
doorways. This is the "fourth side" of the room turned away from us.

1. **Hall door, stage right.** The famous door Nora walks through. Built heavy,
   hung on a real mortise lock so the closing slam carries to the back row.
2. **Study door, stage left.** Torvald's room; backed with a warm amber glow so it
   reads as occupied even when shut.

Wall treatment:

- Wallpaper: muted teal damask, knocked back with a grey wash so costumes pop
- A framed engraving and a wall clock (set to 11:00) dress the stage-right panel
- Crown molding returns 18 inches onto the side walls to fake the corner

Through the gap between the doors you glimpse [[z=12|the window and its backing]].

@plane z=12 label="Window and Snow Backing" y=2
# Upstage window (z = 12 ft)

The deepest built element: a tall casement window centered between the two doors,
opening onto the painted exterior.

- **Window:** double casement, real muntins, gauze sheers that catch a cold blue
  side-light from offstage left
- **Practical snow:** a slow drift of paper flakes tumbles behind the gauze on a
  loop, lit cold so winter presses against the warm room
- The contrast of the cold window against [[z=4|the warm lamp downstage]] is the
  visual spine of the act: home versus the world Nora is about to enter

@plane z=16 label="Painted Drop and Cyclorama"
# Far backing (z = 16 ft)

The last plane, hung behind the window opening. The audience never sees it whole,
only the slice framed by the casement, which is exactly why it can be cheap and
broad.

- **Backdrop:** a soft-focus painted street of Norwegian townhouses under snow,
  values kept low so it never competes with the actors
- **Cyclorama:** a plain muslin cyc behind the drop, washed in deep indigo to
  suggest a winter night sky
- **Glow:** a faint gaslamp warm spot painted into the drop, the only point of
  exterior light, sitting dead center in [[z=12|the window frame]]

Striking order at the end of the run: this drop flies out first, then the rear
wall breaks apart, then the parlor clears toward [[z=0|the apron]].

3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: Storm Watch: One Night in the Light
view: present
keeper: Maren Solås
station: Ryvarden Lighthouse, 59°N
date: October 14, 1953
category: Story
---

A lighthouse keeper's log — one hour at a time, the storm building, cresting, and passing. Read plane by plane. Each hour is its own world.

@plane z=0 label="21:00 — First Warning"

# 21:00

The barometer has been falling since supper. I tapped the glass twice — it keeps dropping. The sea is that particular shade of pewter that means *business*, not beauty.

Lit the lamp at dusk as usual. The mechanism is running clean; I oiled the bearing track yesterday. Good timing.

**Wind:** SW, 12 knots  
**Pressure:** 1003 hPa, falling  
**Visibility:** 9 nautical miles  
**Sky:** Mackerel cloud, thin and fast

> *"A falling glass, a rising wind — sailor take heed."*

The supply boat was here last Tuesday. Enough kerosene for three weeks. Enough coffee for two. I know which one worries me more.

Wrote a letter to Astrid. Did not seal it yet. I like to think she can feel when the weather turns.

@plane z=1 label="23:00 — It Arrives"

# 23:00

There it is.

The sound changed an hour ago — from wind to *roar*. The kind that gets inside the walls and hums in your molars. Rain is coming sideways now; I can barely see the lantern room glass for the white streaks.

**Wind:** SW, 34 knots, gusting 41  
**Pressure:** 994 hPa — dropped 9 in two hours  
**Visibility:** 2 nautical miles, patches of 0  
**Sea state:** Heavy swell, 4–5 metre faces on the outer rocks

I spotted a freighter on the eastern bearing before the visibility closed in — running lights, moving fast, heading north. Someone out there is having a worse night than me.

The light is turning. Every 6 seconds, a flash. I have calibrated this rotation 400 times. Tonight I am glad of every one.

Ate the last of the bread. The butter went rancid two days ago and I threw it into the wind. The gulls followed it down and vanished.

@plane z=2 label="01:00 — The Eye of It"

# 01:00

The lamp room is shaking.

Not a tremor — a *shudder*, a physical thing you feel in your knees and your jaw. The lens assembly weighs 800 kilograms of precision-ground glass and it is vibrating like a bell.

**Wind:** WSW, 52 knots, gusts to 61  
**Pressure:** 988 hPa  
**Visibility:** Near zero — white  
**Note:** Spray reaching the lamp room glass (38 metres above sea level)

```
          ~~~^~~~^~~~
     ~~~^~~~~^~~~~^~~~~~
  ROCK        LIGHTHOUSE        ROCK
     ~~~^~~~~^~~~~^~~~~~
          ~~~^~~~^~~~
```

I checked the emergency oil reservoir. Full. I checked the clockwork drive. Running. I checked the emergency hand-crank. Ready.

Then I sat down on the metal stool bolted to the floor, and I listened to the Atlantic try to remove this building from the earth.

It has been trying for two hundred years. It has not managed it yet.

Thought about the freighter. Hoped they found a harbour.

@plane z=3 label="02:30 — Worst of It"

# 02:30

A wave took the weather vane off the east wall.

I heard it go — a crack like a rifle shot above the general screaming — and then it was simply gone. I will not find it until morning, if I find it at all.

**Wind:** W, 64 knots — *storm force*  
**Pressure:** 985 hPa (holding, perhaps the centre is passing)  
**Visibility:** Zero  
**Status:** Light turning. Light turning. Light turning.

This is the only entry that matters: **the light is turning.**

Everything else — the vane, the butter, the letter to Astrid, the uneaten supper — none of it matters tonight. One job. The ships in the dark need one fixed, reliable, honest thing.

I am that thing. The lamp is that thing.

6 seconds. Flash.  
6 seconds. Flash.  
6 seconds. Flash.

@plane z=4 label="04:00 — Breaking"

# 04:00

I can see the rocks again.

Not well — they appear in the troughs between swells, black and absolute, and then the white water covers them again — but I can *see*. The wind has backed to the northwest and dropped below 45 knots. That is still a gale. Tonight, it feels like silence.

**Wind:** NW, 43 knots  
**Pressure:** 988 hPa, *rising*  
**Visibility:** 1–2 nautical miles  
**Sea state:** Confused, heavy; the swell will take hours to settle

Made coffee. Stood at the south window with the mug in both hands and watched the storm unravel itself, piece by piece, the way anger does when it has nothing left to prove.

There is a colour the sky goes, between black storm and grey dawn, that has no name I know. Not hope exactly. Not relief. Something older than both.

@plane z=5 label="06:00 — First Light"

# 06:00

Dawn.

**Wind:** NW, 22 knots  
**Pressure:** 993 hPa, rising steadily  
**Visibility:** 7 nautical miles and improving  
**Sky:** Washed clean. Pale gold low in the east.

I extinguished the lamp at 06:04, as the horizon brightened enough that a ship could see the rocks by eye. The mechanism wound down slowly, the great lens coasting to a stop. It always feels like watching something fall asleep.

Found the weather vane in the kelp below the north steps. Bent but intact. I can straighten it.

Sealed the letter to Astrid.

The freighter — I think it was the *Vesterheim* out of Bergen, from the silhouette I glimpsed — was mentioned on the radio at 05:40. She made Måløy at 03:15. All hands well.

> One night. One lamp. One storm.  
> The rocks are still there. So is the light.  
> Tomorrow I will oil the bearing again.

*— M. Solås, Keeper, Third Grade*  
*Log submitted to Norwegian Lighthouse Authority, Bergen District*

3md: 1.0

title: 12-Week Strength Mesocycle (4-day)
category: Health
lifts: squat / bench / deadlift / press
deloads: weeks 4, 8, 12
---
A periodized program. Each plane is one training week; scrub Z to move through
the block. Three phases of four weeks: accumulation, intensification, peaking,
each capped by a [[z=3|deload]]. Loads are %1RM, effort is RPE.

@plane z=0 label="Wk 1 - Accumulation"
**Accumulation 1/3** - build volume, RPE 7

- Mon - Squat 4x8 @70% · RDL 3x10
- Tue - Bench 4x8 @70% · Row 4x10
- Thu - Front Squat 3x8 · Leg curl 3x12
- Fri - OHP 4x8 @68% · Pull-up 4x8

@plane z=1 label="Wk 2 - Accumulation"
**Accumulation 2/3** - more sets, RPE 8

- Mon - Squat 5x8 @72% · RDL 4x10
- Tue - Bench 5x8 @72% · Row 4x10
- Thu - Front Squat 4x8 · Leg curl 4x12
- Fri - OHP 5x8 @70% · Pull-up 4x10

@plane z=2 label="Wk 3 - Accumulation"
**Accumulation 3/3** - peak volume, RPE 9

- Mon - Squat 5x10 @72% · RDL 4x12
- Tue - Bench 5x10 @72% · Row 5x10
- Thu - Front Squat 4x10 · Leg curl 4x15
- Fri - OHP 5x10 @70% · Pull-up 5x10

@plane z=3 label="Wk 4 - DELOAD"
**Deload** - cut volume ~50%, RPE 5. Recover.

- Mon - Squat 3x5 @60%
- Tue - Bench 3x5 @60%
- Thu - light technique work
- Fri - OHP 3x5 @58%

Next: [[z=4|intensification block]].

@plane z=4 label="Wk 5 - Intensification"
**Intensification 1/3** - load up, RPE 7

- Mon - Squat 5x5 @78% · RDL 3x8
- Tue - Bench 5x5 @78% · Row 4x8
- Thu - Pause Squat 4x5 · Leg curl 3x10
- Fri - OHP 5x5 @76% · Pull-up 4x6

@plane z=5 label="Wk 6 - Intensification"
**Intensification 2/3** - heavier, RPE 8

- Mon - Squat 5x4 @82% · RDL 3x6
- Tue - Bench 5x4 @82% · Row 4x6
- Thu - Pause Squat 4x4 · Leg curl 3x10
- Fri - OHP 5x4 @80% · Weighted pull-up 4x5

@plane z=6 label="Wk 7 - Intensification"
**Intensification 3/3** - top loads, RPE 9

- Mon - Squat 4x3 @86% · RDL 3x5
- Tue - Bench 4x3 @86% · Row 4x6
- Thu - Pause Squat 3x3 · Leg curl 3x10
- Fri - OHP 4x3 @84% · Weighted pull-up 4x4

@plane z=7 label="Wk 8 - DELOAD"
**Deload** - cut intensity, RPE 5. Prime for the peak.

- Mon - Squat 3x4 @65%
- Tue - Bench 3x4 @65%
- Thu - light technique work
- Fri - OHP 3x4 @63%

Next: [[z=8|peaking block]].

@plane z=8 label="Wk 9 - Peaking"
**Peaking 1/3** - heavy triples, RPE 8

- Mon - Squat 4x3 @88% · light RDL 2x6
- Tue - Bench 4x3 @88% · Row 3x6
- Thu - Deadlift 4x3 @85%
- Fri - OHP 4x3 @86%

@plane z=9 label="Wk 10 - Peaking"
**Peaking 2/3** - doubles, RPE 9

- Mon - Squat 3x2 @91%
- Tue - Bench 3x2 @91% · Row 3x6
- Thu - Deadlift 3x2 @89%
- Fri - OHP 3x2 @89%

@plane z=10 label="Wk 11 - Peaking"
**Peaking 3/3** - openers, sharp and short

- Mon - Squat singles @93-95%
- Tue - Bench singles @93-95%
- Thu - Deadlift singles @92-94%
- Fri - rest / mobility

@plane z=11 label="Wk 12 - TEST / DELOAD"
**Test week** - work up to a new 1RM, then back off.

- Mon - Squat: work to a daily max
- Wed - Bench: work to a daily max
- Fri - Deadlift: work to a daily max

Then rest a week and restart at [[z=0|accumulation]] with the new maxes.
