3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: Suspension Bridge: Load Path in Layers
view: stack
legend: T=🟫 C=🟦 S=🟥 D=🟨 .=⬛ ^=🔼 |=⬜ ~=🌊
category: Engineering
---

A suspension bridge doesn't just hold itself up — it *redirects* forces through a chain of clever handoffs. Each layer here peels back one structural system, revealing how a truck's weight travels from deck to earth.

@plane z=0 label="Full Bridge"

```
..........T.....T.....T.....T..........
.........T|T...T|T...T|T...T|T.........
........T.|.T.T.|.T.T.|.T.T.|.T........
.......T..|..T..|..T..|..T..|..T.......
......T...|...T.|..T..|.T...|...T......
.....CCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCC....
.....|..............D...............|...
SSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSSS
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```

**The complete picture.** A loaded span carries a truck (D) across the stiffening girder deck. Above it, cables (C) sweep catenary curves between two towers (T). Below, the roadway sits on vertical hangers (|). The whole system bears on concrete anchor blocks and piers (S) at the shores.

> Every force you see here is either **tension** (pulling) or **compression** (pushing) — nothing else.

@plane z=1 label="Dead + Live Load"

```
..............🚛..............
......................................
    ↓ 800 kN     ↓ 800 kN
- - - - - D - - - D - - - - -
    ↓           ↓
```

**Where the load enters.** The truck applies concentrated **dead load** (its own weight, constant) plus **live load** (moving, variable) directly to the **stiffening girder** — the horizontal beam running the length of the deck.

- Dead load: self-weight of deck, girder, hangers, cables
- Live load: vehicles, pedestrians, wind, snow
- The girder's job: **distribute** point loads into many smaller forces along its length

**Key principle:** The girder resists local bending, preventing the cable from deforming into an ugly kink at every truck wheel.

@plane z=2 label="Hangers: Tension Transfer"

```
    |       |       |       |
    |  ↑T   |  ↑T   |  ↑T   |
    |       |       |       |
====|=======|=======|=======|====  ← stiffening girder
    ↑       ↑       ↑       ↑
  (load panels transferred upward)
```

**Hangers carry tension — only tension.** Vertical steel rods (or wire bundles) hang from the main cable above and attach to the girder below.

- Each panel of deck load is **lifted** by its hanger
- Hangers cannot push — they go slack if compressed
- Typical hanger spacing: **6 – 12 m**
- Material: high-strength galvanized wire rope or parallel wire strands

> *If a hanger snapped, its load would redistribute to adjacent hangers — the bridge would sag locally but not collapse.*

@plane z=3 label="Main Cable: Catenary Curve"

```
T                               T
 \                             /
  \           C C C           /
   \       C       C       /
    \   C               C   /
     \ /                 \ /
      *                   *
   (low point)         (low point)
      ↓ max tension here ↓
```

**The cable chooses its own shape.** Under uniform load a freely hanging cable forms a **parabola** (not a catenary — that's self-weight only). The main cable is a bundle of thousands of parallel high-strength wires, **cold-drawn** to 1,570 MPa tensile strength.

- Sag ratio (sag ÷ span) is typically **1:10**
- Shallower sag → higher cable tension, larger anchor forces
- Steeper sag → lower tension, but more material and taller towers

The cable is **always in pure tension** — it cannot carry bending. This is why suspension bridges can span so far: tension members are extremely efficient.

@plane z=4 label="Towers: Compression Path"

```
      ████
      ████   ← cable saddle at crown
      ████
     ██████
     ██████
    ████████
    ████████
   ██████████
   ██████████
  ████████████
  ████████████  ← base: max compression
SSSSSSSSSSSSSSSS
```

**Towers carry compression — straight down.** The cable pulls inward and downward on each tower top (the **saddle**). The tower converts this diagonal cable force into a **near-vertical compressive load** transmitted to its foundation.

- Tower material: **steel box sections** (modern) or **reinforced concrete**
- Golden Gate towers: 227 m tall, weigh 22,000 tonnes each
- Base pressure on bedrock: up to **30 MPa**

> *Towers are the only major compression members in a suspension bridge — everything else is in tension.*

@plane z=5 label="Anchor Blocks: Force Resolution"

```
cable →→→→→→→→→→→\
                   \  resultant
                    \  ↘
 ════════════════════ ANCHOR BLOCK ════
                    ↙
 uplift ↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑ weight of concrete resists
 
 ████████████████████████████████████
 ████  GRAVITY ANCHOR  ██████████████   ~100,000 tonnes
 ████████████████████████████████████
 ||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||||
         bedrock / soil
```

**The anchor block closes the load path.** Cables pull outward and upward on each anchor. A massive concrete block (gravity anchor) or a tunnel drilled into rock (rock anchor) provides the equal-and-opposite reaction.

- Gravity anchor: pure dead weight resists cable pull
- Rock anchor: cable strands fan out and bond into drilled holes
- The horizontal cable component → **anchor must not slide**
- The vertical cable component → **anchor must not tip**

**Load path complete:**
`Truck → Girder → Hangers → Cable → Towers → Foundations → Earth`
`       (bending) (tension) (tension) (compression)     (bearing)`

@plane z=6 label="Force Magnitude Map"

| Member | Force Type | Typical Magnitude | Material |
|---|---|---|---|
| Deck girder | Bending + shear | 50 – 200 kN·m | Steel / concrete |
| Hangers | Tension | 500 – 2,000 kN each | Wire rope |
| Main cable | Tension | **500,000 – 2,000,000 kN** | Parallel wire strand |
| Tower | Compression | 200,000 – 800,000 kN | Steel or concrete |
| Anchor block | Tension pull-out | 300,000 – 1,500,000 kN | Concrete + rock |

**Why the cable tension is so enormous:** it carries the *entire bridge* in one continuous element. The Golden Gate's main cables each contain **27,572 wires** — laid end-to-end they would circle the Earth **3.5 times**.

> The elegance of suspension: a bridge that weighs hundreds of thousands of tonnes hangs from wires thinner than your wrist, because tension is the most efficient force a material can carry.

3md: 1.0
axis: path
title: The Tea Shop at the End of the Lane
category: Story
byline: a tiny choose-your-path, by Juniper
view: single
---
A four-minute story you steer yourself. Read a scene, then pick a door.
Every choice is a [[z=1|cross-plane link]] that walks you to another plane.
There is no wrong path, only different cups of tea.

@plane z=0 label="The Lane"
# The Lane

Rain has just stopped. The lane smells of wet stone and woodsmoke, and at the
very end a small shop glows amber through fogged glass. A hand-painted sign
sways: **open, probably**.

You walk up. There is a brass **knocker** shaped like a sleeping fox, and a
door already left a finger's width **ajar**.

- Lift the [[z=1|fox knocker]]
- Slip through the [[z=2|open door]]

@plane z=1 label="You Knock"
# You lift the knocker

It is warm, which knockers should not be. Before you can rap it twice the door
opens itself, and a voice like steam says, *"Punctual. I like that."*

Inside: shelves to the ceiling, every jar labeled in a language of smells.

- Ask for [[z=3|"whatever I need"]]
- Ask for [[z=4|"something brave"]]

@plane z=2 label="You Enter"
# You slip inside

No one is here, but a single cup waits on the counter, still steaming, with a
note: *"For the curious. Sit anywhere."*

You sit. The chair sighs happily. The shop, you realize, has been **waiting**.

- Drink before [[z=3|anyone notices]]
- Wait, politely, for [[z=4|the owner]]

@plane z=5 label="The good cup"
# The cup you needed

It tastes like the afternoon you forgot you missed: sunlight on a kitchen
table, a book you never finished, someone humming in the next room.

You leave a coin you didn't know you had. The fox knocker winks.

> Begin again from [[z=0|the lane]]?

@plane z=3 label="What You Need"
# "Whatever I need"

The shopkeeper, who is mostly silhouette and entirely kind, considers you for
a long moment, then pours something pale gold.

*"This one is honest,"* they warn. *"Drink slowly."*

- [[z=5|Drink it]]

@plane z=4 label="Something Brave"
# "Something brave"

A grin in the dark. *"Now we are talking."* They reach to the highest shelf,
to a jar that hums, and measure three careful drops into roaring hot water.

*"Bravery is mostly just the first sip,"* they say.

- [[z=5|Take the first sip]]

3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Tell Jezreel — Trench G
view: elevator
site: Tell Jezreel, Jezreel Valley, Israel
trench: G (5 × 5 m), opened 2024 season
excavator: Dr. Mara Sondheim, Area Supervisor
datum: 182.4 m asl
category: Information
---
Trench G, opened May 2024. Scrape down with me — each centimeter counts.

@plane z=0 label="Surface (0–15 cm)"
# Topsoil & Disturbance Layer
**Munsell:** 10YR 4/3 — brown, loose, modern rootlets throughout.

Modern intrusions dominate. Bulldozer scar cuts SW corner at 7 cm; tractor tread impressions visible in plan.

**Finds bag G-001:**
- 3× machine-made glass sherds (post-1940)
- 1× pull-tab aluminum (Goldstar beer, c. 1985)
- Olive root mat, *Olea europaea*, modern grove above

> "Nothing here worth drawing. Keep going." — field note, 6 May 2024

@plane z=1 label="Ottoman (15–38 cm)"
# Ottoman Horizon — c. 1600–1900 CE
**Munsell:** 10YR 5/4 — yellowish brown, compact, charcoal flecks.

Hard-packed surface at 22 cm. Possible threshing floor: dense phytolith scatter, cereal chaff impressions in silty matrix. A 2 cm lens of ash sits directly atop it.

**Finds bag G-007:**
- 14× hand-made Gaza Ware sherds (buff fabric, red painted bands)
- 1× Ottoman copper *para* coin, heavily corroded — **pending numismatic ID**
- 3× clay tobacco pipe fragments (çubuk), white-slipped

**Feature 14:** Robber trench cuts south baulk — stone robbing event, likely 18th c.

@plane z=2 label="Crusader (38–61 cm)"
# Crusader/Frankish Destruction Layer — c. 1187 CE
**Munsell:** 2.5YR 4/6 — reddish brown; intense burning event.

Thick (8 cm) destruction debris: mudbrick collapse, vitrified daub, calcined animal bone. Saladin's 1187 campaign destroyed the Frankish manor recorded in the *Tabula Rogeria* as *Gesdreel*.

**Finds bag G-019:**
- 2× Crusader *denier tournois* silver coins (Kingdom of Jerusalem, 1148–1187 CE range)
- 11× Frankish wheel-thrown fine ware (cream fabric, green glaze drips)
- 1× iron crossbow bolt tip, 9.4 cm, square-section — **conservation priority**
- Burnt olive wood charcoal → submitted for AMS: **Lab #OxA-41882**

*Absolute date expected back September 2024. Predicted 1150–1200 CE.*

@plane z=3 label="Byzantine (61–90 cm)"
# Byzantine Farmstead — c. 500–700 CE
**Munsell:** 10YR 6/3 — pale brown, loose sand, lime mortar chunks.

Plaster floor surface at 74 cm (F.22): white hydraulic lime, 1.5 cm thick, red-painted border stripe intact at north edge. Room function unclear; seed press weight found *in situ* suggests olive processing.

**Finds bag G-031:**
- 38× Late Roman amphora (LRA 1 type, Cilician origin — olive oil trade)
- 1× bronze coin, *nummus*, nearly illegible — likely Justinian I reign (527–565 CE)
- Bone stylus, 8.1 cm, incised cross near tip
- Mosaic tesserae scatter (white limestone): floor above destroyed, tesserae washed down

**Ceramic specialist note:** LRA 1 amphora concentration suggests storage/redistribution node on Byzantine road visible in aerial survey 400 m east.

@plane z=4 label="Iron Age II (90–130 cm)"
# Israelite Occupation — Iron Age IIB, c. 900–750 BCE
**Munsell:** 7.5YR 5/4 — brown, dense, high ceramic concentration.

This is the layer. Tell Jezreel is synonymous with the Omride dynasty; this horizon is coeval with **Ahab's royal enclosure** documented in Trenches A–D, 60 m north.

**Finds bag G-048:**
- 112× Iron Age IIB sherds — store jars (collared rim), cooking pots, *lmlk* handle fragment (four-winged seal impression, *Hebron* — administrative storage)
- 1× ivory plaque fragment, 3.4 × 2.1 cm, lotus carved in relief → **Tell Jezreel ivories assemblage**
- Carbonized barley (*Hordeum vulgare*) in sealed pit F.29 → AMS submitted
- 3× loom weights, fired clay, biconical

**Phytolith analysis pending.** Grain storage function strongly implied for this cell of the enclosure.

@plane z=5 label="Iron Age I (130–158 cm)"
# Pre-Omride Gap — Iron Age I, c. 1150–950 BCE
**Munsell:** 10YR 6/4 — light yellowish brown, sparse finds, wind-blown silt lenses.

Occupation hiatus or light pastoral use. No architectural remains; only pits and ephemeral surfaces. Collared-rim store jars continue but density drops sharply. Possibly open grazing land between the Late Bronze collapse and the Omride building program.

**Finds bag G-059:**
- 9× collared-rim store jar body sherds
- 1× bronze fibula, simple arc type
- Animal bone: *Bos taurus* (cattle), *Capra aegagrus hircus* (goat) — pastoral assemblage

> The silence between cities. Nothing marks this earth but hoofprints that decomposed three thousand years ago.

@plane z=6 label="Late Bronze (158–190 cm)"
# Egyptian-Canaanite Town — Late Bronze IIB, c. 1350–1150 BCE
**Munsell:** 5YR 5/6 — yellowish red, dense mud-brick melt, high bone density.

Mud-brick architecture fully exposed at 168 cm: two walls meeting at right angle, mud-brick (32 × 16 × 8 cm), still standing 4 courses. Egyptian influence pervasive — this is the era of Thutmose III's campaigns; Jezreel appears in the **Karnak toponym lists** (list 1, entry 84, *i-dz-r-ʿr*).

**Finds bag G-071:**
- 1× Canaanite storage jar, complete, mended — **78 cm tall, red-slipped**
- 1× Egyptian blue faience amulet, Bes figure, 2.2 cm — import or local copy?
- 4× Cypriot Base Ring II ware juglets (opium poppy morphology debated in literature)
- Cuneiform tablet fragment? **3 signs visible** → epigraphic photography submitted to Göttingen

**Absolute date:** Thermoluminescence on mudbrick (Lab #TL-2024-88): **1290 ± 65 BCE**. High-confidence Late Bronze IIB.

--- 3md: 1.0 axis: frame title: Tendu Sequence: Five Positions to Croisé view: play fps: 4 counts: 8 tempo: Adagio, ♩= 52 music: Bach Sarabande in D minor category: Animation --- Five positions, one *battement tendu* to croisé devant. Eight counts. Mark each with your right hand. @plane z=0 label="1st Position" ``` o /|\ / \ / \ ──────/─────\────── ◁▷ ◁▷ ``` **1st Position** Heels together, feet turned out 180°. Weight centered. Tailbone dropped. > *"Find the floor through your heels before you find it through your toes."* Count: **&**
Arms: *bras bas* — soft oval, fingertips nearly touching. @plane z=1 label="2nd Position" ``` o /|\ / | \ / | \ ─────/───┼───\───── ◁▷ ◁▷ ``` **2nd Position** Feet hip-width apart along the same line. Inner thighs rotated forward and up. > *"Don't sit in 2nd — lift your inner arch."* Count: **1**
Arms: *à la seconde* — palms down, elbows soft, wrists leading. @plane z=2 label="3rd Position" ``` o /|\ /|\ / | \ ──────/──┼──\────── ◁▷◁▷ ``` **3rd Position** Right heel to left instep. A gateway position — taught before 5th. > *"Use it as a checkpoint, not a stopping point."* Count: **2**
Arms: one in 1st, one à la seconde. @plane z=3 label="4th Position" ``` o /|\ /|\ / | \ ──────/──┼──\────── ◁▷ ◁▷ ↑ right foot forward ``` **4th Position (open)** Right foot forward, one foot-length gap. Hips square. Don't shift forward! > *"The space between your feet is earned, not given."* Count: **3**
Arms: one in 1st (*en avant*), one à la seconde. @plane z=4 label="5th Position" ``` o /|\ /|\ / | \ ──────/──┼──\────── ◁▷◁▷◁▷ ↑ heels & toes crossed ``` **5th Position** Right foot fully crossed in front of left. The most demanding — knees over toes. > *"Earn 5th with your hips, not your ankles."* Count: **4**
Arms: *en couronne* — both overhead, soft wrists, no tension in the neck. @plane z=5 label="Dégagé" ``` o /|\ / \ / \──────→ ⌒ ──────/───────────── ◁▷ ``` **Dégagé à la 4th (croisé)** Right leg brushes forward through 1st. Foot skims the floor — *tendu* in motion. > *"The brush is the whole story. The destination is just punctuation."* Count: **5–6**
Arms open from *en couronne* to *croisé devant*: left arm in 1st, right à la seconde, head inclined left. @plane z=6 label="Croisé Devant" ``` o ←head inclines /|╲ / | ╲──arm / | ╲ ─────/───┼────────→ ⌒ ◁▷ | ↑ supporting leg ``` **Croisé Devant — Held** Right leg fully extended front, foot *sur la pointe* or *demi-pointe*. Body crosses the line of the leg. > *"Croisé is not a shape — it is an intention toward someone in the audience."* Count: **7–8**
Line of energy: from the back heel of the supporting leg, through the spine, through the crown, through the extended fingertip of the right hand. One unbroken filament. See the opening position again: [[z=0|return to 1st]]. @plane z=7 label="Choreographer's Notes" **Phrase Notes — Adagio Sarabande** | Count | Action | Body | |-------|--------|------| | & | prepare in 1st | breathe in | | 1 | open to 2nd | arms rise | | 2 | close to 3rd | settle | | 3 | open to 4th | clarify hip square | | 4 | close to 5th | crown lifts | | 5–6 | dégagé croisé | brush initiates | | 7–8 | hold croisé devant | find the line | **Common corrections this phrase surfaces:** - [ ] Gripping the standing hip in 2nd - [ ] Collapsing the lower back in 5th - [ ] Rushing the brush on count 5 - [ ] Losing the back of the neck in croisé *Repeat to the left beginning count & of the next phrase.* ---

3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: Account Settings Screen, Theme Variants
component: SettingsCard
viewport: 375x812
tokens: design-tokens/v3
---
One screen, the account settings card, described three times. Each plane is the
same layout and the same copy; only the resolved design tokens change. The light
layer is the default, the dark layer remaps surfaces and text, and the
high-contrast layer pushes ratios past WCAG AAA for accessibility audits.

Read the planes as overlays of one another: identical geometry, different
treatment. Cross-plane links point at the matching region in a sibling layer so
you can diff a single control across themes.

@plane z=0 label="Light" x=0 y=0
# Settings Card, Light

The baseline theme. Warm neutral surfaces on white, ink-dark text, a single
brand accent for the primary action.

## Resolved tokens

| Token              | Value     | Role                          |
| ------------------ | --------- | ----------------------------- |
| `--bg-canvas`      | `#FFFFFF` | Page behind the card          |
| `--bg-surface`     | `#F6F5F2` | Card fill                     |
| `--text-primary`   | `#1A1A1E` | Headings, labels              |
| `--text-muted`     | `#5C5C66` | Helper text, captions         |
| `--accent`         | `#2F6BFF` | Primary button, focus ring    |
| `--border-hair`    | `#E2E0DB` | Dividers, input outlines      |

## Layout

```
+--------------------------------------+
|  Account                             |
|  jamie@corvidlabs.dev                |
|  --------------------------------    |
|  [ Email notifications      (on) ]   |
|  [ Two-factor auth         (off) ]   |
|                                      |
|         ( Save changes )             |
+--------------------------------------+
```

- Surface to text contrast: `#F6F5F2` on `#1A1A1E` is about 14.8:1.
- The accent (`#2F6BFF`) carries the only saturated color, so the eye lands on
  Save changes first.
- Toggle "on" state uses the accent fill; "off" uses `--border-hair`.

Compare this exact card in [[z=1|the dark layer]] or push it to
[[z=2|high contrast]].

@plane z=1 label="Dark" x=0 y=0
# Settings Card, Dark

Same card, remapped for low-light. We do not simply invert: surfaces sit a step
above pure black so the card reads as an elevated panel, and the accent is
lightened so it still pops against a dark field.

## Resolved tokens

| Token              | Value     | Role                          |
| ------------------ | --------- | ----------------------------- |
| `--bg-canvas`      | `#0B0B0F` | Page behind the card          |
| `--bg-surface`     | `#17171C` | Card fill (elevation 1)       |
| `--text-primary`   | `#F2F2F5` | Headings, labels              |
| `--text-muted`     | `#9A9AA4` | Helper text, captions         |
| `--accent`         | `#6E9BFF` | Primary button, focus ring    |
| `--border-hair`    | `#2A2A31` | Dividers, input outlines      |

## What changed from the light layer

- Canvas drops to near-black (`#0B0B0F`), the card lifts to `#17171C` so the
  edge is visible without a hard border.
- Accent shifts from `#2F6BFF` to `#6E9BFF`. The darker blue would muddy against
  the dark surface, so we raise lightness to keep a 5.1:1 ratio on the button.
- Muted text is recomputed (`#9A9AA4`) to hold above 4.5:1 on the card fill,
  matching the muted role in [[z=0|the light layer]].

## Layout

```
+--------------------------------------+
|  Account                             |
|  jamie@corvidlabs.dev                |
|  --------------------------------    |
|  [ Email notifications      (on) ]   |
|  [ Two-factor auth         (off) ]   |
|                                      |
|         ( Save changes )             |
+--------------------------------------+
```

Geometry is byte-for-byte identical to light; only fills and strokes resolve
differently. For the audited version, see [[z=2|high contrast]].

@plane z=2 label="High Contrast" x=0 y=0
# Settings Card, High Contrast

The accessibility layer, tuned for low-vision users and forced-colors mode. Every
foreground-on-background pair clears WCAG AAA (7:1 for body text), borders become
explicit rather than hairline, and the focus ring is doubled in weight.

## Resolved tokens

| Token              | Value     | Role                              |
| ------------------ | --------- | --------------------------------- |
| `--bg-canvas`      | `#000000` | Page behind the card              |
| `--bg-surface`     | `#000000` | Card fill (flat, no elevation)    |
| `--text-primary`   | `#FFFFFF` | Headings, labels                  |
| `--text-muted`     | `#E8E8E8` | Helper text (still about 17:1)    |
| `--accent`         | `#FFD400` | Primary button, focus ring        |
| `--border-strong`  | `#FFFFFF` | 2px solid outline on every edge   |

## What changed from the dark layer

- Surfaces collapse to pure black and text to pure white, the maximum 21:1 pair,
  versus roughly 13:1 in [[z=1|the dark layer]].
- Hairline dividers are replaced by `--border-strong`, a solid 2px white outline,
  so card edges, inputs, and toggles never rely on subtle fills.
- The accent moves to amber (`#FFD400`) because blue cannot reach 7:1 against
  black at a comfortable size. Amber on black is about 15.9:1.
- Toggle states no longer lean on color alone: "on" shows a filled box with a
  checkmark, "off" shows an empty box.

## Layout

```
+======================================+
|  Account                             |
|  jamie@corvidlabs.dev                |
|  ==============================      |
|  [ Email notifications     [x]on ]   |
|  [ Two-factor auth         [ ]off ]  |
|                                      |
|        [[ Save changes ]]            |
+======================================+
```

Back to the default treatment: [[z=0|light layer]]. Same card, three honest
renderings of the same screen.

3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: Threat Model by Depth - Customer Records API
system: orders-api
methodology: STRIDE + attack-surface layering
reviewed: 2026-06-23
owner: platform-security
---
This threat model stacks the same system along a depth axis, from the
outermost attacker-facing surface down to the data at rest. Each plane
holds the assets, trust boundaries, and mitigations that live at that
depth. Read it front to back to follow an attacker's path inward, or jump
straight to the layer you own.

Depth convention: z=0 is the public edge (closest to the attacker), and
larger z values sit deeper inside the trust perimeter. The deepest plane
(the database) is the asset everything else exists to protect.

@plane z=0 label="Public edge" x=0 y=0
# Depth 0 - Public Edge

The internet-facing surface: DNS, the CDN, and the TLS-terminating load
balancer. Everything here is reachable by anonymous, unauthenticated
clients, so it is the broadest and least-trusted layer.

## Assets
- Public hostname `api.example.com` and its DNS records
- TLS certificate and private key (terminated at the load balancer)
- Edge WAF rules and rate-limit counters

## Trust boundary
Anonymous internet -> edge. Treat every byte as hostile.

## STRIDE highlights
- **Spoofing**: domain takeover via a dangling CNAME pointing at a
  decommissioned bucket.
- **Tampering**: request smuggling through inconsistent header parsing
  between the CDN and the origin.
- **Denial of service**: volumetric floods and slow-loris connections
  exhausting the listener pool.

## Mitigations
- HSTS with preload, TLS 1.2 minimum, and OCSP stapling.
- Monitored DNS with CAA records; alert on any record change.
- Anycast scrubbing plus per-IP and per-token rate limits at the WAF.

Once a request survives the edge it reaches the gateway, where it must
prove who it is: see [[z=1|the authentication layer]].

@plane z=1 label="Authentication and gateway" x=0 y=1
# Depth 1 - Authentication and Gateway

The API gateway authenticates callers and applies coarse authorization
before any business logic runs. This is the first layer that distinguishes
one principal from another.

## Assets
- OAuth2 / OIDC token validation keys (JWKS)
- Session and refresh tokens
- API keys issued to partner integrations

## Trust boundary
Unauthenticated request -> authenticated principal. Crossing it grants an
identity, so the integrity of token validation is critical.

## STRIDE highlights
- **Spoofing**: forged JWTs from an `alg: none` downgrade or a leaked
  signing key.
- **Elevation of privilege**: a token minted for a low-tier partner being
  accepted for admin scopes because audience and scope claims are not
  checked.
- **Repudiation**: missing correlation IDs make it impossible to tie an
  action back to a specific token.

## Mitigations
- Validate `alg`, `iss`, `aud`, and `exp` on every token; pin the JWKS and
  reject unknown key IDs.
- Short token lifetimes with rotating refresh tokens; revocation list
  checked at the gateway.
- Stamp every authenticated request with an immutable correlation ID that
  flows down to [[z=4|the audit log]].

The attacker who got past the edge ([[z=0|public edge]]) now needs a valid
identity. With one in hand, they reach the application logic at
[[z=2|the service layer]].

@plane z=2 label="Application service" x=0 y=2
# Depth 2 - Application Service

The order and customer microservices: where authenticated requests turn
into business actions. Authorization decisions and input handling live
here.

## Assets
- Business logic for reading and mutating customer and order records
- Per-resource authorization rules (who may see which customer)
- Internal service-to-service credentials

## Trust boundary
Authenticated principal -> authorized action on a specific resource. A
valid token does not imply access to a given record.

## STRIDE highlights
- **Information disclosure**: insecure direct object reference, where
  `GET /customers/{id}` returns any id the caller names.
- **Tampering**: mass-assignment letting a client set `role` or
  `account_balance` through an unfiltered request body.
- **Elevation of privilege**: server-side request forgery from an
  order-import feature reaching the internal metadata service.

## Mitigations
- Enforce object-level authorization on every record access, not just at
  the route. Default deny.
- Allowlist writable fields per endpoint; reject unknown keys.
- Egress allowlist for outbound calls; block link-local and metadata IP
  ranges.

Validated, authorized requests now read and write through the persistence
layer at [[z=3|the data access layer]].

@plane z=3 label="Data access" x=0 y=3
# Depth 3 - Data Access Layer

The ORM, query builders, and the cache that sit between the service and the
database. This layer shapes how data is fetched and is a classic injection
chokepoint.

## Assets
- Parameterized query templates and the ORM mapping
- Connection pool credentials to the database
- The read-through cache (may hold sensitive rows in memory)

## Trust boundary
Application intent -> concrete database operations. A flaw here can turn a
benign request into an arbitrary query.

## STRIDE highlights
- **Tampering / disclosure**: SQL injection through string-concatenated
  filters or unsafe `ORDER BY` clauses.
- **Information disclosure**: cache poisoning or cross-tenant cache key
  collisions returning another tenant's rows.
- **Denial of service**: unbounded queries (no `LIMIT`) that scan and load
  entire tables.

## Mitigations
- Parameterized queries everywhere; allowlist sortable columns rather than
  interpolating them.
- Tenant ID baked into every cache key and every query predicate.
- Mandatory pagination caps and statement timeouts on the connection.

Every operation that reaches this layer is mirrored into [[z=4|the audit
log]], and the data itself lives one layer deeper at [[z=5|the data store]].

@plane z=4 label="Audit and detection" x=1 y=3
# Depth 4 - Audit and Detection

A cross-cutting layer (offset on x to show it spans the stack) that
records what happened and surfaces anomalies. It does not block requests;
it makes attacks visible and recoverable.

## Assets
- Append-only audit log of authenticated, authorized mutations
- Security event stream feeding detection rules
- Alerting and on-call routing configuration

## Trust boundary
System behavior -> durable, tamper-evident record. The value of this layer
collapses if the log can be edited or silenced.

## STRIDE highlights
- **Repudiation**: an actor denies a deletion because the log lacks the
  correlation ID minted at [[z=1|the gateway]].
- **Tampering**: an attacker with database access edits or truncates the
  log to cover tracks.
- **Information disclosure**: logs capturing full request bodies leak
  tokens or personal data.

## Mitigations
- Write audit records to append-only, write-once storage shipped off-host
  in near real time.
- Redact secrets and personal fields before they ever reach the log.
- Detection rules on bulk reads, off-hours admin actions, and repeated
  authorization denials.

@plane z=5 label="Data store" x=0 y=4
# Depth 5 - Data Store

The deepest layer: the customer database and its backups. This is the
crown-jewel asset that every layer above exists to protect. Reaching it is
the attacker's goal and the last line of defense.

## Assets
- Customer PII and order history (the primary database)
- Encryption keys and the key management service
- Automated backups and their restore path

## Trust boundary
Internal service network -> persistent storage. By the time an action
arrives here it is assumed authorized, so defenses focus on containment and
limiting blast radius.

## STRIDE highlights
- **Information disclosure**: a stolen backup or snapshot exposing the full
  dataset at once.
- **Elevation of privilege**: a single overprivileged database account that
  can read every table and drop schemas.
- **Denial of service**: ransomware encrypting both the live store and its
  backups.

## Mitigations
- Encryption at rest with keys held in a separate KMS; rotate keys and
  scope grants per service.
- Least-privilege database roles: the order service cannot read the
  credentials table.
- Immutable, access-controlled backups with periodic restore drills.

An attacker who reaches this depth has traversed every layer above, from
[[z=0|the public edge]] inward. Defense in depth means no single layer is
trusted to be the only thing standing between them and these rows.

3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: The Three-Card Veil
view: present
deck: Rider-Waite-Smith
querent: anonymous
drawn: 2026-06-23
category: Story
---
*A three-card spread for today. Shuffle inward. When still, draw.*

@plane z=0 label="Past — What Was"
# VII. The Chariot — *Reversed*

```
  ╔═══════════╗
  ║  ☽  ☽    ║
  ║ ◈  ◈     ║
  ║    ▼     ║
  ║  ░░░░░   ║
  ║ ░ ⊕⊕ ░  ║
  ╚═══════════╝
      ▽  ▽
```

**Upright:** the iron will that drove you here — deadlines met through sheer momentum, a season of forward motion at all costs.

**Reversed (your past):** the cost of that momentum. You arrived, yes. But the reins slipped somewhere around mile forty. Exhaustion as sediment.

> *"You won every race you entered, and forgot you chose the races."*

What you carry forward from this card is not the victory.  
It is the question the victory left unanswered.

[[z=1|Cross into the Present]]

@plane z=1 label="Present — What Is"
# II. The High Priestess

```
  ╔═══════════╗
  ║ B   ◉   J ║
  ║ ║  ═══  ║ ║
  ║ ║ ░ ☽ ░ ║ ║
  ║ ║ ░   ░ ║ ║
  ║   ▓▓▓▓▓   ║
  ╚═══════════╝
       🌊
```

**Core meaning:** the veil between knowing and speaking. She does not explain herself. Neither does the thing you already understand but have not yet said aloud.

**In this position:** you are standing in the doorway. Both temples — Boaz and Jachin, restriction and mercy — are at your shoulders. You are not blocked. You are *between*.

The pomegranates behind her veil are not forbidden fruit.  
They are patience wearing the costume of secrecy.

- [ ] What do you know that you haven't written down?
- [ ] Who would hear it if you did?

> *The scroll in her lap is half-unrolled. The half you can't see is the half you already wrote.*

[[z=0|Return to the Past]] · [[z=2|See what's coming]]

@plane z=2 label="Future — What Will Be"
# XIX. The Sun

```
  ╔═══════════╗
  ║ ☀☀☀☀☀☀☀☀☀ ║
  ║ ☀         ║
  ║ ☀  \o/   ☀║
  ║ ☀   |    ☀║
  ║ ☀  /|    ☀║
  ║ ░░░░░░░░░░ ║
  ╚═══════════╝
  ~~~~  ~~~~
```

**Core meaning:** not happiness as a reward. Happiness as a *condition*. The child on the white horse isn't riding toward joy — joy is already the ground under the hooves.

**In this position (your future):** this card does not promise ease. It promises clarity. After the Chariot's cost and the Priestess's patience, what comes is simple light — the kind that makes shadows obvious rather than frightening.

The sunflowers face it directly.  
You will too.

**What the spread says, whole:**
You burned hard (*Chariot*), learned to wait (*Priestess*), and the reward is not rest but *sight* — the ability to see your own life in full sun, without the fog of urgency.

> *"The sun doesn't rise to reward the morning. It rises because that is what suns do."*

**One thing to do before the next reading:**  
Write the question you were too afraid to ask when you shuffled.

[[z=1|Back to the Present]]

3md: 1.0
axis: depth
title: The Anatomy of a Tide Pool
author: Claude (blind-test author)
license: CC-BY-4.0
# the line below is a comment and is ignored by the parser
camera: "looking straight down, noon light"
---
A single tide pool, read as a stack of **depth planes** rather than a timeline.
Start at the glittering surface and descend, plane by plane, into the cold dark
where the rock meets the sea floor. Each plane is the same patch of water at a
different depth; the Z axis here is literal vertical distance, measured in
centimetres below the waterline.

Begin at [[z=0|the surface film]] and sink toward [[z=3]].

@plane z=0 label="Surface Film" depth_cm="0"
# 0 cm - The Surface Film

A skin of water one molecule could almost balance on. This is where air and
ocean negotiate.

- **Glare** scatters off every ripple; you see more sky than water.
- **Sea foam** collects in the lee of the rock, a lace of popped bubbles.
- A single **water strider** ghosts across, dimpling the meniscus.

> Everything below is refracted, bent, and a little bit lied about.

Look past the shine and descend to [[z=1|the sunlit shallows]].

@plane z=1 label="Sunlit Shallows"
# 1-10 cm - The Sunlit Shallows

Light still rules here. Photosynthesis hums.

1. **Green algae** fur the upper rock, soft as wet velvet.
2. **Snails** graze slow trails through the film of diatoms.
3. **Shrimp** flicker, near-transparent, betrayed only by their shadows.

This is the loud, bright, crowded floor of the pool - the part a child kneeling
on the rock actually sees. The real strangeness waits in the cold layers under
[[z=2]].

@plane z=2 label="The Cold Middle" x="0" y="-12"
# 10-25 cm - The Cold Middle

Colour starts to drain. Reds vanish first, so the **red anemones** here read as
muddy brown until you bring them up into the light.

| Inhabitant   | Strategy          |
|--------------|-------------------|
| Anemone      | Wait and sting    |
| Hermit crab  | Borrow and hide   |
| Brittle star | Curl and creep    |

The `x`/`y` attributes on this plane mark where, in the pool's footprint, the
cold pocket actually sits - twelve centimetres back from the front lip, dead
centre left-to-right.

Below this, the rock closes in. Continue to [[z=3|the dark floor]].

@plane z=3 label="The Dark Floor" mood="primordial"
# 25 cm and down - The Dark Floor

The bottom. Cold, still, and old.

- **Encrusting sponges** plate the rock in dull orange.
- A **chiton** clamps down, an armoured tongue of the sea.
- Detritus drifts and settles: the pool's slow memory of every tide.

Nothing here hurries. Nothing here needs the sun. When the tide returns it will
rinse this floor and reset the whole column - but for now, this is as deep as the
pool goes.

Rise back to [[z=0|the surface]] and start the descent again.

3md: 1.0
axis: stage
title: Tonkotsu: 18-Hour Broth
view: elevator
subtitle: A living record of one pot across a full day
servings: 6 bowls (about 2.4L finished broth)
bones: 1.8 kg pork trotters + 600 g neck bones
start: 06:00
finish: 00:00
category: Home
---
One pot. Eighteen hours. Each plane is a stage — open the next only when you reach it.

@plane z=0 label="6:00 — Cold Water"

# The Blank Slate

Fill a 10L stockpot with **cold** water. Add bones straight from the fridge — no blanching skip here.

Bring to a **rolling boil** over high heat. You want the ugly stuff: grey foam, marrow scum, bits of everything. This is the purge.

- Duration: ~20 min hard boil
- Don't skim yet — let it all surface
- The water will look like dishwater. Perfect.

> "A clean broth is a liar. Let it confess first."

When the foam peaks and starts to recede: **drain everything, rinse each bone under cold tap water, scrub the pot.**

You are now ready. [[z=1|Go to Hour 1]]

@plane z=1 label="Hour 1 — The Simmer Begins"

# 07:00 — Fresh Water, Fresh Start

Return the clean bones to the clean pot. Cover with **fresh cold water**, about 3cm above bones.

Bring back to boil, then dial to **a vigorous simmer** — not a gentle one. Tonkotsu needs turbulence. The fat and collagen must emulsify into the water, not float on top of it.

| What you want | What to avoid |
|---|---|
| Milky white roil | Clear gentle simmer |
| Lid off or cracked | Lid fully on |
| Active bubbling surface | Still surface with ring of fat |

Add now:
- 1 whole head of garlic, halved crosswise
- 4 cm knob of ginger, sliced

Set a timer. Walk away. [[z=2|Hour 4]]

@plane z=2 label="Hour 4 — Colour Check"

# 11:00 — First Colour

The broth should be turning **pale ivory**. Lift a ladle and look through it — it should be opaque, not translucent.

**Taste it now.** It will taste thin and faintly porky. That is correct.

### Adjust heat if:
- Still pale/clear → increase to full vigorous simmer
- Reducing too fast → add boiling water from kettle to maintain level

**Add the aromatics for the second phase:**
- 1 large white onion, halved and charred cut-side down in a dry pan
- 2 tbsp sake
- 1 tsp white pepper, cracked

The charred onion brings a faint sweetness that will deepen by hour 10. You won't taste it now. Trust it.

[[z=3|Hour 8 — The Midpoint]]

@plane z=3 label="Hour 8 — Midpoint"

# 14:00 — It Becomes Itself

The kitchen smells like **rendered fat and bone mineral**. This is the smell.

Broth colour: **milky white to light cream**. The surface should have a constant lazy roll — small bubbles breaking, not a dead flat skim.

### The spoon test
Dip a cold metal spoon. Pull it out. Watch the broth:
- Runs off clean → not yet
- Clings slightly → on track
- Coats the back → ahead of schedule, lower heat slightly

**Do not add salt yet.** Salt concentrates as water evaporates. You will calibrate at the end.

Check water level. The bones should stay submerged. Add boiling water if the level has dropped more than 4cm.

[[z=4|Hour 13]]

@plane z=4 label="Hour 13 — Tare Time"

# 19:00 — Make the Tare While You Wait

Your broth needs 5 more hours. Use this window to build the **shio tare** (salt seasoning).

### Shio Tare (makes enough for 8 bowls)
```
60 ml sake
60 ml mirin
Bring to boil, cook off alcohol: 2 min

Add:
  25 g fine sea salt
  5 g MSG (do not skip — this is not negotiable)
  1 tsp white soy sauce (shiro shoyu)

Stir until dissolved. Cool. Store in jar.
```

Each bowl gets **1.5 tbsp tare** added to the bowl before ladle-ing broth over it. Never season the pot — season each bowl individually. Control is everything.

[[z=5|Hour 16 — Reduction]]

@plane z=5 label="Hour 16 — Reduction"

# 22:00 — Final Push

Two hours remain. **Increase heat** to reduce by about 20%. You want to concentrate without burning.

The broth should now be:
- **Deep creamy white**, nearly the colour of whole milk
- Viscous enough to feel slightly coating on the tongue
- Smelling of deep pork, sweet bone, faint char

**Remove and discard:** garlic, ginger, onion — they've given everything.

### Taste and balance
- Too flat? The tare will fix salt. This is collagen depth, not seasoning.
- Too rich? Add a small splash of cold water and let it simmer 10 min.
- Too thin still? Hard boil for 20 minutes with lid off.

Prepare your toppings now. Chashu should already be sliced. [[z=6|Midnight — The Bowl]]

@plane z=6 label="Hour 18 — The Bowl"

# 00:00 — Assembly

Strain the broth through a fine mesh sieve. Press the bones with a ladle — extract every drop.

**The bowl, in order:**

1. Warm bowl with hot water, dump it
2. Add **1.5 tbsp shio tare** to the dry warm bowl
3. Ladle **300 ml broth** directly over the tare — it will swirl and integrate
4. Add noodles (fresh thin straight, 130 g cooked, drained)
5. **Toppings:**
   - 2 slices chashu, blowtorched if you have one
   - 1 soft-boiled egg, halved (marinated 4+ hrs)
   - Nori sheet, standing vertical
   - Thin-sliced green onion
   - Black garlic oil: 1 tsp, drizzled last

> Eat immediately. Ramen waits for no one.

The 18 hours exist for this 8 minutes. [[z=0|Start again tomorrow?]]

---
3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: Traffic Light State Animation
fps: 1
loop: "true"
cycle_seconds: "12"
description: One full signal cycle for a four-way intersection, frame by frame.
---
A single playable cycle of a standard three-aspect traffic signal. Each plane is
one animation frame. Played in `z` order it loops forever: green holds, yellow
warns, red stops, and the red-plus-amber "get ready" beat (used in UK and German
signals) leads back into green.

The `x`/`y` offsets place each lamp where it sits on the physical housing: the
red lamp on top (`y=0`), amber in the middle (`y=1`), green at the bottom
(`y=2`). A spatial viewer can stack the lit lamp at the correct height.

Legend: a filled circle is lit, an empty circle is dark.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=2 label="Green - GO"
# Frame 0: GREEN

```
( )   red    off
( )   amber  off
(O)   green  ON
```

**State:** `GO`
**Hold:** 6 seconds (the long phase)
**Meaning:** Traffic may proceed if the way is clear.

Vehicles flow through the intersection. The crossing pedestrian signal shows a
steady "Don't Walk" because the conflicting approach is stopped.

Next beat: the green lamp drops out and amber lights up. Continue to
[[z=1|Frame 1: Yellow]].

@plane z=1 x=0 y=1 label="Yellow - CAUTION"
# Frame 1: YELLOW

```
( )   red    off
(O)   amber  ON
( )   green  off
```

**State:** `CAUTION`
**Hold:** 3 seconds
**Meaning:** The signal is about to turn red. Stop if it is safe to do so.

This is the shortest of the holding frames on purpose. The amber interval is
timed to the approach speed so a driver at the stop line can either clear the
intersection or come to a controlled stop.

Came from [[z=0|Frame 0: Green]]. Next up is the all-red moment in
[[z=2|Frame 2: Red]].

@plane z=2 x=0 y=0 label="Red - STOP"
# Frame 2: RED

```
(O)   red    ON
( )   amber  off
( )   green  off
```

**State:** `STOP`
**Hold:** 6 seconds (includes the all-red clearance interval)
**Meaning:** Do not proceed past the stop line.

While this approach is held at red, the cross street gets its own green. The
pedestrian "Walk" symbol lights for the parallel crosswalk. A short all-red
overlap at the start of this frame lets the intersection clear before any
conflicting movement begins.

Hold here, then advance to the priming beat in [[z=3|Frame 3: Red + Amber]].

@plane z=3 x=0 y=0 label="Red + Amber - GET READY"
# Frame 3: RED + AMBER

```
(O)   red    ON
(O)   amber  ON
( )   green  off
```

**State:** `PREPARE`
**Hold:** 2 seconds
**Meaning:** Stay stopped, but green is imminent. Release the brake, do not move.

This combined aspect is the British and German "get ready" phase. It gives
drivers a predictable cue to prepare without rolling forward early, which smooths
the launch when green returns.

This is the last frame of the cycle. Loop back to [[z=0|Frame 0: Green]] and
the animation repeats.

3md: 1.0
axis: time
title: 16-Week Marathon Training Plan
plan: Beginner to First Marathon
goal_time: sub-4:30
peak_weekly_mileage: 42
race: October 11 Sunday
---
A four-month build toward 26.2 miles, organized one plane per training block.
Each block stacks on the one before it: base first, then strength, then peak
volume, then a taper that sets up race day. Read the planes in Z order.

All paces assume an easy effort of roughly 10:30 per mile and a goal marathon
pace (GMP) near 10:18 per mile. Long runs are on Sundays. Take rest seriously,
it is where the adaptation actually happens.

@plane z=0 label="Base (Weeks 1-4)"
# Block 1: Base Building

The job here is simple: run consistently and get your body used to time on feet.
Keep every run conversational. If you cannot speak a full sentence, slow down.

| Week | Mon  | Tue 5K easy | Wed   | Thu 4mi | Fri  | Sat 3mi | Sun long | Total |
|------|------|-------------|-------|---------|------|---------|----------|-------|
| 1    | rest | 3.0         | cross | 4.0     | rest | 3.0     | 6.0      | 16    |
| 2    | rest | 3.0         | cross | 4.0     | rest | 3.0     | 7.0      | 17    |
| 3    | rest | 4.0         | cross | 4.0     | rest | 3.0     | 8.0      | 19    |
| 4    | rest | 4.0         | cross | 5.0     | rest | 3.0     | 6.0      | 18    |

Week 4 is a small step-back week (note the shorter long run) so the legs absorb
the first three weeks before you push on.

## Focus for this block

- Build the Sunday long run by no more than 1 mile per week.
- Cross-training (Wed) is a 40-minute easy bike or swim, zero pounding.
- Buy real shoes now and start breaking them in, not in week 15.

Next up: [[z=1|Block 2 adds tempo work]] once the aerobic base is in place.

@plane z=1 label="Strength (Weeks 5-8)"
# Block 2: Strength and Tempo

Now we introduce quality. One weekly tempo run teaches your legs to clear
lactate at a faster, sustained effort. Tempo pace is "comfortably hard", roughly
9:45 per mile, about 35 seconds quicker than easy pace.

| Week | Tue easy | Wed tempo        | Thu easy | Sun long | Total |
|------|----------|------------------|----------|----------|-------|
| 5    | 4.0      | 2mi @ 9:45       | 4.0      | 10.0     | 24    |
| 6    | 4.0      | 3mi @ 9:45       | 5.0      | 12.0     | 28    |
| 7    | 5.0      | 4mi @ 9:45       | 5.0      | 13.0     | 31    |
| 8    | 4.0      | 2mi @ 9:45       | 4.0      | 10.0     | 26    |

Week 8 cuts back again. Resist the urge to "make up" the volume, the cutback is
load-bearing.

## Workout detail: the tempo run

1. 1 mile easy warm-up.
2. The tempo block at 9:45 (hold it steady, do not start too hot).
3. 1 mile easy cool-down.

Add two short strength sessions a week: squats, lunges, single-leg calf raises,
and planks. Strong hips and core are what keep your form together at mile 22.

Back to [[z=0|the base block]] if a run still feels hard, or push on to
[[z=2|peak mileage]] when the tempos feel repeatable.

@plane z=2 label="Peak (Weeks 9-13)"
# Block 3: Peak Mileage

The most demanding block. This is where you bank the long runs that make 26.2
possible and rehearse goal marathon pace (GMP, near 10:18) on tired legs.

| Week | Wed quality       | Sun long             | Total |
|------|-------------------|----------------------|-------|
| 9    | 4mi @ 9:45 tempo  | 15.0                 | 33    |
| 10   | 5mi @ 9:45 tempo  | 17.0                 | 36    |
| 11   | 6x800m @ 5K pace  | 18.0 (last 4 @ GMP)  | 38    |
| 12   | 5mi @ 9:45 tempo  | 20.0 (last 6 @ GMP)  | 42    |
| 13   | 4mi @ 9:45 tempo  | 14.0                 | 34    |

The week-12 long run of 20 miles is the keystone of the whole plan. The final
6 miles at goal pace teach you what mile 20 of the race will actually feel like.

## Fueling the long run

- Practice race-day breakfast 3 hours before every long run.
- Take a gel every 40-45 minutes, starting at minute 40 (do not wait until you
  feel empty).
- Drink to thirst, roughly 4-6 oz every 20 minutes in the heat.
- Whatever you eat and drink here is exactly what you use on [[z=3|race day]].

Week 13 begins the wind-down. Do not panic, [[z=3|the taper]] is when fitness
becomes performance.

@plane z=3 label="Taper and Race (Weeks 14-16)"
# Block 4: Taper and Race Day

Mileage drops sharply while intensity stays sharp. You will feel restless and
slightly slow, this is normal and means the taper is working. The hay is in the
barn.

| Week | Wed              | Sun long | Total | Note                  |
|------|------------------|----------|-------|-----------------------|
| 14   | 4mi @ GMP        | 12.0     | 28    | first taper week      |
| 15   | 3mi @ GMP        | 8.0      | 20    | sleep is the workout  |
| 16   | 2mi shakeout     | 26.2     | race  | trust the training    |

## Race week checklist

- [ ] Lay out clothes, bib, and gels the night before (nothing new on race day).
- [ ] Eat a familiar dinner, hydrate, and stop drinking caffeine early.
- [ ] Set two alarms. Arrive at the start with time to spare.

## Race-day pacing

1. **Miles 1-6:** start 10-15 seconds SLOWER than GMP. Bank patience, not time.
2. **Miles 7-20:** settle into goal pace (about 10:18) and run the tangents.
3. **Miles 21-26.2:** this is the race. Hold form, shorten your stride, finish.

You arrived here from [[z=2|the 20-mile peak run]]. Everything you rehearsed
there, the fuel, the pace, the patience, is the plan for today. Go get it.

3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: The Old Pond, Three Ways
poet: Matsuo Basho
written: c. 1686
form: haiku (5-7-5)
note: One source plane plus two English renderings, stacked as toggleable layers.
---
A single haiku by Matsuo Basho, kept in three layers: the Japanese source on the
base plane, a literal gloss above it, and a free verse rendering on top. Toggle the
layers to compare how each translator handled the same five-seven-five.

@plane z=0 label="Original (Japanese)" x=0 y=0
# Layer 0: Source

## furuike ya

```
古池や          furuike ya
蛙飛びこむ      kawazu tobikomu
水の音          mizu no oto
```

Romaji, line by line:

1. furu-ike ya (old pond, plus the cutting word *ya*)
2. kawazu tobikomu (a frog leaps in)
3. mizu no oto (the sound of water)

The pivot is *ya*, a *kireji* (cutting word) that holds the old pond in silence
before the frog disturbs it. There is no verb tense and no subject pronoun, so the
scene stays open. See the word-by-word gloss in [[z=1|the literal layer]], or skip
straight to [[z=2|the free rendering]].

@plane z=1 label="Literal gloss (English)" x=2 y=0
# Layer 1: Literal Gloss

A close, almost mechanical reading that preserves word order and the 5-7-5 count
where English allows it.

> Old pond, ah,
> a frog jumps in,
> the sound of water.

Notes on the choices:

- *furuike* becomes "old pond" (two words for one compound).
- *ya* is rendered as "ah," a small breath standing in for the cut.
- *tobikomu* is "jumps in," present tense, no named actor.
- *mizu no oto* is left as plain "the sound of water," resisting the urge to add
  "splash."

This layer sits directly over [[z=0|the source]]. For a version that breaks the
literal grid in favor of music, lift up to [[z=2|the free rendering]].

@plane z=2 label="Free rendering (English)" x=4 y=0
# Layer 2: Free Rendering

A looser translation that trades the strict syllable count for rhythm and a single
sharp image. (After Robert Hass, adapted.)

> The old pond,
> a frog jumps in:
> the splash of silence.

What this layer gives up, and what it keeps:

- It drops the 5-7-5 count entirely, choosing three short breaths instead.
- It adds the paradox "splash of silence," which is not in [[z=1|the gloss]] but
  honors the way the *ya* in [[z=0|the source]] frames quiet against sound.
- The colon does the work of the cutting word, pausing the eye before the splash.

Read top to bottom (z=2, z=1, z=0) and the poem grows more spare; read bottom to
top and it grows more interpreted. Same eleven syllables underneath all three.

3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: Blinking Typing Cursor
fps: 2
loop: true
glyph: U+2588 (full block)
duration_ms: 1000
---
A four-frame loop for a text caret that blinks on and off at the end of a
prompt. Played back at 2 frames per second, the cursor is solid for half a
second, then hidden for half a second, giving the classic 500 ms blink that
terminals and text editors use.

Read the frames in order: [[z=0|on]], [[z=1|dim]], [[z=2|off]], then
[[z=3|dim again]], and the player wraps back to [[z=0|on]] to repeat.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="On (solid)"
# Frame 0 - cursor fully on

The caret is drawn at full opacity right after the prompt text.

```
> hello world█
```

- Opacity: 100%
- Glyph: full block (`█`)
- Hold: 250 ms

This is the resting state when you stop typing. Next comes the
[[z=1|fade out]].

@plane z=1 x=0 y=0 label="Dim (fading out)"
# Frame 1 - cursor fading out

Halfway through the off-stroke the caret is rendered at reduced opacity so the
transition does not snap. On hardware that cannot fade, this frame is simply
skipped and the cursor jumps straight from [[z=0|on]] to [[z=2|off]].

```
> hello world▓
```

- Opacity: 40%
- Glyph: medium shade (`▓`)
- Hold: 250 ms

@plane z=2 x=0 y=0 label="Off (hidden)"
# Frame 2 - cursor fully off

The caret is not drawn at all. Only the prompt and the typed text remain, so
the underlying character (or trailing space) shows through cleanly.

```
> hello world
```

- Opacity: 0%
- Glyph: none
- Hold: 250 ms

This is the darkest point of the blink. The loop now climbs back toward
visibility via [[z=3|the fade in]].

@plane z=3 x=0 y=0 label="Dim (fading in)"
# Frame 3 - cursor fading in

The mirror of [[z=1|frame 1]]. The caret returns at partial opacity before
snapping back to solid, which softens the on-stroke the same way frame 1
softened the off-stroke.

```
> hello world▓
```

- Opacity: 40%
- Glyph: medium shade (`▓`)
- Hold: 250 ms

After this frame the player wraps to [[z=0|frame 0]] and the one-second cycle
starts over.

3md: 1.0
axis: lap
title: Undercut Window: 2024 Spanish GP Lap 32
view: present
race: Spanish Grand Prix · Circuit de Catalunya
session: Race · 66 laps total
lead_car: VER #1 · Hard C2 · deg 38%
chase_car: NOR #4 · Hard C2 · deg 43%
gap_at_entry: 2.3s
analyst: Wall Strategy / Stint 2 decision window
category: Data
---
The clock is at lap 32. Norris is 2.3 s behind Verstappen. Both on ageing Hards. The undercut window is cracking open — but only just. Six planes. One call.

@plane z=0 label="Situation"
# The Setup · L32 of 66

| | VER #1 | NOR #4 |
|---|---|---|
| Tyre | Hard C2 | Hard C2 |
| Age | 28 laps | 28 laps |
| Deg % | 38 | 43 |
| Lap delta | — | +0.4 s/lap |
| Gap | **2.3 s** | — |

> NOR is losing 0.4 s/lap to VER on worn rubber. At this rate, gap grows to **3.5 s by L36** — outside clean air range.

**Decision point:** box NOR now (L32) or commit to L35+ and accept track position.

See pit-stop delta model → [[z=1|Undercut Math]]

@plane z=1 label="Undercut Math"
# Undercut Model · L32 Box

**Assumptions**
- Pit loss (in + stationary + out): **22.8 s** (Catalunya pit lane measured)
- Fresh Medium C3 vs aged Hard C2: **+0.9 s/lap pace advantage**
- Laps remaining after box: **34**

**Break-even calc**

```
Gap to recover:  2.3 s (current) + 22.8 s (pit loss) = 25.1 s deficit on exit
Recovery rate:   0.9 s/lap (tyre delta) + 0.4 s/lap (deg) = 1.3 s/lap
Laps to recover: 25.1 / 1.3 = ~19 laps
```

**Verdict:** NOR rejoins ahead of VER if VER pits L33–L38 (expected). If VER reacts L32, undercut **fails** — see [[z=2|Overcut Fallback]].

@plane z=2 label="Overcut Fallback"
# Overcut Fallback · Stay Out to L36+

If Red Bull covers immediately (box VER L32 reactive), NOR's options shift:

| Stay-out to | Clean air laps | Exit gap to VER | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| L34 | +2 laps | ~1.1 s behind | Tyre cliff looming |
| L36 | +4 laps | ~0.3 s behind | **Cliff zone** |
| L38 | +6 laps | Likely lose 2+ s | Too late |

**Overcut only works to L36.** Beyond that, NOR's C2 deg (43%) enters the cliff — lap time drops 0.7 s in a single lap.

- [ ] Monitor VER lap time L32 S1 split
- [ ] If VER pits L32 → commit NOR to L36 overcut
- [ ] If VER stays → execute undercut NOW

See tire compounds → [[z=3|Compound Choice]]

@plane z=3 label="Compound Choice"
# Compound Choice · What Goes On?

Three options for NOR's final stint (34 laps):

| Compound | Pace vs Hard | Deg/lap | Cliff lap | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Medium C3** | +0.9 s | 0.31% | ~L58 | Recommended. Covers to end. |
| Soft C4 | +1.6 s | 0.62% | ~L52 | Fastest but risks L52+ collapse |
| Hard C2 (new) | +0.3 s | 0.19% | None | Safe but won't close the gap |

**Call: Medium C3.**

Soft looks tempting (+1.6 s) but a cliff at L52 with 14 laps left hands VER the position back. Medium gets NOR to the flag with ~0.1 s margin on deg.

Gap projection on Medium → [[z=4|Gap Projection]]

@plane z=4 label="Gap Projection"
# Gap Projection · L32–L66 on Medium C3

Assumes NOR boxes L32, VER boxes L33 (one lap later, standard reactive).

```
L32  NOR pits →  exits P2, 4.1 s behind VER (in-lap + pit loss)
L33  VER pits →  NOR gap: 1.8 s (NOR gains 1.3 s while VER in pit lane)
L36  NOR gap: 1.5 s  (closing 0.1 s/lap — tyre advantage minus clean air)
L42  NOR gap: 0.9 s
L50  NOR gap: 0.3 s  ← DRS window opens
L52  NOR within 1.0 s → DRS enabled on back straight
L55  NOR attempts pass T1
```

> **Key risk:** If VER manages to stretch to L35, he exits on fresher tyres and the gap never closes. **Timing is everything.**

Final recommendation → [[z=5|The Call]]

@plane z=5 label="The Call"
# The Call · Radio Script

**BOX NOR · L32 · Medium C3**

---

> **Engineer:** "Lando, box this lap. Box box box. Medium tyres. You're undercutting."

> **NOR:** "Understood. Box box box."

---

**Post-box targets**
- [ ] Exit gap < 5.0 s (nominal: 4.1 s) ✓
- [ ] Beat VER out of pit lane (if he reacts L33) → need 22.6 s stop or faster
- [ ] Tyre temp window hit by L34 exit corner
- [ ] DRS window by L50

**What kills this strategy**
- Safety car L33–L38 (free stop for VER, nullifies undercut)
- NOR stop > 24 s (slow wheel, traffic)
- VER pits same lap (L32) — reduces to pace race only

> *"You don't win races in the pits. But you lose them by staying out one lap too long."*

3md: 1.0
axis: layer
title: Vermeer's Girl with a Pearl Earring: Layer by Layer
view: elevator
artist: Johannes Vermeer
date: c. 1665
medium: Oil on canvas
dimensions: 44.5 × 39 cm
location: Mauritshuis, The Hague
accession: 670
curator: Leif (annotated June 2026)
category: Creative
---
*Peel back the canvas from varnish to ground — five centuries of craft in five layers.*

@plane z=0 label="Surface: Varnish & Gaze"
# What We First See

The gaze arrests before anything else. She turns *toward* the viewer mid-motion — the Dutch call it a *tronie*, a character study, not a portrait of anyone known. No commission, no patron named.

The varnish applied in 1994 (conservation, Jørgen Wadum) now reads as a warm amber cast over the blacks, which Vermeer glazed to near-total darkness — a void he used as a compositional anchor. Modern reflectography reveals he originally sketched a curtain in that corner and painted it out.

**The earring** sits below and slightly *behind* her ear — physically implausible as a hung pearl, which led conservator Abbie Vandivere (2018 Scanning the Masterpiece project) to suggest it may be glass or tin, not pearl at all. The single specular highlight is just two brushstrokes.

> "The background was never blank. It was a choice made twice."

[[z=1|See the underdrawing →]]

@plane z=1 label="Paint Layers: Wet-in-Wet"
# Vermeer's Technique

Vermeer worked **wet-in-wet** (alla prima), completing passages in single sessions before the paint could skin over. Macro X-ray fluorescence mapping (2018) confirmed:

- **Flesh tones:** Lead white + smalt (cobalt-blue glass, now faded) + vermilion
- **Yellow jacket (other works):** Suggests access to expensive pigments via his art-dealer network
- **Ultramarine in the headscarf:** Lapis lazuli, ~25–30 particles per square millimeter — an extravagance even by 17th-century standards
- **Black background:** Carbon black, 6–8 thin glazes, not a single opaque coat

The transition from jaw to shadow (lower left cheek) contains a passage of **pure impasto lead white**, scraped and re-applied at least once. You can feel it under raking light at the Mauritshuis — a ridge 0.3 mm proud of the canvas.

No pentimenti in her face. He knew exactly where she was going.

[[z=2|Underdrawing & ground →]]

@plane z=2 label="Underdrawing & Ground"
# What Lies Beneath

**Ground layer:** Double chalk ground (calcium carbonate), unusual for a Dutch painter of this period — most used lead white. The double ground gives Vermeer's lights their particular luminosity; the chalk scatters light back through the paint film.

**Underdrawing (infrared reflectography):** Minimal. Unlike contemporaries (Rembrandt drew obsessively into his grounds), Vermeer appears to have worked almost freehand from a **camera obscura** projection. The 2001 Tim Jenison experiments and Penn State optical studies confirm the geometry is too precise for unaided freehand work at this scale.

The collar — those three loose white strokes — has no underdrawing at all. Pure confidence.

| Layer | Material | Thickness (μm) |
|-------|----------|----------------|
| Varnish | Dammar resin | 15–30 |
| Paint (highlights) | Lead white + ultramarine | 80–120 |
| Paint (darks) | Carbon black glazes | 20–40 each |
| Ground (upper) | Chalk + linseed | 100 |
| Ground (lower) | Chalk + animal glue | 150 |

[[z=3|Symbolism & iconography →]]

@plane z=3 label="Symbolism"
# Reading the Painting as Language

**The pearl (or glass drop):** In 17th-century Dutch iconography, pearls signal *vanitas* — beauty that fades. But they also denote *purity* and *Venus*. The ambiguity is deliberate. Vermeer painted pearl earrings in at least four works; they are never incidental.

**The turban:** No Dutch woman of 1665 wore a turban. It is an *orientalist fantasy* — exotic, erotic, fictional. Vermeer is signaling: *this is art, not record*. The turban also flattens the silhouette, forcing all attention to the face and the earring.

**The turned pose (contrapposto of the bust):** She is leaving, or arriving. The open mouth suggests interrupted speech — a *suspended moment* technique Vermeer borrowed from Italian *paragone* debates about whether painting could capture time better than sculpture.

**What is absent:** No domestic setting, no window, no table, no allegory props. Pure person against pure dark. In Vermeer's 36 known works, this is the most stripped composition. It was probably unsellable in his lifetime — too uncommissioned, too strange.

[[z=4|Restoration history →]]

@plane z=4 label="Restoration: 1994 & 2018"
# The Two Great Interventions

## 1994 — Jørgen Wadum, Mauritshuis
The painting arrived filthy: two layers of discolored varnish, old retouches over minor flake losses near the lower right, and a canvas lining applied c. 1882 that had introduced cupping distortions.

- Varnish removed with isopropanol/water mixtures, under microscope
- Old retouches lifted; losses inpainted with **Gamblin Conservation Colors** (reversible)
- Lining stabilized but not removed
- New Paraloid B-72 varnish applied — now a standard

The cleaned painting shocked the museum. The background was not warm brown — it was **cold, almost blue-black**, dramatically changing the tonal temperature of the whole composition.

## 2018 — Scanning the Masterpiece (Abbie Vandivere, lead)
Non-invasive. MA-XRF, OCT, 3D surface scanning. Findings:

- Eyelashes added late in campaign (not underdrawing)
- Left eyebrow slightly repositioned
- The earring: no drill hole in the ear visible — likely a clip or simply painted fiction
- Micro-crack patterns (craquelure) consistent with age, no active flaking

**Current condition:** Stable. Next examination scheduled 2029.

3md: 1.0
axis: frame
title: ASCII Walk Cycle
fps: 8
loop: true
direction: rightward
artist: CorvidLabs
---
A four-frame walking loop drawn in monospace ASCII. Play the frames in order
(0, 1, 2, 3) and then jump back to [[z=0|frame 0]] to repeat. The figure keeps
its head and torso fixed while the legs and arms swing through a contact, a
recoil, a passing pose, and a high point, the four poses every classic walk
cycle is built from.

Read each plane as a single drawn cel. Render at 8 fps for a natural stroll;
drop to 4 fps for a slow, heavy trudge.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Contact"
# Frame 0 - Contact

The lead foot has just landed and the trailing foot is still pushing off. This
is the widest stride of the cycle and the pose viewers read as "a step."

```
  O
 /|\
 / \
/   \
```

- Lead leg: forward and straight, heel striking down
- Trail leg: extended back, toe still grounded
- Arms: opposed to legs (the wide `/|\` swing)

Next pose absorbs the impact: see [[z=1|frame 1, Recoil]].

@plane z=1 x=2 y=1 label="Recoil"
# Frame 1 - Recoil

The body sinks to its lowest point as the front leg takes the full weight. Knees
bend, the torso dips by one row, and momentum is briefly soaked up before the
push forward.

```
  O
 /|\
  |
 / \
```

- Lead leg: bent, bearing weight directly under the hips
- Trail leg: lifting, beginning to swing through
- Body height: lowest in the cycle (the dip sells the weight)

Coming up out of the dip leads to [[z=2|frame 2, Passing]].

@plane z=2 x=4 y=0 label="Passing"
# Frame 2 - Passing

The trailing leg passes underneath the body and the two legs cross. The torso is
balanced over a single support leg, rising back toward neutral height. Arms are
nearly straight down at the midpoint of their swing.

```
  O
 /|\
  |
  |
```

- Support leg: vertical, directly under the head
- Free leg: passing forward, knee high
- Arms: hanging near center, about to reverse direction

This is the balance point between [[z=1|the recoil]] and the next high step,
[[z=3|frame 3, High Point]].

@plane z=3 x=6 y=-1 label="High Point"
# Frame 3 - High Point

The body lifts to its highest point as the passing leg reaches forward and the
support leg straightens onto its toe. One more frame and the reaching foot will
land, returning the figure to the contact pose.

```
  O
 /|\
 / \
 \
```

- Reaching leg: swinging forward, heel about to drop
- Support leg: pushing up onto the toe (tallest pose)
- Arms: opposed swing widening again, mirroring frame 0

The reaching foot lands and the loop restarts at [[z=0|frame 0, Contact]].

3md: 1.0
axis: slide
title: A Toast to Mara and Devin
speaker: Best Man (Theo)
occasion: Wedding Reception, The Old Mill
duration: about 3 minutes
---
A five-beat toast. Each plane is one slide, advanced as the words land. Hold a
beat of silence between slides; let the room catch up before you move on.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="The hook"
# Beat 1: Stand up, get the room

*(Raise glass. Wait for the clink to settle. Smile.)*

Good evening, everyone. For those who do not know me, I am Theo, and I have had
the questionable honor of being Devin's best friend since we were eleven, when he
traded me his entire lunch for a single, slightly melted chocolate bar.

I tell you that because I want you to understand the kind of negotiator Mara
married.

*(Pause for laugh.)*

Next: [[z=1|how these two actually met]].

@plane z=1 x=1 y=0 label="The story"
# Beat 2: The how-they-met

Mara and Devin met in line for terrible coffee at a conference neither of them
wanted to be at. Devin says he opened with a brilliant line. Mara says he spilled
half his cup on her notes and apologized for ninety straight seconds.

Both versions are true, which is the first thing you should know about this
marriage: there will always be two accounts, and somehow both of them are real.

What I noticed, the first time I met Mara, was simple. Devin laughs at his own
jokes. Around her, he started waiting to see if she laughed first.

Back to [[z=0|the beginning]], or on to [[z=2|the turn]].

@plane z=2 x=2 y=0 label="The turn"
# Beat 3: The turn (get a little real)

*(Slow down here. Quieter.)*

There was a hard winter a couple of years ago. Devin's dad was sick, and Devin
was not himself, and I watched Mara do the unglamorous work of love: showing up,
making soup nobody asked for, sitting in waiting rooms with a deck of cards.

She did not fix anything. You cannot fix some things. She just refused to let him
go through it alone.

That is when I stopped thinking of them as two people who were dating and started
thinking of them as a team.

@plane z=3 x=3 y=0 label="The line about them"
# Beat 4: The thing that is just *them*

Here is what I want you both to remember, on the nights that are easy and the
nights that are not.

- Mara, you make Devin braver than he believes he is.
- Devin, you make Mara laugh in the exact places she forgets to.
- Together, you are kinder than either of you is alone.

Marriage is not one big decision. It is ten thousand small ones, made at the
kitchen counter, in the dark, half-asleep. Keep choosing each other in the small
ones, and the big ones take care of themselves.

The last slide is [[z=4|the toast itself]].

@plane z=4 x=4 y=0 label="The toast"
# Beat 5: Glasses up

*(Raise glass. Look at the couple, then the room.)*

So please, everyone, on your feet.

To Mara and Devin. May your coffee always be better than the cup that started
this, may your two versions of every story always agree on the ending, and may
you keep choosing each other for all the small, ordinary, extraordinary days
ahead.

To the happy couple.

*(Drink. Sit down before the applause finishes.)*

Return to [[z=0|the top]] if the room asks for an encore.

---
3md: 1.0
axis: day
title: Week of Small Weights
view: single
week: June 16–22, 2026
mood-range: 4–8
category: Notes
---
Seven days, one thread. Each plane is a day. Read forward, or jump to the one that still has a pull on you.

@plane z=0 label="Mon Jun 16"
# Monday

Woke at 6:40 without the alarm, which felt like a gift. Made coffee before my phone touched my hand — the rule I keep breaking and today kept.

The thing about the Kellerman proposal is that I've been afraid of it being rejected *and* afraid of it being accepted. Wrote that down on an index card and left it on the kitchen counter. It's still there tonight. Looking at it every time I refill my water feels like progress.

**One true thing I did today:** walked the long way home through the park even though it added 18 minutes.

Mood: **6/10**

@plane z=1 label="Tue Jun 17"
# Tuesday

Proposal submitted. 11:47 PM. Not my finest hour but it is out of my hands now.

I read somewhere that the window between finishing a hard thing and receiving its verdict is the only free time that exists — nothing you do can change the outcome, so you're genuinely off the hook. I think that's true. I made soup and watched a documentary about harbor seals. Neither thing was wasted.

Talked to Reva for an hour. She said the quiet dread I described is just "having a nervous system." I wrote that down too.

**One true thing I did today:** submitted the thing.

Mood: **5/10**

@plane z=2 label="Wed Jun 18"
# Wednesday

A bad afternoon for no specific reason, which is its own specific reason.

Ate lunch at my desk, which I said I wouldn't do this week. The light at 3pm was extraordinary — that amber-through-rain quality — and I noticed it for maybe four seconds before I looked back at the screen. Four seconds of noticing is still noticing. I'm keeping it.

Things that helped: the soup (second bowl), rereading the first chapter of *The Transit of Venus*, the specific sound the radiator makes when it first kicks on.

Things that did not help: checking email six times after 9pm, reading the comment section on anything, forgetting to eat dinner until 10.

Mood: **4/10**

@plane z=3 label="Thu Jun 19"
# Thursday

Kellerman wrote back. They want a call. I don't know yet if that's a yes shaped like a meeting or a no shaped like a courtesy. I am choosing to believe it is the former until I have reason not to.

Yoga class at 7:30. Nadia adjusts my downward dog every single week and every single week I learn something new about where I'm holding tension. Today it was my right jaw. My jaw. In a forward fold. The body is a strange country.

Cooked a real dinner: roasted chicken thighs with the olives and preserved lemon. Ate at the table with the candle. This is the life I am trying to be living.

**One true thing I did today:** let myself feel excited instead of just anxious.

Mood: **7/10**

@plane z=4 label="Fri Jun 20"
# Friday

The call was a yes.

I sat in my car for ten minutes afterward and cried a little, which surprised me. Not from relief exactly — more like from the specific loneliness of having wanted something for so long that no one else knew mattered to you. Reva knew. I texted her one word: *yes.* She sent back three exclamation points and a photo of her dog in sunglasses. This was the correct response.

Celebrated by doing nothing unusual, which felt revolutionary. Read for two hours. Lights out at 11.

Mood: **8/10**

@plane z=5 label="Sat Jun 21"
# Saturday

Farmers market before 9. Came home with: a bunch of chard I had no plan for, a jar of hot honey, a small ceramic bowl from the potter on the north end who remembers my name. The bowl is the color of old moss and I don't know where it will live yet but it will tell me.

Spent the afternoon reorganizing the desk — not as a procrastination, actually as a celebration, which is a new thing I'm trying. Cleared the index cards. Started a new stack.

Wrote 600 words of the thing I never talk about. Didn't reread them.

Mood: **7/10**

@plane z=6 label="Sun Jun 22"
# Sunday

A slow day. The good kind of slow, where time feels like something you own rather than something that's running out.

Called my mother. Forty minutes. She told me a story about my grandmother I had never heard — something about a winter trip to Montreal in 1962, a fur coat borrowed from a stranger on the train. I wish I had been recording it. I wrote it down after we hung up, trying to get the shape right before it blurred.

Looking back at the week: I did the hard thing, I let myself feel it, I ate well on three out of seven days, and I learned that my jaw holds grief like a closed fist. That seems like enough. That seems like more than enough.

**The index card rule stands. The long way home stands.**

Mood: **7/10**

3md: 1.0
axis: step
title: 20-Minute HIIT Circuit
duration: 20 min
rounds: 3
equipment: none (bodyweight only)
---
A no-equipment HIIT circuit you move through one step at a time. Each work
interval is 40 seconds on, 20 seconds rest, then you cycle the whole circuit
three times. Start at the warm-up and follow the steps in order.

@plane z=0 x=0 y=0 label="Warm-up"
# Step 0: Warm-up (3 minutes)

Wake the joints and raise the heart rate before the hard work begins. Do not
skip this, cold muscles are how ankles roll.

| Drill | Time |
| --- | --- |
| March in place, arms swinging | 45 sec |
| Bodyweight squats, slow tempo | 45 sec |
| Arm circles forward then back | 30 sec |
| Leg swings, 10 each side | 30 sec |
| Jumping jacks, easy pace | 30 sec |

Aim for a 6 out of 10 effort here. You should be breathing harder but still
able to talk. When you feel loose, advance to [[z=1|the first work block]].

@plane z=1 x=1 y=0 label="Block A: Lower Body"
# Step 1: Block A, Lower Body

Two movements, 40 seconds work and 20 seconds rest each. Push the pace but keep
the form clean.

1. **Jump squats** - drop to a parallel squat, explode up, land soft through
   the midfoot. Scale down to regular squats if the knees complain.
2. **Reverse lunges** - alternate legs, keep the front knee stacked over the
   ankle, torso tall.

> Coaching cue: drive through the heels on every squat and lunge. If your knees
> cave inward, slow down and reset.

Recover for 30 seconds, then move to [[z=2|Block B]].

@plane z=2 x=2 y=0 label="Block B: Upper Body"
# Step 2: Block B, Upper Body

Push and pull patterns to balance the legs you just torched.

1. **Push-ups** - hands under the shoulders, body in one line, chest to a fist
   off the floor. Drop to the knees when reps get sloppy.
2. **Plank shoulder taps** - hold a high plank, tap each hand to the opposite
   shoulder, keep the hips still and square.

Same 40 on, 20 off rhythm. If your hips are sagging on the plank, that is the
sign to take the optional knee variation.

When both moves are done, push into [[z=3|the core finisher]].

@plane z=3 x=3 y=0 label="Block C: Core"
# Step 3: Block C, Core

The grindy part. Brace your abs as if bracing for a punch on every rep.

1. **Mountain climbers** - drive the knees fast, hips low and level, do not let
   the shoulders creep forward past the wrists.
2. **Bicycle crunches** - slow and controlled, opposite elbow toward opposite
   knee, fully extend the straight leg.

After this block you have finished one full round. Catch your breath for 45
seconds.

- Rounds remaining 2 or more: loop back to [[z=1|Block A]] and run it again.
- Final round complete: continue to [[z=4|the cooldown]].

@plane z=4 x=4 y=0 label="Cooldown"
# Step 4: Cooldown (3 minutes)

Bring the heart rate down gradually and stretch the muscles you worked.

| Stretch | Hold |
| --- | --- |
| Standing quad stretch, each side | 30 sec |
| Forward fold for hamstrings | 30 sec |
| Chest and shoulder opener in a doorway | 30 sec |
| Child's pose for the lower back | 45 sec |
| Slow nasal breathing, eyes closed | 45 sec |

Hydrate, log how many rounds you actually finished, and note which block was the
limiter. Next session, try to add one round or shave the rest down to 15
seconds. Jump back to [[z=0|the warm-up]] whenever you are ready to go again.

3md: 1.0
axis: scene
title: The Yes-And Escalator
view: present
genre: Improv Scene
players: 2 (Alex & Bex)
setting: A dentist's waiting room
note: Each plane is one beat. Watch the stakes climb.
category: Entertainment
---

A two-person scene built line by line. Watch the ordinary become extraordinary through the power of **yes-and**. Read forward — never back.

@plane z=0 label="The Seed"

**Beat 1 — Ordinary World**

*Alex sits, flipping through a magazine.*

> **Alex:** "These chairs are uncomfortable."

**Bex:** "Yes, and they've been here since 1987."

---

*A perfectly normal dentist's waiting room.*  
*Stakes: 🌡️ 1/10*

@plane z=1 label="First Spark"

**Beat 2 — Something's Off**

**Alex:** "Yes, and I think that one has a face."

**Bex:** "Yes, and I've seen it blink."

---

*The chair is now a character.*  
*Stakes: 🌡️ 3/10*

> **Coach note:** Players accepted the absurd offer without flinching. That's the move.

@plane z=2 label="Commitment"

**Beat 3 — Both In**

**Alex:** "Yes, and it blinked at *me* specifically."

**Bex:** "Yes, and Dr. Morano says not to make eye contact."

---

*Now there's a rule. Rules mean the world has logic.*  
*Stakes: 🌡️ 5/10*

```
IMPROV PRINCIPLE:
When your partner gives you a rule,
it means the world is REAL to them.
Match that reality.
```

@plane z=3 label="Escalation"

**Beat 4 — The Stakes Arrive**

**Alex:** "Yes, and I've been making eye contact for six minutes."

**Bex:** "Yes, and the chair just stood up."

---

*Stakes: 🌡️ 7/10*

The moment one player makes the impossible **physical**, the other must **physicalize** it too.

> No one laughs at this. They both just… deal with it.  
> That's the discipline.

@plane z=4 label="The Peak"

**Beat 5 — Full Commitment**

**Alex:** "Yes, and I think it wants my 2 o'clock appointment."

**Bex:** "Yes, and it has better insurance than I do."

---

*Stakes: 🌡️ 9/10 — grounded in a real, petty human detail*

**The magic move:** bring the absurd back to something painfully mundane.  
Insurance. Traffic. A late fee.

*The audience breaks.*

@plane z=5 label="The Button"

**Beat 6 — The Button**

**Alex:** "Well. I hope it flosses."

**Bex:** *long pause* "It doesn't."

---

*Scene.*

```
THE BUTTON CHECKLIST:
✓ Short
✓ Callbacks the opening world
✓ Does NOT explain the joke
✓ Leaves one beat of silence
```

*Stakes: 🌡️ 10/10 — and then released.*

@plane z=6 label="Coach Debrief"

**What Just Happened**

| Beat | Move | Principle |
|------|------|-----------|
| 1 | Ordinary offer | Establish the world |
| 2 | First weird offer | Accept without flinching |
| 3 | Add a rule | Give the world logic |
| 4 | Physicalize | Commit with your body |
| 5 | Ground in mundane | Earn the laugh |
| 6 | Short button | Let it land, then shut up |

---

> **The whole game is trust.**  
> You can't yes-and what you don't fully hear.  
> Listen first. Build second. Never correct.

[[z=0|Run it again from the top →]]
